Also das, was wir jetzt auf der Hannover Messe gelauncht haben, wo eben ein System auf Basis eines Prompts eine Software schreibt, das ist deterministisch. Deswegen nennen wir das auch Eigen-Agent oder Eigen-Engineering-Agent. Weiß nicht, ob du dich an deine Physikstunden erinnerst: Der Eigenzustand eines Atoms, der ist definiert, der ist dann eben nicht mehr vage. Oder der Eigenvektor. Ja ja, der verändert sich nicht. Ja ja. So, wir schauen schnell nach China. Das ist das Land, in dem es passiert, habe ich den Eindruck. Das ist heute schon oft gefallen, dieser Begriff China. Da wird ja auch möglicherweise gar nicht viel erklärt, sondern einfach nur gemacht. Wo sind die uns in ihren Fertigungstechnologien noch überlegen und warum? Und wie können wir denen die Stirn bieten? Wir wollen ja nicht abgehängt werden in diesem Land. Also jetzt aus 30.000 Fuß kann man sagen: USA ist, was Modelle anbelangt, Fundamentalmodelle, weiter als China. Wenn's um Fertigungstechnologie geht, vor allen Dingen Skalieren, Cost-out, ist es China. Das heißt, wenn du mich heute fragst: Wie würde der beste humanoide Roboter ausschauen? Der hätte die Hardware aus China und das Gehirn aus USA. Das wäre die Kombination, die wahrscheinlich das Rennen machen würde. Was China... aber man muss dazu sagen, China holt extrem schnell auf. Da gibt's technologische Einschränkungen, also die letzte Generation an GPUs von Nvidia, die darf nicht nach China eingeführt werden. Die fangen an, ihre eigenen Halbleiter zu bauen, sind auch schon ganz gut. Sind zwei Knoten hinten dran, also ein Knoten ist immer eine kleinere Struktur, dann kommen von 10 Nanometer auf 5 Nanometer auf 3 Nanometer. Da sind die weiter. Aber China holt extrem schnell auf. China arbeitet mit offenen Modellen, die lernen schneller, weil sie von Kunden leichter angenommen werden, man kann die auch noch selbst modifizieren. Das heißt, da ist die Lernrate eine höhere. Lernt USA übrigens auch, die gehen auch mehr und mehr in offene Modelle. Und letztendlich... also wo China richtig gut ist, das ist jetzt nicht eine Fertigungs... es gibt Fertigungstechnologien, wo sie richtig gut sind: Das ist Batterien. Also in dem Thema Lithium-Ionen-Batterien hat uns China komplett abgehängt. Also nicht nur uns, auch die USA. Es gibt eigentlich China, dann kommt lange nichts, dann kommt Korea, vielleicht noch Japan und dann war's das. Da sind die saugut in jeder Beziehung, sei's jetzt das Beschichten, sei's jetzt das Stacking, also das Rollen von Material, wie man dann eben Batterien macht. Und das liegt aber an einem kompletten Ökosystem. Das ist also nicht eine Fertigungstechnologie, sondern es ist das komplette Ökosystem, die Zulieferindustrie. Also da gibt's diese Stacking-Maschinen, die diese Materialien rollen, um eine Batterie draus zu machen. Da gibt's genau genommen einen Marktführer, der macht achtzig, neunzig Prozent dieser Maschinen, und die kommen von einer Firma, und die sitzt in China. Ich habe immer bisher gedacht, dass es auf diesem Planeten zwei Sportartikelhersteller gibt. Das eine ist Adidas, das andere ist die Firma mit dem N. Jetzt gibt es in China aber auch Sportartikelhersteller, Sportmarken, die rasant wachsen. Anta und Li-Ning heißen die zum Beispiel. Die werden auch technologisch immer stärker. Wächst da eine völlig neue Konkurrenz heran? Na gut, die Konkurrenz gibt es jetzt seit einiger Zeit. Das Neue ist, dass die Firma Anta ja sehr viele Westernfirmen gekauft haben. Ich meine, die kaufen ja auch die Firma mit P, auf jeden Fall sieht es so aus. Und die haben Salomon gekauft, die haben Arc'teryx gekauft. Und die haben ja den Vorteil – wieder das Gleiche –, dass die skalieren können, wenn die etwas kaufen. Weil China hat 1,x Milliarden Menschen, die kaufen, ne? Und weil die da fertigen und ihre eigenen Läden haben, gehen sie von null auf fünfhundert Millionen Umsatz in zwei Jahren. Das dauert hier ein bisschen länger. Aber ich habe keine Angst vor diesen Konkurrenten, weil Konkurrenz ist gut. Und ich meine, auf jeden Fall bei uns: Die Adidas-Marke ist so gut bekannt überall in der Welt mit 76 Jahren Geschichte. Wir haben Technologien, wir haben Partner, wir haben Athleten, wir haben super viele talentierte Leute. So, da habe ich wirklich keine Angst. Wo ich Angst habe, wenn ich das sagen darf, weil es sitzen ein paar Politiker hier: Das Problem in Deutschland gerade jetzt ist, dass Demokratie ist Bürokratie geworden. Wir haben Vorschriften und wir haben so viele Dinge, die uns langsam machen. Was ist die Erste, die weg muss? Nee, das Problem ist ganz, dass wir konkurrieren gegen Amerikaner und wir konkurrieren gegen andere, und die sind viel schneller als uns. Und wenn du ein Hauptquartier haben willst hier für weltweit ein Geschäft, ist es halt sehr, sehr schwierig. Und wenn ich mir eines wünschte, wäre es, dass wir nicht für jedes Ding eine Vorschrift und eine Dokumentation hätten, sondern dass ein gewisses Vertrauen da ist. Wissen Sie, wenn ich alle Vorschriften lese und alle Dinge, die auf alle möglichen Niveaus kommen... weißt du, dann stirbst du. Und es gibt so viele Formulare, Samstag eine Veranstaltung zu haben oder Leute... und du musst so viele Dinge machen, die falsch sind. Und es sind nicht mal die Leute, die diese Vorschriften dann kontrollieren, weil das ist ja deren Job. Aber du kommst ja nicht durch. Und jetzt sage ich, ich bin ja Norweger, so ich kann es ja sagen: Dass Leute sich nicht wagen, darüber zu reden, ne? Verstehen Sie, ich meine die Presse... wenn du irgendetwas sagst, dass es so ist, dann kommt die Presse und macht dich kaputt. Wir müssen ein bisschen ehrlicher werden. Roland hat einmal tief Luft geholt... Ja, der kann es ja sagen, der sagt es viel besser als ich. Es treibt mich zum Wahnsinn, wirklich. Also diese, diese Regulierungswut, die uns da trifft, fängt in Europa an, und wir machen Gold-Plating in Deutschland nochmal oben drauf. Bürokratie kostet – das ist eine Zahl, die ist abgeschätzt worden –, kostet der deutschen Industrie dreizehn Milliarden Euro im Jahr an Bürokratiekosten, weil wir irgendwas erstellen müssen. Es gibt in jeder Firma mittlerweile einen Leiterbeauftragten. Wahrscheinlich ist irgendwann mal einer von der Leiter gefallen, und dann nageln wir ein Gesetz hinterher. Das funktioniert einfach nicht, ne? Aber sprechen wir nicht seit Jahrzehnten von Bürokratieabbau? Wann kommt der denn? Keine Ahnung. Also es geht immer nur in eine Richtung. Also ein Gesetz wegzunehmen ist unglaublich schwer, weil es kann ja immer was passieren. Wenn ich jetzt die Leiterbeauftragten wegnehme, und wir haben aber 90 Millionen Menschen im Land, passiert irgendwas. Und wir glauben, dass wir egal, wenn was passiert ist, ein Gesetz hinterhernageln. Also zu deiner Frage: AI Act, Data Act, Cybersecurity Act. Das ist einfach ein No-Go. Und ich meine, ich kann dir natürlich Schoten erzählen, aber um das mal zu verdeutlichen: Was man macht ist, wenn es den Data oder AI Act gibt, dass man eine Regulierung, die für den Endconsumer gedacht ist – also für den Konsumbereich, persönliche Daten –, eins zu eins auf den industriellen Bereich mit Industriedaten und Industrieanwendern überträgt. Das funktioniert so nicht. Und nebenbei gibt's im industriellen Bereich schon vertikale, also ganz spezifische Gesetze: Also wenn ich eine Software auf eine Maschine spiele, dann gibt's ein Gesetz dafür. Jetzt kommt AI nochmal oben drauf, das widerspricht sich teilweise. Also Beispiel wenn wir, wenn wir Züge bauen, dann... und da geht's um Belegung zum Beispiel, wie ist ein Zug belegt, dann sagt das eine Gesetz: Du musst es offenlegen, damit dann andere einen Service betreiben können für unsere Züge, was totaler Irrsinn ist. Warum soll ich da Geld investieren, damit es andere machen? Das heißt, ich muss es offenlegen, auf der anderen Seite darf ich es nicht offenlegen, weil wenn ich's offenlege, dann gebe ich ja persönliche Daten bekannt. Das heißt also am besten ist, man baut Züge, die dumm sind, die keine Daten mehr generieren, dann funktioniert es am besten. Das kann nicht sein. Oder was auch noch schön ist: Cybersecurity Act, das muss ich jetzt noch loswerden. Also bei uns ist es so, die Gesetze in Europa sind so gemacht, dass... ich übertrage's mal auf einen Use Case, den wir alle kennen: Wenn du heute regulär in Erlangen dein Auto parkst, musst du nach Europa nach Brüssel melden, dass du dein Auto regulär geparkt hast. Also es geht nicht mehr darum zu gucken, wer hat was falsch gemacht, sondern du musst beweisen, dass du regulär geparkt hast. So machen wir das. So, und das übertragen wir jetzt auf einen Use Case wie Aufzüge. Also das ist gestaffelt, Cybersecurity, also wenn's um kritische Infrastruktur geht, dann hast du höhere Auflagen. Bei Aufzügen kann was passieren, da hast du eine hohe Auflage. Wenn du jetzt einen Aufzug hast, der vielleicht vierzig Jahre alt ist – gibt's viele –, die sind gar nicht elektronisch, sondern die arbeiten mit einem Schalter Ein/Aus. Diese armen Menschen, die so einen Aufzug betreiben, müssen jetzt nachweisen, dass dieser Aufzug nicht gehackt werden kann, also cyber-secure ist. Probiert das mal, da brichst du dir die Finger ab. Also ich mein, das ist alles der Quatsch, der auf uns reinprasselt und... willst noch einen hören? Ja bitte. Seid ihr bereit? Also wir haben ja in Deutschland den Artenschutz. Das ist gut so, weil wenn man was baut, muss man gucken. Und dieser Artenschutz ist ein individueller Artenschutz. Also es reicht eine Eidechse oder ein Lurch, der stoppt deinen Bau. So gesehen in Stuttgart, wir verlagern die jetzt... es gibt deutsche und italienische dort übrigens, ich weiß nicht, welche geschützt ist, eine davon ist es. Wie jetzt, Lurch oder Eidechse? Keine Ahnung, sagen wir mal eine Eidechse. Auf jeden Fall verlagern wir die, und da kostet eine zweitausend Euro. Vermutlich hätte die ohnehin einen anderen Weg gefunden, wenn man da baut, weil warum soll die da bleiben? Jetzt geht's darum, jetzt will unser Verteidigungsminister endlich Kasernen bauen. Und jetzt findet man da also eine so eine Eidechse und dann steht das Ganze. Dann war der Antrag: "Könnte man denn nicht von diesem individuellen Artenschutz auf den Artenschutz der Art an sich gehen?" Also man muss nicht eine finden, sondern vielleicht eine Familie, dann könnte man ein Stück weiter kommen. Aber da steht dann das Ministerium im Weg und sagt: "Nein, nein, nein, Artenschutz!" Das heißt, wenn irgendwann mal Russland angreift, sagen wir: "Wir können es nicht verteidigen, weil die Eidechse war im Weg, wir konnten keine Kasernen bauen." Es kann nicht sein. Wir können noch weitermachen, weil... du merkst, ich habe mich da so eingearbeitet, dass wenn ich mal nach Brüssel fahre, dass ich ein paar Beispiele habe, weil die wollen ja auch immer Beispiele hören. Ich habe noch ein Thema, das nicht so wirklich lustig ist, sondern eher traurig: Sportinfrastruktur in Deutschland, auch in Erlangen. Wir haben viele Sportstätten in Deutschland, die größtenteils marode sind, Schwimmbäder werden zugemacht, da läuft nichts mehr. Auf der anderen Seite haben wir aber in Deutschland das große Geschenk der Vereine. Es gibt in keinem Land der Welt so viele Vereine wie in Deutschland. Wir haben diese ehrenamtlichen Menschen, das Kitt der Gesellschaft, die sorgen dafür, dass der Laden läuft. Das heißt, wo ist der Hebel, wo wir jetzt ansetzen können mit diesem Geschenk, das wir eigentlich haben? Vielleicht hilft das? Also das ist jetzt nicht unbedingt mein Fach, aber was ich weiß – das weiß ich auch, weil ich im Universitätsrat bin –, wenn eine öffentliche Institution wie eine Universität baut, sind die Baukosten zwei-, zweieinhalbmal so hoch, wie wenn es in der Privatwirtschaft funktioniert. Das heißt, wir müssen einfach wegen Auflagen, was auch immer – dann will halt irgendwie das Bauministerium bauen, oder wenn's dann irgendwo am Bau stehen bleibt und man braucht irgendeine Genehmigung, dann muss man warten, bis man vom Ministerium wieder irgendwie das Okay bekommt: Okay, ihr könnt weitermachen. Das funktioniert alles nicht. Das wird zwei-, zweieinhalbmal so teuer. Das heißt also, das Geld wäre wahrscheinlich da, man müsste es halt einfach so ausgeben, wie man's privatwirtschaftlich ausgeben würde, dann sähe das ganz anders aus. Also es geht um Investitionen, im Übrigen nicht nur in die Sportinfrastruktur, auch in die Universitäten, in die Schulen. Das ist wirklich ein massiver Investitionsstau. Und der wird natürlich nicht aufgelöst dadurch, dass man Bauen unendlich teuer macht, und das liegt an dem Prozess, liegt an den Auflagen. Wir kriegen ja jedes Jahr neue Auflagen. Man kann ja gar nicht mehr sozialen Wohnungsbau vernünftig machen mit vernünftigen Kosten, weil die Auflagen so hoch sind, dass es sich einfach... einfach nicht mehr rechnet. Und das überträgt sich auch in so ein Thema wie Sportstätten, ja. Dann fragen wir doch mal den Norweger. Die Norweger haben bei den Olympischen Winterspielen richtig abgeräumt, Bjørn. Was machen die da anders? Haben die mehr Geld? Haben die bessere Ideen? Also erstmal, als ich nach Deutschland kam in den 80er Jahren, war Deutschland ein Vorzeigeobjekt, wenn es um Infrastruktur geht. Ich meine Sporthallen, Stützpunkte, Fußballanlagen waren im Vergleich zu Norwegen meilenweit voraus. Jetzt gewinnt Norwegen nicht nur im Wintersport, was nicht so viele machen, sondern auch in der Leichtathletik, sogar im Fußball, glaube ich, sind wir jetzt besser als Deutschland. Bitte? Ihr habt diesen einen da. Na na, na na, da gibt's schon mehr. Und viele, das lese ich auch in den deutschen Zeitungen, haben eine Philosophie, weil die versuchen zu erklären, warum Norwegen gut ist. Und da kommen alle diese Möglichkeiten auf, dass man nicht so früh konkurriert, man macht viele Sportarten. Aber die Antwort ist Infrastruktur. Jede Schule in Norwegen hat eine Sporthalle, und wenn eine Sporthalle da ist, was machen die Kinder? Die machen Sport. An jedem... was weiß ich... Ort, wo es irgendwie Schnee gibt, macht man noch mehr Kunstschnee, man kann Langlaufen und Skifahren. Und gut, Norwegen hat das Öl, so die haben jetzt das Geld, aber die haben sehr, sehr bewusst die letzten fünfundzwanzig Jahre Infrastruktur gebaut. Und da kommen die Ergebnisse. Wissen Sie auch als Eltern, ich meine, man wird ja nicht Handballtrainer, wenn man keine Handballhalle hat. Nee, das ist richtig. Und man wird den Kindern nicht im Fußball helfen, wenn man nur in der Ecke steht. Und ich glaube wirklich, dass da kein Hokuspokus dahintersteckt. Das ist: Wenn man einen Swimmingpool hat, dann lernt man zu schwimmen. Schwieriger ist es nicht. Ja ja, aber das Interessante ist doch: In Norwegen ist es ja noch mal kälter als in Deutschland. Also ihr habt im Winter mit ganz anderen Klimaproblemen zu kämpfen als wir hier bei uns. Das heißt, die energetische Ausstattung einer solchen Halle wäre doch von Vorteil. Wie kann denn Siemens da helfen, diesen Bogen noch mal kurz zu schlagen? Wir können da immer helfen, aber wir sind, glaube ich, nicht das Problem. Waren wir übrigens auch am Berliner Flughafen nicht. Wir waren da eigentlich Teil der Lösung. Sicher, sicher. Absolut. Kann ich dir hundertprozentig sagen, weiß ich ganz genau. Wir waren da Teil der Lösung. Also technologisch ist das nicht das Thema. Ich glaube, dass wir... es geht schon damit los, dass man einen Flughafen auslegt, wo der Rauch, der nach oben abzieht, erst mal nach unten gesogen werden muss, bevor man ihn dann rausbläst. Das funktioniert einfach nicht, ne? Also wir verkünsteln uns an so vielen Stellen, und nochmal, jetzt sind wir bei dem Thema Regularien: Wir schaffen eine nach der anderen, und das würgt irgendwann mal nicht nur die Investitionstätigkeiten ab, sondern auch die Innovationsgeschwindigkeit. Aber jetzt haben wir zwei CEOs von Global Playern hier auf dieser Bühne sitzen, deswegen mal der Vorschlag: Wie wär's denn mit so einer Art Leuchtturmprojekt mit Adidas und Siemens? Ein konkretes Pilotprojekt hier in der Metropolregion Erlangen-Nürnberg, eine smarte, energieeffiziente Sporthalle, zwei ortsansässige Firmen. Auf den Adidas-Campus, wir bauen es bei uns. Also nur so für den Anfang, weil ich weiß, dass beide Unternehmen natürlich ihre Wurzeln kennen und ihre Wurzeln auch pflegen und nicht vergessen haben. Wäre so was denkbar? Wir haben ja unsere... ich duze Siemens-Roland, eure Real-Estate-Verantwortlichen sind hier. Genau, irgendwo. Also wir haben bei uns im Campus unfassbar schöne Sportanlagen. Stimmt. Genial. Also die Infrastruktur bei uns, jede Sportart ist wirklich gut, und wir öffnen es auch für sehr viele, sagen wir mal Nicht-Adidas-Leute. Wir haben die Special-Olympics-Leute da, fast jeden Monat. Wir haben Amateurvereine, wir haben alle möglichen Mannschaften. Und das ist wichtig, weil ich glaube, dass... und da kommt es ja wieder: Wir haben einhundertsiebenundzwanzig Nationalitäten bei uns, die bei uns arbeiten, und diese Möglichkeit zu haben, gemeinsam Sport zu machen, integriert die in eine Kultur. Und ergo glaube ich wirklich – nicht nur, weil ich vom Sport komme –, aber Infrastruktur für Sport ist auch das Beste, was es gibt, um Leute von anderen Kulturen zu integrieren, ne? Weil da spielt es ja keine Rolle, von wo du kommst, ne? Ob du Fußball spielst oder Handball oder Basketball. Und ich glaube, wir können gerne darüber reden. Aber ich glaube, die Politiker, wenn ich das nochmal sagen darf, müssen da reinschauen und sehen, wie wichtig das wäre, mit ein bisschen besserer Struktur Leute zu aktivieren. Das ist gut für die Gesundheit, das ist gut für die Stimmung, das ist gut für, was weiß ich, die Integration von Ausländern. Ich meine, da ist nur Gutes dabei. Und ich glaube, dass diese Ehrlichkeit... jetzt bin ich ja Ausländer und wohne hier, und ich finde Deutschland ist super, wenn man hier wohnt, aber man muss wieder Dinge tun, um Wachstum zu kriegen, und dann kann man verteilen, ne? Und das Leistungsprinzip – man kommt nicht darum herum, dass Leistung etwas bedeutet. Wissen Sie, wenn man diese Diskussion im Sport hat: "Soll man nicht gewinnen, bevor man vierzehn Jahre alt ist", da kriegst du einen Schock, wenn du fünfzehn bist. Es ist halt so, dass man in seinem ganzen Leben lernt, dass man gewinnen oder verlieren muss. Und das ist halt so, das kann man ja nicht verstecken. Man kann nicht Hochsprung machen und sagen: "Ja, du springst so hoch wie der andere", wenn du siehst, dass die Latte zwanzig Zentimeter höher liegt. Du kannst nicht Fußball spielen und sagen: "Ja, die haben zehn Tore geschossen, wir null, aber eigentlich ist es null-null." Es geht nicht. Ja, aber man lacht darüber, aber das ist ja ein bisschen so. Und das Problem ist aber – und da kommt es wieder –, wenn man das sagt – jetzt sind wahrscheinlich Journalisten da –, dann wird man irgendwie dargestellt als: "Oh, was für ein böser Mensch!" Die Frage ist, ob Roland das genauso betonen würde, den Leistungsgedanken. Müssen wir da nochmal ran? Absolut, absolut. Ich meine, es geht schon gar nicht, dass man irgendwann – in der Schule habe ich mal gehört – aufhört zu zählen, weil man dann benachteiligen könnte, ne? Aber so ist die Welt eben nicht. Am Ende geht's darum, auch in einem fairen Wettbewerb bei Firmen: Es geht um Gewinnen oder Verlieren. Ich gewinne einen Auftrag oder ich verliere einen Auftrag, und nebenbei ist das auch ganz toll, weil Wettbewerb schafft einfach Innovation. Und auch diese Leistungsbereitschaft zu sagen, wir müssen wieder bereit sein – das Thema Arbeitsmarkt ist zum Beispiel auch ein Riesenthema in diesem Bereich –, bereit sein, wieder uns einzubringen, Leistung zu honorieren. Leistung zählt was. Und da haben wir irgendwo die Spur verloren. Und gerade das Thema marode Sportstätten hat mich natürlich hellhörig gemacht, weil ich selbst sehr aktiv im Verein hier in der Region bin. Und bei uns ist das große Problem tatsächlich die hohen Energiekosten, die uns an die finanziellen Grenzen bringen, weil wir alte Gebäudetechnik haben. Und ich glaube auch, das große Problem liegt daran, dass man einfach gar nicht weiß: Wo geht die Energie hin? Wo liegt der Verbrauch? Und deswegen wäre meine Frage: Wie wichtig ist Ihnen als Unternehmen die Transparenz über Gebäude- und Energiedaten, um zum einen Kosten zu senken, zum anderen aber auch langfristig wettbewerbsfähig zu bleiben, gerade in so einem Land wie Deutschland, wo die Energiekosten doch sehr hoch sind? Ich? Ja ja. Wo ist mein Real-Estate-Guy? Also ich glaube, dass... da bin ich kein Experte, um das zu beantworten. Aber erst mal ist es ja so, dass in unserer Sustainability-Strategie tracken wir ja alles Mögliche und versuchen, so effizient zu sein, wie es nur möglich ist. Und wir haben ja das Ziel, dass wir nur mit erneuerbarer Energie arbeiten bis 2030. So klar ist es wichtig. Aber das ist so wie überall, es geht ja nicht nur um Deutschland, es geht ja um überall in der Welt. Und ich glaube, wir sind sehr fokussiert darauf, das Richtige zu machen, weil das ja auch Sinn macht, ne? Ich meine... so die Antwort ist: Sehr wichtig. Jetzt willst du mir wahrscheinlich etwas verkaufen, schätze ich, oder? Nee, könnte ich machen, könnte ich machen. Ja ja. Aber Energiesysteme der Zukunft fangen dann beim Verbrauch an, ne? Also jede Kilowattstunde, die ich hinten nicht verbrauche, muss ich vorne halt drei weniger reinschieben. Das Zweite ist, wenn wir über Erneuerbare sprechen, dann ist die Flexibilität, die Flexibilisierung des Verbrauchs natürlich unglaublich wichtig, weil ich die Energie dann nicht speichern muss oder keine Grundlast brauche. Also zu wissen, was Verbraucher tun, wie ich dort die Effizienz steigern kann, ist das A und O von einem neuen modernen Energiesystem. Dazu bräuchte man zum Beispiel auch einen Smart Meter Rollout in Deutschland, über den wir auch seit zwanzig Jahren sprechen und der nicht gemacht wird. In anderen Ländern sind wir schon viel weiter. Und da gibt es ja das Gleiche: Bei der Zielsetzung, die wir setzen für erneuerbare Energie und Effizienz, da sind wir super, super drauf. Also wo treffen Sie sozusagen aktiv selbst noch Entscheidungen und wo setzen Sie KI ein? Also die meisten Entscheidungen, denke ich, werden bei uns nach wie vor von Menschen getroffen. Also wenn's um Entscheidungen geht, wenn's um Prozesse geht, die automatisiert sind, dann offensichtlich nicht mehr. Aber die meisten Entscheidungen werden nach wie vor von Menschen getroffen. Und weil eben der Mensch noch unglaubliche Vorteile hat im Kontextualisieren von Themen – also wirklich einen Kontext herstellen von verschiedenen Informationen, die da zusammenfließen –, da sind wir einfach deutlich besser. Und da, glaube ich, das ist zum Beispiel eine Facette, da wird KI noch lange, lange hinten dran bleiben. Also der menschliche Aspekt, der Zusammenarbeitsaspekt und das Kontextualisieren von echt schwierigen Aufgaben, das ist ein Thema, das macht Menschen aus. Und ich würde sagen, die meisten echten Entscheidungen werden bei uns nach wie vor von Menschen getroffen. Das Gleiche. Also auch. Super Ball. Ups. Der kriegt keinen, weil der arbeitet bei uns. Ich bin immer ein bisschen traurig, Sie haben die Leistung angesprochen, unsere Sportvereine hier in der Region. Da gibt es zwei, die haben früher um die Meisterschaft gestritten. Nee, ich meine... erst mal die Universitäten, die wir in Deutschland haben – das muss man mal wirklich als Positives sagen –, die sind nach wie vor wirklich sehr, sehr gut. Die FAU kenne ich ja jetzt ein bisschen besser, ganz toll. Was hilft, ist, dass wir nach wie vor auch ausländische Studenten anziehen, was wir ja machen, also diese Mischung macht's ja. Diversität, auch Top-Talente anziehen. Wo ich dran arbeiten würde, wäre wirklich, die Curriculums von der Schule noch mal zu überarbeiten, auch in den Universitäten. Ich glaube, gerade Schulen – wenn wir darüber reden –, das ist ja wie ein römischer Meilenstein, da ändert sich ja nichts, ne? Da muss man dran arbeiten, das substanziell verändern. Universitäten tun das ja auch, also KI-Lehrstühle oder Ähnliches einzurichten. Und der letzte Punkt, den ich mitgeben würde – aber auch da sind wir, glaube ich, ganz gut, auf jeden Fall in Erlangen, was man noch hochskalieren kann –, ist halt die Zusammenarbeit mit den Firmen. Dieser Wechsel, Ausbildungs- oder Studiengänge, die sich sozusagen kombinieren mit einer Ausbildung an einer Firma, bis hin eben zu Promotionen und so weiter. Also das ist extrem stark, da schauen auch viele auf uns, was wir da tun. Und dann, glaube ich, wird das was, ja. Genau. Jetzt willst du den... ach so. Gerne. Hier, die war so lange dran. Ach, das habe ich nicht gesehen, die saß in meinem Rücken. Genau, nämlich hatten wir's ja von KI vorhin. Und ich arbeite als Hilfswissenschaftlerin gerade am Lehrstuhl FAPS an der FAU und setze mich da auch mit Robotern und KI auseinander. Und da haben wir die ethische Frage gerade wegen der Daten, die verwendet werden, um die Roboter zu trainieren. Es wurde ja gesagt, dass andere Länder viele Daten auch aus der breiten Gesellschaft nehmen, aus der Menschenmenge einfach raus. Was ich jetzt gerade aktuell auch in dem Whitepaper, das ich schreibe, betrachte, ist, dass dann ja auch Dinge wie Rassismus oder Diskriminierung aufgefasst werden könnten, dass eben die Maschinen falsche Dinge lernen. Wie stehen Sie denn dazu in der Industrie, wenn man das breiter anwenden würde, die Daten? Also ein super relevantes Thema. Weil wir vorhin über Regularien gesprochen haben: Wenn da der Eindruck entsteht, dass man Technologie gar nicht regulieren muss, dann ist das falsch. Ich glaube, dass Technologie Leitplanken braucht – also keine Detailregulierungen, sondern Leitplanken. Leitplanken zum Beispiel, was das Kopieren von Biometrik anbelangt, das ist ein No-Go, das darf nicht passieren. Was das Copyright anbelangt, oder eben auch, dass man guckt, dass Algorithmen eben das nicht tun, was da passiert. Das passiert jetzt weniger bei den Fundamentalmodellen, weil die eben fundamental trainiert werden, sehr horizontal. Das passiert dann, wenn diese Fundamentalmodelle in soziale Medien gehen, in Plattformen für soziale Medien. Dort kriegen die... das ist ja auch teilweise Deep Learning, also die kriegen ja sozusagen Punkte für Erfolg. Und jetzt kommt das Problem: Erfolg heißt für diese Plattformen, dass sie um deine Minuten und deine Sekunden kämpfen, die du länger dran bleibst. Und so sind die Algorithmen gemacht. Individuen haben da fast gar nichts dagegen zu setzen. Auch wenn man glaubt, man wird da nicht beeinflusst – das ist sehr subtil. Und da sind dann Limits, wo ich glaube, da muss man einschreiten. Und das schließt auch Algorithmen mit ein, die dann eben nicht diskriminieren in irgendeiner Weise oder Rassenhass verbreiten oder Ähnliches. Das kann man steuern über Algorithmen, in die eine wie in die andere Richtung, und da braucht es Regulatoren. So, Achtung. Kannst du fangen? Here we go. Die kann fangen und kann werfen. So, ich glaube, damit beschließen wir diese Runde. Vielen Dank, lieber Roland. Vielen Dank, lieber Bjørn. Und alles Gute. Und vielen Dank auch für Ihr Interesse, meine sehr verehrten Damen und Herren, und alles Gute Ihnen. Tschüss. Hier steht Siemens, ne?

LIVE: Sec. Pete Hegseth Testifies Before The Senate Amid War With Iran

Forbes Breaking News(5.5M)·206:28·13.1K views·2026-04-30
Pete HegsethSecretary of Defense
TL;DRSecretary of War Pete Hegseth and Joint Chiefs of Staff Chairman General Dan Kane testified before the Senate Armed Services Committee to defend a historic $1.535 trillion FY2027 budget request amid intense bipartisan scrutiny over the cost and…
strategy of the war with...

TL;DR

Secretary of War Pete Hegseth and Joint Chiefs of Staff Chairman General Dan Kane testified before the Senate Armed Services Committee to defend a historic $1.535 trillion FY2027 budget request amid intense bipartisan scrutiny over the cost and strategy of the war with Iran, high-level military dismissals, and delayed Ukraine aid.

The $1.535 Trillion Budget and Defense Industrial Base

  • Historic Budget Request: The Department of War is seeking $1.535 trillion for FY2027, representing a near-50% increase over the previous year's $1 trillion top line.
      • Key Allocations: The request includes $331 billion for munitions, $120 billion for the defense industrial base, $71 billion for the nuclear triad, $65 billion for shipbuilding, $54 billion for drone dominance, and $44 billion for quality-of-life improvements (including a 7% pay raise for junior enlisted service members).
      • Deferred Maintenance: Secretary Hegseth defended the massive request as a necessary generational investment to reverse "four years of underinvestment and mismanagement" and rebuild a hollowed-out defense industrial base.
  • Transition to a Business Model: Hegseth highlighted a paradigm shift from a bureaucratic acquisition model to a business-driven model.
      • Private Investment: The Pentagon has reportedly stimulated over $50 billion in private investments (not taxpayer dollars) across 39 states, resulting in 280 new or expanded manufacturing facilities and 70,000 new jobs.
      • Reconciliation Funding Delays: Out of $154 billion allocated in last year's reconciliation bill, only $26 billion has been officially put on contract due to administrative delays from the Office of Management and Budget (OMB).
  • The "Golden Dome" Air Defense: The budget funds "Golden Dome for America," a domestic missile defense system modeled after Israel's Iron Dome and David's Sling. Senator Mark Kelly raised concerns that the system's cost could spiral to between $500 billion and $1 trillion, urging the department to reassess the physics and sizing of space-based interceptors.

Controversy Over Operation Epic Fury and Regional Conflicts

  • The Strategic Cost of the Iran War: Ranking Member Jack Reed and other committee Democrats sharply criticized the unilateral, unauthorized 61-day-old war with Iran (Operation Epic Fury and Operation Midnight Hammer).
      • Human and Material Toll: The conflict has resulted in 13 to 14 U.S. service member deaths, more than 400 wounded, dozens of lost aircraft, and significant damage to regional bases.
      • Financial Drain: Acting Comptroller Jay Hurst confirmed that Operation Epic Fury has cost $25 billion to date, which critics note has contributed to skyrocketing global energy and fertilizer prices.
      • Strategic Impasse: Despite Hegseth declaring a "historic and overwhelming victory," Senator Reed pointed out that Iran’s regime remains in place, its nuclear program remains viable, and the critical Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed or heavily contested.
  • Operation Southern Sphere: Senators raised serious legal and operational concerns regarding the ongoing anti-drug campaign in Southcom's theater, which has resulted in nearly 200 fatalities.
      • Targeting Demands: Senator Tim Kaine questioned the legal justification for striking vessels in international waters without public evidence of narcotics on board, urging colleagues to review classified targeting criteria in the SCIF. Hegseth defended the operations, stating the targets are designated terrorist organizations ("the Al-Qaeda of our hemisphere").
  • Rules of Engagement and Civilian Casualties: Senator Reed criticized Hegseth’s past boast of "no stupid rules of engagement" shortly after a missile strike tragically killed hundreds of Iranian schoolgirls. Hegseth noted that the incident is currently under a formal "15-6" general officer investigation.

Military Leadership Changes and Cultural Reforms

  • General Officer Dismissals: Hegseth was heavily grilled over his decision to fire dozens of senior military leaders without public explanation, including highly decorated Army Chief of Staff General Randy George.
      • Demographic Concerns: Senator Reed pointed out that 60% of the two dozen dismissed officers were female or Black, questioning if the administration was intentionally targeting diverse leaders. Hegseth denied this, asserting that "merit" is the only metric used.
      • Culture War Accusations: Senator Reed accused Hegseth of pursuing an agenda rooted in Christian nationalism and hollowing out the military’s bench of experience. Hegseth countered: "I am not ashamed of my faith in Jesus Christ."
      • Praise for Gen. George: Senator Joni Ernst entered speeches into the record honoring General George, noting he successfully pulled the Army out of its worst recruiting crisis since the Vietnam era, exceeding FY24 recruitment goals with over 61,000 new soldiers.
  • Review of Women in Combat: Hegseth confirmed he has ordered a formal review of the effectiveness of women in combat roles, stating the study will rely on "real science" rather than "social engineering" and will be made public upon completion.
  • Rhetorical Fallout: Senator Jacky Rosen confronted Hegseth for repeatedly using the term "Pharisees" to describe journalists and congressional critics, calling it a historically weaponized, anti-Semitic term. Hegseth stood by the term, stating it accurately describes critics who focus on flaws rather than strategic successes.

Global Threats and Delayed Ukraine Aid

  • The "Axis of Aggressors": Chairman Roger Wicker framed the hearing around a dangerous global security environment, noting that the U.S. is locked in a high-stakes competition with a growing alliance of China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea.
  • Redirection of Ukraine Aid: Senator Jeanne Shaheen demanded to know why the department had delayed a spend plan for the $400 million in security assistance enacted by Congress for Ukraine in January.
      • The PEARL Program: The administration is utilizing the Prioritized Ukraine Requirements List (PEARL) to require European partners to pay for U.S.-supplied weapons to Ukraine, a move Senator Shaheen argued violates congressional intent.
  • Arctic and Pacific Power Projection: The budget requests a $6.9 billion total authorization ($2.2 billion in FY27 appropriations) for the JBER Fighter Town recapitalization in Alaska. General Kane highlighted JBER's strategic importance in countering rising joint Russian-Chinese naval and bomber incursions in the Arctic and High North.

Notable Quotes

"Because when leaders fear to speak honestly, people die, missions fail, wars are lost. The American people's trust in our military took 250 years to build. You are dismantling it in a fraction of that time..." — Senator Jack Reed
"We have flipped the Pentagon acquisition process from a bureaucratic model to a business model, decisively moving from an acquisitions environment paralyzed by bureaucratic red tape into an outcomes-driven organization focused on delivering the most for taxpayer dollars." — Secretary Pete Hegseth
"My blueprint for this role has always been that of General George C. Marshall. His firm commitment to civilian control and a non-partisan military remains my constant standard... delivering the facts leaders need to hear, not always what they want to hear." — General Dan Kane
"And it's defeatist Democrats like you that cloud the mind of the American people and would otherwise fully support preventing an Iran from having a nuclear weapon." — Secretary Pete Hegseth
All right, the hearing is in order. We completed a productive classified session down in the SCIF, and now we will begin the public portion of this hearing. I welcome back Secretary Hegseth, General Caine, and our acting comptroller, Mr. Jay Hurst. I thank all of them, including their families, for their service. For the dozens of Americans that regularly watch our hearings, my next remarks will be no surprise, but for new viewers, I want to reiterate some context. I've said this at almost every hearing: We live in the most dangerous security environment since World War II. Every uniformed officer who has come before this committee has agreed with that statement. First and foremost, we're locked in a competition with Xi Jinping and the Chinese Communist Party. The competition is high stakes, and it is about whether this will be an American-led century, or a century defined by authoritarian, autocratic regimes that care little for the needs of their citizens or those in neighboring countries. The Chinese Communist Party has accelerated its historic military build-up and its predatory economic practices against Americans and countries the world over. Xi Jinping leads not only China, but also an axis of aggressors. This growing alliance cannot be denied, and includes China, Russia, Iran, and North Korea. They're united around this goal: to oppose America's interests and the interests of other like-minded democratic countries across the globe. Vladimir Putin's war of choice in Ukraine has now entered its fifth year, and in Putin's objectives, we hear echoes of the imperialistic ambitions of World War II's aggressors, including Adolf Hitler. Vladimir Putin has suffered 1.2 million casualties and failed miserably in his military objectives. Along the way, he has transformed Russia's economy into one fueled by war, raising the prospect of an even more aggressive Moscow for the foreseeable future. Most of Iran's leaders are now deceased, but they and those who survive them have consistently sought violence against America, Israel, our Gulf allies, and the Iranian people. We saw this during the October 7th massacre, during their continued support for Hezbollah and Hamas, and in their desire to engage in nuclear blackmail. Iran's Ayatollahs have consistently represented a threat to American interests. Kim Jong Un has joined Mr. Putin's war of aggression. He continues a military and nuclear build-up that threatens South Korea, Japan, and the United States. Ties have never been closer among these four dictatorships. They support each other's aggressive endeavors, they prop each other up financially, and they scheme to undermine America's objectives. We should expect them to continue this behavior. This context plays out across every dimension of national power: the economy, technology, diplomacy, and more. But today, we're here to talk about the military dimension of this competition. These regimes have regularly tried to take by force what they cannot secure through the political process. For that reason, we must be ready to deter conflicts, and if necessary, to win them. President Trump has used the US military appropriately and effectively for American interests. He has viewed our adversaries as a united block, and has taken action in light of that reality. In Operation Absolute Resolve and associated statecraft, the President removed an aspiring dictator off the board and set up Venezuela for a future aligned with democratic interests. In Operation Midnight Hammer, he sought to eliminate the Ayatollah's nuclear program. When the Ayatollah chose to double down, the President launched Operation Epic Fury. In that mission, he has worked to remove the regime's conventional military capabilities and force it back to the table for a permanent solution. While we all mourn the tragic loss of the 14 service members who've lost their lives in this conflict, we do so knowing the world is safer without a nuclear Iran. All of these actions are part of a "peace through strength" strategy. In this approach, we seek first to avoid war, but we take military action when necessary to achieve US interests. And so, Mr. Secretary, I'm pleased that you are here testifying today in support of President Trump's historic $1.5 trillion defense budget request. That sum will go a long way toward rebuilding our military capabilities for a generation. I should say up front that this may be a long hearing. There is much to discuss. This $1.5 trillion request is chock-full of important programs and initiatives that are absolutely necessary to secure American interests in the 21st century. I think this funding underpins and accentuates three comparative advantages the United States possesses over the axis of aggressors. The first comparative advantage America enjoys over our adversaries is that we have the best innovation and industry in the world. So, I hope our witnesses today will cover the progress we've made in just the past year rebuilding the American arsenal. Last year, our reconciliation bill, combined with bipartisan appropriation bills, achieved about a trillion-dollar defense budget. This year's request would represent a near-50% increase. Every penny of it should be money well spent, making down payments on crucial transformational capabilities such as drone warfare, low-cost munitions, and missile defense. Also last year, Congress and the executive branch achieved historic acquisition reforms. Consequently, we are well positioned to make huge gains on efficiency this year and in the years to come, making it a much more flexible and timely process. I look forward to discussing how we might accelerate implementation of these actions. In particular, I'd like to see the Pentagon do more this year to drive competition in the defense industrial base. Competition absolutely drives better outcomes for our service members and taxpayers. Of course, our people are the final comparative advantage we have over our adversaries. We've enjoyed significant improvements in recruitment and retention, but we need to solidify a merit-based environment that fully cares for our personnel. I commend you, Mr. Secretary, for your efforts over the past year to do just that. That task will never be finished, of course, but we embrace it gladly, and we salute the progress. We will always be striving to care for and equip American service members as much as possible. I look forward to more work between this committee and the department this coming year. With that, I turn to my friend and colleague, Ranking Member Jack Reed. Well, thank you very much, Mr. Chairman. Secretary Hegseth, General Caine, Mr. Hurst, welcome, and please convey my appreciation, all of our appreciation, to our military service members and defense civilians. We owe them our deepest sense of gratitude. Mr. Secretary, this is your first public appearance before this committee in nearly a year. Since your last public testimony, you and President Trump have unwisely taken the United States to war with Iran. You ordered an attack on Venezuela, which, while it succeeded, was incredibly risky. Back home, you ordered thousands of Active Duty troops to American cities like Washington, Los Angeles, Chicago, and Portland, to police American citizens. And you have personally intervened to end the careers of dozens of military leaders without explanation. These actions will have significant and long-term consequences. Now, you appear before us to ask for a $1.5 trillion budget, a 45% increase above last year. I must say I am skeptical, and such a request demands intense scrutiny. 61 days ago, President Trump unilaterally began the war in Iran. He had no coherent strategy. He refused to make a case to the American people or consult Congress. He failed to present any evidence of an imminent threat, and he ignored the advice of military and intelligence experts who warned him of the consequences. Today, our nation is in a worse strategic position. The Strait of Hormuz was open; now it is closed. 13 service members have tragically lost their lives, and more than 400 have been wounded. We have lost dozens of aircraft, sustained significant damage to our bases in the area, and expended an alarming amount of our missile inventory. Morale and readiness across the force, especially among overdeployed units and vessels like the USS Gerald R. Ford aircraft carrier, have suffered. Gasoline and fertilizer prices throughout the world have surged. American families are bearing the cost of a war they wanted nothing to do with, and have gained nothing from. And yet, Secretary Hegseth, you declared victory a month ago. On April 8th, you said, in your words, "Operation Epic Fury was a historic and overwhelming victory. By any measure, Epic Fury decimated Iran's military and rendered its combat forces ineffective for years to come." Let me be clear. Tactically, the United States military performance against Iran has been remarkable. And I salute the service members who have executed this mission with skill and bravery. The problem with your statements, Mr. Secretary, is they are dangerously exaggerated. Iran's hardline regime remains in place, it still retains stockpiles of enriched uranium, and its nuclear program remains viable. Iran's military retains enough combat effectiveness to keep the conflict at an impasse. Its missiles and drones remain a far greater threat than you have acknowledged, and the regime has demonstrated it can effectively control the Strait of Hormuz when it chooses. Mr. Secretary, I am concerned that you have been telling the President what he wants to hear, instead of what he needs to hear. Bold assurances of success are a disservice to both the Commander-in-Chief and the troops who risk their lives based on them. Our military has performed heroically, but military force without a sound strategy is a path to long-term defeat. I'd like to know what options you're considering now, given the cost of this war and the stalemate President Trump has put us in. More broadly, Mr. Secretary, too often you have made dangerous statements that are counterproductive to the mission. You boasted about, quote, "no stupid rules of engagement," just days after hundreds of Iranian schoolgirls were tragically killed in a missile strike. You have made troubling statements about showing "no mercy" and "no quarter" to the Iranians, orders that would constitute war crimes. As importantly, while our men and women are fighting and dying overseas, you have focused unduly on your own personal agenda. In the past two months alone, you have taken it upon yourself to overhaul the Chaplain corps, cancel flu vaccine requirements, repeal firearm restrictions on military posts, and bar service members from attending certain universities. Just this week, you brought performer Kid Rock to an army base to go for a joyride in an Apache helicopter after dismissing an earlier investigation into the pilots who recklessly chose to hover above his home. That runs directly counter to the chain of command and maintaining good order and discipline. Most disturbingly, during your tenure you have fired dozens of our most senior military leaders and personally interfered to block the promotions of many others. That is a betrayal of the merit-based system that forms the foundation of our military. You are hollowing out the military's bench of experience and highest performing senior officers while making young officers wonder if they should continue to serve. My colleagues and I have heard from countless service members throughout the ranks, many of whom will be watching right now, who are confused and disturbed by your actions. Hopefully you can explain them today. Additionally, this committee expects a fulsome update on Operation Southern Sphere. This ongoing campaign against suspected drug-trafficking boats has resulted in nearly 200 fatalities. The administration has failed to explain the long-term objectives of this mission or provide any evidence of reduced drug flows into the United States. I would ask for a credible answer to this most fundamental question: what is the operation actually meant to accomplish? Mr. Secretary, you are here to promote the President's $1.5 trillion defense budget. While this budget provides funding for necessary programs including shipbuilding and drone manufacturing, many other critical programs like barracks repair and aircraft procurement would rely on the passage of a party-line reconciliation bill. Further, this budget slashes research and development, provides no funding for Ukraine, and includes no funding for losses incurred from the Iran war. Yesterday, Mr. Hurst testified that Operation Epic Fury has cost $25 billion. If nothing else, that help clarifies that we certainly do not need a supplemental anywhere near $100 billion, much less $200 billion. And in this record-breaking budget, there is no pay adjustments for the civilian workforce, and with inflation that is a pay cut. After a year of DOJ layoffs and a hiring freeze across the department, this is an insult to the 800,000 men and women who support our warfighters every day. I cannot imagine a faster way to erode readiness and distract from our abilities to deter our adversaries. Ultimately, Mr. Secretary, I believe you are causing lasting harm to the military. Like many members of this committee, I had the opportunity and the privilege to serve in the military. And every officer knows they are duty-bound to give their best professional advice, even if it is not what their superiors want to hear. Because when leaders fear to speak honestly, people die, missions fail, and wars are lost. The American people's trust in our military took 250 years to build. You are dismantling it in a fraction of that time, and trust once lost can take generations to rebuild. Mr. Secretary, today I hope you'll take a step forward toward rebuilding the trust that has been lost. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Mr. Secretary, you are now recognized for your opening statement, sir. Well, Mr. Chairman, Ranking Member Reed, Senators, thank you for the opportunity to testify in support of President Trump's historic, as you said, Mr. Chairman, $1.5 trillion fiscal year 2027 budget for the Department of War. The President's budget request reflects the urgency of the moment, addressing both the deferment of long-standing problems as well as positioning our forces for the current and future fights. I'm honored to appear alongside General Dan Caine, Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, and Jay Hurst, our Chief Financial Officer and Comptroller. I'd like to start by thanking this committee and Congress for your partnership in securing the investments needed for a stronger, prouder, and more secure military. Your focus on acquisitions and efficiency are reflected in our department as well as in this budget. A nation's ability to build, to innovate, and to support the critical needs of its warfighters at speed and at scale is the foundation upon which its deterrence and survival rests. However, upon taking office on January 20th, 2025, President Trump inherited a defense industrial base that had been hollowed out by years of "America Last" policies, resulting in a diminished capacity to project strength. Under the previous administration we were offshoring, outsourcing, and beset by cost overruns and degraded capabilities. But under the leadership of President Trump, our builder-in-chief, we are reversing this systemic decay and putting our defense industrial base back on a wartime footing. Urgency informs everything we do. We're rebuilding a military that the American people can be proud of, one that instills nothing less than unrelenting fear in our adversaries and inspires historic morale and recruiting in its ranks. We fight to win in every scenario. The $1.5 trillion budget put forward by the President will build upon a previous $1 trillion FY26 top line and will continue to reverse the four years of underinvestment and mismanagement of the Biden administration. The $1.5 trillion budget will ensure that the United States continues to maintain the world's most powerful and capable military as we grapple with a complex threat environment across multiple theaters. Not to mention, the budget also includes a historic troop pay increase, 7% for junior enlisted, and the budget eliminates all poor or failing barracks. Quality of life for our troops is front and center in this budget. By supercharging our defense industrial capacity and transforming how the department does business, we are restoring American commercial dominance at a pace unseen in generations, transforming the defense industrial base from the broken, slow-moving systems of the past. We have flipped the Pentagon acquisition process from a bureaucratic model to a business model, decisively moving from an acquisitions environment paralyzed by bureaucratic red tape into an outcomes-driven organization focused on delivering the most for taxpayer dollars. Over the past year, through historic multi-year procurement agreements that this committee supported, we've cut smart business deals that have sent unambiguous demand signals to industry to build more and build faster. The result has been a surge, a revitalization of our great American factories and a massive reinvestment in the skilled American workers who serve as the industrial muscle behind our warriors. Hegseth, you're a war criminal! You use munitions on civilians! What you're doing is despicable! The American people do not want to go to war! We don't want to fight a war for anything! This is not acceptable! Stop Hegseth! This is war! Further interruptions of our hearing will be treated in like manner. We appreciate the First Amendment rights of Americans to express themselves, but disruption of this hearing will not be tolerated. So, Mr. Secretary, you may continue. I'll briefly provide some concrete high-level metrics of what we've accomplished over just the past few months. These are announced new facilities and investments to support American warfighters. The department has helped stimulate more than 250 private investment deals in 39 states, 180 cities, and 150 companies, worth more than $50 billion. It's resulted in 280 new or expanded facilities, more than 18 million new square feet of American manufacturing, and more than 70,000 new jobs. These $50 billion in investments in new plants, new assembly lines, and new factories are private investments, not taxpayer dollars. By completely transforming our department's business model, American companies are investing in America with their own dollars. This is a historic demonstration of American manufacturing and defense revitalization, all with their money, not Uncle Sam's. This has never been done before and is long overdue, moving from a bureaucratic model to a business model. These investments equal great things for America, for American families, and American workers to ensure that our warfighters have everything they need, all American-made. Together with the help of the policy updates and appropriations passed by Congress, President Trump's War Department has begun to turn the lights back on in our manufacturing towns across this country, forging a lethal arsenal of freedom. Every policy we pursue, every budgetary item we request serves to ensure that this department remains laser-focused on increasing lethality and survivability from the front lines to the factory floor. This is a historic budget, as you said, Mr. Chairman. This is a fiscally responsible budget. And this is a warfighting budget. Speaking of warfighting, the topic of Iran I'm sure will come up often today, which I welcome. I look forward to sharing the incredible success of our military effort achieved in a matter of weeks. President Trump has the courage, unlike other presidents, to ensure that Iran never gets a nuclear weapon and that their nuclear blackmail never succeeds. We have the best negotiator in the world driving a great deal. Unfortunately, as I said yesterday and I'll say it again today, the biggest adversary we face at this point are the reckless naysayers and defeatist words of congressional Democrats and some Republicans. Defeatists from the cheap seats who two months in seek to undermine the incredible efforts that have been undertaken and the historic nature of taking on a 47-year threat with the courage no other president has had, to great success and great opportunity for preventing Iran from having a nuclear weapon. Despite this, under President Trump we are restoring the unbreakable might of American manufacturing. We're providing for our warfighters and we are putting the people and interests of this country first. May Almighty God continue to watch over our troops wherever they are and may we honor the legacy of those brave Americans that we have lost. This is our sacred mission and this is what we will continue to execute on. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Thank you for that statement, Mr. Secretary. General Caine, you're recognized. Thank you, Chairman Wicker, Ranking Member Reed, members of the committee, and your staff who we never get to say thanks to. Thanks for having me today. I'm honored to be here alongside the Honorable Pete Hegseth and the Honorable Jay Hurst to testify on the President's fiscal 2027 budget. I'm grateful for the opportunity to testify today and I'm thankful for your continued partnership in support of our warfighters defending the homeland and our interests around the world. It's a privilege to speak with you today about the foundation of America's strength, the 2.8 million members of our joint force. I am continually inspired by the soldiers, sailors, airmen, marines, guardians, coast guardsmen, and civilians standing the watch for the nation, supported always by their families. They could have chosen a much easier path, any other path, but they volunteered for a life of purpose and passion and service. And every single day they rise to meet the nation's challenges, from combat operations to critical support roles, with the courage, tenacity, and grit that keeps our nation strong and secure. I would also like to express my deep gratitude for the 39 members of the joint force who've passed in operations, combat, and training during my time as the Chairman, and specifically highlight the 14 who've passed in Operation Epic Fury. The Secretary and I are deeply grateful for each of them and their families, and their names will never be forgotten. As the Chairman, my duty is to ensure our civilian leadership has a comprehensive range of military options and the associated risks required to make the nation's hardest and most complex decisions. I owe the President, the Secretary, and the Congress the truth at every turn, and my blueprint for this role has always been that of General George C. Marshall. His firm commitment to civilian control and a non-partisan military remains my constant standard, and I strive to emulate his candor, delivering the facts leaders need to hear, not always what they want to hear. Once a decision is made, executing it with absolute dedication while keeping the joint force precisely where it should be is the demand of our profession. As I sit before you today representing our incredible joint force, I want to emphasize my commitment to this committee and to the Congress. I will always follow General Marshall's steadfast example by providing clear and candid, non-partisan military advice, working together to ensure the military remains squarely focused on one thing: being prepared to deter and, if called upon, fight and win in our nation's war. And that is our mission. America's joint force is operational at its core, purpose-built for the realities in a complex world. We're organized, trained, and equipped to execute the most demanding missions across the globe with unrivaled precision. And over the past year, our warfighters have consistently demonstrated exactly what it means to be the most capable and most professional force on earth. Our shared goal is to ensure the joint force sustains the strategic initiative, advantage, and ability to project power to respond to the global challenges on our nation's terms. During Operation Rough Rider, Midnight Hammer, Southern Spear, Absolute Resolve, and Epic Fury, the joint force executed globally integrated missions alongside our interagency and international partners. Once our leaders made a decision, our forces demonstrated the unmatched ability to seamlessly synchronize actions and activities from the seabed to cislunar space. We're able to accomplish these complex things that we are asked to do because we draw from a deep reservoir of training, professionalism, and commitment. Our operational tempo is high, but we're designed to sustain it, rebuilding readiness every day, training professionals every day, and sharpening our edge every day. I am incredibly proud of this joint force team and the leaders at every echelon who command it. As the Chairman said, we are living in a complex environment. Today, I look forward to discussing how we can sustain America's military advantage, and I know this committee recognizes the challenges and the urgency in the environment that we face. We're operating in delicate and dangerous times where risk is scaling and the complexity of the modern battlefield demands America's constant adaptation, innovation, and partnership with Congress. As a joint force we're up to the challenge; we're built for this environment. However, our continued success is not guaranteed by our past achievements. We must continue to be forward-looking and innovate together with the Congress to drive the pace of change and maintain our superiority. It requires timely, predictable, and sustained investment. The resources we're going to discuss today are critical to modernizing the joint force and ensuring whatever threats might emerge, we are prepared to defeat them, to protect our interests, and defend the nation and win. This President's budget for 2027 supports the Secretary and the department's goal of reinvigorating and recharging the defense industrial base and the national industrial base, enhancing our readiness and securing our military advantage to ensure that our warfighters are properly armed, globally integrated, and ready, while always taking care of our people. And that is what truly sets America's joint force apart, especially the 1.8 million enlisted members of our joint force. It is them, the character and the competence of that force, that transforms our capabilities into a decisive advantage. Our enlisted force is represented today by the senior enlisted advisor to the Chairman, United States Navy Fleet Master Chief Dave Isom sitting behind me, a teammate who I greatly appreciate and many of you on this committee know from his time in the Indo-Pacific. While we face dynamic and dangerous times, I have absolute trust and confidence in the extraordinary men and women within our joint force who every day execute the missions we ask them to, quietly and with precision. Coupled with the American spirit to outthink, outcompete, and relentlessly innovate, we will maintain our decisive edge, but doing so requires your continued partnership. We stand ready today to answer the nation's call. I humbly ask that as we are here today in this hearing, we remember those deployed service members who are out there right now doing our nation's work. May we always remember our fallen and never forget them or their families who continue to show us what courage looks like. Thank you for your enduring support, and I look forward to your questions. Thank you very much, General. We appreciate your service. Let's jump right in. Secretary Hegseth, let's talk about the money from reconciliation 1.0 last year. There've been some complaints about the speed, but not everything we hear is actually accurate. How much of the 154 billion dollars from reconciliation has the Pentagon put on contract? My understanding, Mr. Chairman, first I'd like to say what an important vehicle reconciliation was for us and how it gave us a chance coming out of FY25 to advance the President's priorities, whether it's drone dominance, Golden Dome for America, shipbuilding, or the defense industrial base. It was a critical vehicle for us. The number you're looking for is about what I'm looking at, about 26 billion right now. But we've got the floodgates about to open and apply to those priorities. Okay. So unfortunately you're starting a bit late through no fault of your own because the money was not sent timely by the Office of Management and Budget to the department until last month. That's over and done with, but it should be mentioned. Mr. Secretary, where are we on the obligation rates as far as a normal appropriation bill? Are we a little behind, a little ahead, or what? I would say probably a little bit behind as it pertains to reconciliation, but part of that is, as you know, this is a new funding vehicle for the department. And twofold: one, you got to make sure you do it right and do it in a fiscally responsible way in conjunction with the Congress to ensure that we meet congressional intent. But also, we've been using it to energize our ability to exercise new pathways to get at problems in different and more dynamic ways that don't get stovepiped or stuck in the bureaucracy. So yes, there've been some delays, but ultimately, I think it's all goodness on the other side given the new nature of this funding vehicle. Right. Yes. Well, and things have been done differently, and we appreciate that. But Mr. Secretary, will you commit to us that you'll keep the committee informed frequently of your efforts to get all this money out the door so our industrial base can start building, as you have described, in this new flexibility that we provided them? Absolutely. And you mentioned a few things in reconciliation that you think have been game changers. I don't think we've talked enough about some of the game changers. For years, we failed to take action on rebuilding America's drone industrial base and critical mineral supply chains. After the last reconciliation bill and the National Defense Authorization Act, we're in a very different position on drones and critical mineral supply chains, are we not? Very much so, mineral supply chains and drones. We went from GIAT 4.1 to an autonomous warfare group. We're looking at the concept of a sub-unified command, and you're looking at 54 billion dollars in the FY27 budget dedicated to drone dominance: UAS, counter-UAS, ensuring we can scale not just exquisite drones but also the attritable ones that are proliferating on the battlefield today. We need to be ahead. Are there any other initiatives from last year's bill that you want to point out? And you only have a minute and a half. The investment in Golden Dome for America, the ability to get running on that, and we are on schedule to deliver capabilities inside this administration. Minerals, ensuring and shoring up supply chains on minerals. The Office of Strategic Capital, which, with its ability to loan, gives 10x to new entrants into the department. The opportunity to fund things that wouldn't normally meet the threshold for the department but give them the running room to invest in those capabilities, and we've already seen fruit from that as well. And briefly, General Caine, there's no question that Vladimir Putin's Russia is taking serious action to undermine our efforts for success in Iran. Is there any question about that? Senator, I think there're actions and activities that are mindful of the hearing room we're in, but there's definitely some action there. Thank you very much. Senator Reed, you're recognized. Thank you very much, Mr. Chairman. Mr. Secretary, you recently fired the Army Chief of Staff, General Randy George, who is one of the most distinguished and decorated officers of this generation. General George's nomination came before us, we reviewed it thoroughly, and we concurred. Why did you fire General George? Well, as I did then and I'll say now, we thank General George for his service. And out of respect to him and other officers, we never talk about the nature of why certain officers are asked to step down. But we all serve at the pleasure of the President. And ultimately, my view in coming into this department, as I stated in my confirmation hearing, was to change the culture of the department. And it's ultimately challenging to change the culture of a department with the same people who were a part of or in that department. So, I have made many changes with general officers. We will continue to make changes as necessary with general officers, and they will be in keeping with the trajectory of where we would like to take the department. But it doesn't take away from the service of those, and I think you'll note that every officer that's been asked to leave has been treated with respect. Interesting. Of the two dozen officers that you have fired for reasons unrelated to performance, since you have not indicated any cause, 60% are Black or female. Now, did the President direct you to single out female and Black officers to be dismissed? Senator, of course not. And as we've emphasized at this department from the beginning, the only metric is merit. Members on this committee and the previous leadership of this department were focused on social engineering, race, and gender in ways that we think were unhealthy for the department. Focusing on those things, making decisions based on those things. In President Trump's War Department, we make decisions based on only one thing: merit. And that's how we've made decisions going forward, that's how we've made them, and that's how we'll make them going forward. Well, let me go back to General George. What did he fail in terms of his lack of merit to continue serving? As I've said, I don't talk about the nature of dismissal out of respect for these officers. But ultimately, we want to take the department in a particular direction, certain services in a particular direction, and we want leadership that's running as fast in that direction as possible. And in some cases, we make changes accordingly, but do so out of respect to those officers. Well, I think that direction from your behavior is an intense interest in Christianity, in nationalism, and in not recognizing the talents of women and non-white gentlemen, and that's the wrong direction. I don't know what you're insinuating, Senator, but I am not ashamed of my faith in Jesus Christ. Now, and if you want to shame me for it, go ahead. Well, you shouldn't be, but are you critical of other faiths? I am a believer, I'm quite open in that, and our department allows for a multitude of faiths. So, I don't know what you're suggesting. I've heard the likes of things that people like you suggest to try to smear my character, and I won't give into it. I'm sorry, Mr. Secretary, but broadcasting before the National Religious Broadcasters, stressing the need for more Christianity in the military forces, doesn't seem like a neutral position in which you tolerate and accept all religions. Let me move on. The strategic aspects of this operation in Iran. The President declared that we're going to destroy their missiles and raze their missile industry to the ground. And after more than 13,000 strikes, unclassified assessments conclude that Iran retains more than 40% of its drone arsenal and 60% of its ballistic missile launchers compared with pre-war levels. That's one of his objectives. The second objective was regime change. To the great, proud people of Iran, I say tonight that the hour of your freedom is at hand, and we will finish taking over your government—well, when we finish, we'll take over your government. That has not succeeded. And then, one of his other things at the onset of the war: the President said we will ensure that Iran does not obtain a nuclear weapon. Military operations against Iran have not achieved that goal yet. And it also seems to indicate that his pronouncements about Operation Midnight Hammer obliterating the nuclear policy and structure of the Iranians was false. So, you have not achieved any of the objectives yet that the President mentioned. Well, in this setting I won't talk about the nature of metrics which are classified, as you know, Senator, but I can say that looking at the objectives we set out to achieve from the beginning, some of which you laid out, our military objectives have been stunningly effective. Look, take for example their defense industrial base. They're completely incapable at scale at any level of reconstituting the capabilities you referred to, which is a devastating result for any country, especially one whose ambitions are as wide as Iran's. So, we've put the President in a very strong position to ensure Iran never gets a nuclear weapon. That's the takeaway that's been underneath every single aspect of this. For 47 years, Iran's tried to blackmail its way to a nuclear weapon. They were closer than ever before because of bad deals under the previous administration. President Trump was willing to do something about it and not allow their conventional missile shield. That's the North Korea strategy. That's, to be clear, what Iran was pursuing: hiding their nuclear ambitions, revealing them over time, and then building a conventional shield of missiles so powerful that no country would challenge them for fear of what would happen if they unleashed that arsenal. Weakened after the 12-day war in Midnight Hammer, which did obliterate their sites, President Trump saw an opportunity because their ambitions continued, to ensure that umbrella of nuclear blackmail did not allow them to get to a nuclear weapon, and the world is safer because of his bold and historic choice. Mr. Secretary, I think that's rhetorical but not factual. Thank you. Thank you, Senator Reed. Mr. Secretary, Mr. Hurst, General Caine, welcome. Over the last several months, I've worked closely with some of the new direct reporting program managers and I've been encouraged by how they're approaching the department's most complex acquisition systems. General White has pulled forward the next milestone for the Sentinel program by at least six months. General Gutlein has completed the initial blueprint for the Golden Dome architecture and is beginning to build it out. For years, this committee has known that we must improve our ability to defend our homeland against a wider variety of threats, and we finally have a partner with the full backing of the department to lead the charge. Mr. Secretary, what's the advantage of this new type of program management structure? Well, thank you for the question, Senator. It's acquisition authority, technical authority, contracting authority; it's consolidating decision making in one place under a highly screened, highly capable general in General White and General Gutlein, who know that terrain extremely well and understand what mistakes have been made in the past in programs of that magnitude, and then are given the authority to cut through the red tape. That's the key. Success or failure lands with them, and they know it. As a result, they are incentivized to ensure that program, and then given every dollar and authority needed to move it as quickly as possible. So whether it's Sentinel, whether it's F-47, whether it's Golden Dome for America, these critical strategic assets, the direct report construct, along with Deputy Secretary Feinberg, who is a national treasure and is changing the way we do business at this department, is giving us a chance to ensure these critical systems are delivered. Thank you. And Chairman Caine, Secretary Hegseth, whoever would like to answer this, should our nuclear command, control, and communication systems like the SYAC be given the same level of priority as Congress considers the department's budget request as our triad? I think so, but I defer to the Chairman. Yes, ma'am, we got to be able to see to anything. So yes, ma'am. Thank you. Senator Shaheen, you are recognized. Thank you, Madam Chair. Secretary Hegseth, Congress enacted 400 million dollars to provide security assistance to Ukraine in January. Now, the committee received a notification just yesterday confirming only that the funding would go toward Ukraine. It contained no details about the type of equipment, no delivery timelines, nothing that is typically included in these notifications. And when asked about the delay in funding, the committee was told that Bridge Colby was developing a spend plan, but we've received nothing. So when can we expect the full spend plan for this appropriation? And Madam Chair, if this is not already part of the record for the committee, can I enter it into the record? Without objection. We acknowledge and are executing on the European capacity building amount of 400 million that you referred to. Under Secretary Colby's done a great job looking at options and worked very closely with our European commander, General Grinkovitz, so his requests of what makes the most sense will inform what ultimately is invested in. Well, this notification says that EUCOM coordinated on the spend plan in March, but General Grinkovitz told this committee on April 16th that he had not yet been asked to review any spend plan for this appropriation. So General Caine, have you received the spend plan for funds in Ukraine, and have you asked the EUCOM commander for his concurrence? I do not believe so, but I will find out, Senator, and get back to you by the end of the day. Thank you. And yesterday, Mr. Hurst, you told the House that you needed to seek legal review to appropriate the funds as Congress intended. So can you share with us what the nature of that legal review is that you have to get? And seems to me the law was pretty clear. I saw it, it was part of the defense appropriations bill that we passed in January, and as you know, violating congressional intent on appropriating funds is a violation of the Impoundment Control Act. So what's the nature of the legal review that you have to get? Well, thanks for the question, Senator. What we were looking at is if we could use the funds in the same manner as USAI, and we had our counsel look at that, and so they provided us a legal opinion on how the funds could be used to support European capacity building. And can you share with this committee what that legal opinion is? Ma'am, I don't have a copy of that, but we can ask the OGC office if they can supply it to you. Madam Chair, can we ask that that legal opinion is shared with the committee officially? Thank you. Also, I don't know who can answer this, but it says that consistent with the President's priority to shift the financial burden of Ukraine support to European partners, the United States will seek commensurate financial contributions via the prioritized Ukraine requirements list, or PEARL, from the European partners for this program. So what's the justification for using PEARL when there's 400 million dollars in appropriated funds? PEARL is a reflection of the President's priority and the belief that any weapons that are supplied are paid for by European partners and used as they see fit, whether it's Ukraine or otherwise, per the burden-sharing approach this President takes, which is important. But that was not congressional intent, and that's what I'm asking you: why you're using PEARL to do something that Congress intended to go directly to Ukraine, not paid for by the Europeans, but provided from the United States to support Ukraine. So again, I don't understand what the justification is for using PEARL when that's not the intent that Congress provided. Mr. Hurst, can you answer that? What was the legal opinion on this? Have you asked the attorneys if the 400 million could be used for the PEARL program? Let's get back to you; we'll take it for the record, ma'am. And what portion of the funding that's committed from the Europeans under PEARL is being used to assist Ukraine rather than restocking our own shelves? Can you answer us that? That's up to... that's up to Europe. Ultimately, Europe pays for any weapons that we provide, and they can utilize them as they see fit, whether it's Ukraine or otherwise. Thank you, Senator Shaheen. Thank you, Senator Shaheen.
