So if you're going to be replacing your entire software engineering team with models that are writing your code, you probably don't want that coming from China because it might be subtly introducing vulnerabilities that you're not going to catch. And so the trust barrier that I was describing, the trust barrier to enterprise adoption, continuously gets higher and higher. Right. And it sounds like, you know, Cohere's models have a very specific use case when you can have a model on-prem and you need the utmost security. When you say though that sort of the whole—so I think also what you're talking about is sort of the back door, right? That's been a concern with these Chinese models. You can self-host them but you don't know if there's a back door that can get into your data. Why then does AWS, the hyperscalers, Michael Dell, why are they hosting and promoting these open-source Chinese models? Well, for certain applications I think they're great, right? Like in low-sensitivity settings it's pretty reasonable to use a Chinese model. I think for startups that are looking for the lowest cost option and aren't operating at scale yet, it seems like a reasonable and very helpful tool. Of course, I think everyone would prefer to be using a democratically supported and aligned piece of technology. So I hope there is, and and Cohere's contributing to much more democratic open source. We want to continue to put that out there. But, you know, there is a market for these and they are cheap, as you say, and for the less secure settings maybe there's a place for them. Right. And, you know, you see sort of usage go up and you hear all the time about how we're compute constrained. So is that calculation for enterprises changing, especially if you're not in an industry that requires so much security? Is that calculation changing, especially because some of these open-source models are getting very close to the frontier and that sort of lag is closing? Yeah, cost control and then the other thing is like the compute bottleneck, right? So there's simply not enough compute to support using these massive models. So we need efficient small models that are good enough for the use cases that people are pursuing. Otherwise, we're just not going to have sufficient compute to meet demand. So there is a shift in the market. I think everyone over the past year has been racing to, you know, at all costs adopt AI. There's going to be another phase that we enter into where CFOs are looking at the expenses being spent on some of these models and they're going to try to optimize. They certainly aren't going to pull back and it's going to continue to grow, but we're going to need to find ways to use smaller models and more efficient models. I hope that doesn't mean shifting over to a Chinese tech stack to satisfy that. I think there are companies like Cohere and others who are building more aligned technology that satisfies that efficiency need. Right. But it does sort of beg the question, are the American models better than Chinese ones? Like what is the gap? Is DeepSeek V4 a real threat especially when we see sort of it matching or surpassing some of the frontier ones on the benchmarks? I think it's very close. The gap between the two countries, in addition to Canada, in addition to France, Germany, it's closing rapidly. Right. And you're saying that there's options. I mean Cohere's working on it, Nvidia's working on it, the startup Reflection is working on open source. Is there enough companies working on it in America and what happens to OpenAI and Anthropic if they aren't working on this sort of open-source model race? I think there's a lot of folks contributing to open source. At Cohere we've been investing in it for years now. I'm not so concerned about the number of players. I think there will continue to be good open-source options coming from democratic nations like the US, like Canada, like Germany. But I do think that the thing that China is doing that's setting it ahead is effectively distilling the large models. This is against the terms of service of a lot of these large models and so that gives them a shortcut. They can very quickly catch up just from distillation of someone else's work. Whereas for the rest of us that are trying to build a completely independent stack from scratch, it requires more effort and more work to build competitive great models. But I do see things converging. I do see capabilities converging between all the major providers. So what does that tell us about sort of the huge infrastructure spend that we see from the hyperscalers? Meta just talked about this coming off of massive earnings days. They're all planning to spend more than 700 billion dollars in infrastructure this year. From where you sit, especially as Cohere is building these very specialized models on just a few GPUs, does that money come back? Or do you think that the trend is going towards these smaller more specific models, the kind that Cohere's doing? I definitely think it's pointed towards more efficient models. There's going to be a big wave in the market seeking to reduce costs and make things more efficient. It's easy to do POCs on some of these large models, but then as soon as you push that into production and you're dealing with production-level scale, suddenly the price tag just changes the buying equation. And cost becomes a huge constraint. So I see things moving heavily in Cohere's direction. We've seen that over the past year with our revenue 6Xing last year and continuing to grow very rapidly this year. So I do think there's going to need to be a lot more infrastructure because the demand is virtually insatiable. But as that infrastructure comes online, we're also going to make a big push towards efficiency. So there will be much more AI in the world, but those AIs will be using less GPUs to do the same work as they were doing six months ago. Are the hyperscalers plus OpenAI and Anthropic, are they building for that kind of world? The hyperscalers are definitely building the infrastructure necessary to serve a significant amount of this demand. Although it's important to say that beyond cloud, on-prem deployments and private deployments, they're growing massively as well. And this is becoming an increasingly well-understood security threat that moving too much to the cloud is a mistake and exposes you to vulnerabilities especially in this new frontier of potential cyber attacks thanks to these models. And so on-prem deployments, folks building their own data centers, Neo-clouds, you're going to see a whole new array of compute providers to satisfy all of this demand. So certainly the hyperscalers are building out the infrastructure that is needed, but they won't capture the entire market. They'll probably capture half of it and the rest will be these on-prem security deployments. So where does that leave an OpenAI or Anthropic that's not, you know—the cloud business is not the main business, it's frontier models? I think the demand for frontier models and the demand for all models is going to be extraordinary. I think there's going to be—so it's basically a rising tide lifts all boats. Everything is going to benefit from this. I'm not too worried about the demand for their products or our own. There is a huge market out there. The applications for AI in the enterprise side, it feels like we're still scratching the surface. Like we're still doing very simple things. We know the models are capable; we know the models from 18 months ago are capable of way more than we're doing actually out in the industry. So even if you don't update the models for a year and a half, the industry is still catching up. So the demand piece, I feel extremely confident. There's a lot to go do in the global economy. A good question from Carson Allen, who's a regular viewer of our live stream, asking: are your pricing increasing as you see these compute constraints? No, so we don't actually charge our customers for the compute. We deploy inside of their private deployment. So it's their own compute. What we see with our customers purchasing their own compute, yes, the GPUs are becoming more expensive and there is a scarcity. You know, it's something that we try to help our customers with but our model of sales is not to provide the compute with the model; instead, we just provide the software. So are you in the server building business as well? Is that sort of what this looks like in the future? No, we don't do any infrastructure from us. We just focus purely on the models, the agentic platform that the models power, so that they integrate with all the different tools and data that enterprises use to automate things, right? Like to automate workflows for big banks, you know, inside Capital Markets divisions, wealth management, investment banks, we automate work and augment employees inside those organizations and we don't build the actual data centers themselves or help source it. They do that and you come in and implement the tools, the AI tools. Got it. Now Aidan, part of the reason I wanted to talk to you so much as well is of course you co-authored that famous paper that started essentially the modern AI age. This is a really general question. Watching where it's gone in, you know, a small number of years, you've got infighting at the top. Musk versus Altman's a big focus in San Francisco this week. You see growing backlash in the public. What do you think? Did you expect to be here? Do you think that the industry has mishandled the public image side of AI? I certainly—I mean it's a completely general technology. It's like computing, right? Like it can be deployed into any sector, it can be deployed against any use case. It is the most general piece of technology humanity has created. And so it's going to have very sweeping impacts and touch everyone's lives, both professionally and as consumers. So of course it was very important that that technology be developed in a way and communicated to the public in a way that was accessible, that they could try for themselves, that they could test and find the faults and give feedback. In terms of how the sentiment has evolved throughout that process over the past three years, I am concerned. I do think that you know there are certain folks who feel skeptical of the technology. Whether it's AI slop and in the creative industry there's a lot of resistance to this technology. I think we need to better compensate artists and ensure that they can contribute to the development of the next generation of models. Or whether it's the energy crisis, right? Like to power all of this adoption we need a ton of energy and if that is making things more expensive for people I think that's a hugely regrettable outcome. So investing in energy infrastructure, ensuring that prices don't go up for consumers as a result is a massive priority. I think people are rightly skeptical. I think criticism is a good thing, being aware of the weaknesses, being aware of the shortcomings about how the technology's being rolled out and the potential consequences is net positive in the long run. It's a tricky thing that both sides are figuring out. Both the public and the companies building the technology, we both want it to go well. That's a really thoughtful answer. Do you think we have the right spokespeople? Thinking of Dario at Anthropic and Sam Altman at OpenAI, Elon Musk. What's needed here? It is kind of like a narrative and image problem where you did mention really real things but also it does feel like this narrative problem at the top. Yeah, since you're Canadian I can say it, I try to bring a Canadian touch to things. I think we need to be empathetic, kind, thoughtful in this and not just steamroll people. And I think it's really great that the conversation is happening. I think it's essential. There should be criticism. We should respond to that criticism with action to try to mitigate the potential downsides for people. Well, we hope to hear a lot more from you especially that sort of nuanced thoughtful answer acknowledging both sides of this and the real criticisms. Aidan, thank you so much for taking the time. We really appreciate it. And from a fellow Canadian to another, thanks again. Thank you for having me. Well, back to Big Tech's earnings week. Stocks they are still under pressure today digesting the numbers that we got. Meta, Microsoft in the red. Amazon was sort of moving back and forth. The person who I want to break this all down with is Daniel Newman, CEO of the Futurum Group. He's been covering this sector for years, of course. Cloud Wars, CAPEX cycle, AI rollout. Dan, thanks for making the time. Really appreciate you being here. It's good to be with you. I'm not Canadian but I am Midwestern. So I think we can— And you told us that you like real maple syrup, but you can't skate, so it's okay. We're going to bring you into the fold today. I do, I'm as Canadian as I get, but I do believe in being polite. So I think that's an important value. My daughter went to North Dakota, University of North Dakota, for university and she brought back a little bit of that, which I think North Dakota is pretty much Canada when you listen to them talk. Pretty much, we'll take it unless the US wants to take it to Canada of course. But you know in Canada, Canadians they don't usually argue with each other, but I'm kind of looking forward to having a little bit of an argument with you. We talked about this off camera. I'm perhaps on the more skeptical side. I've been sort of posting about free cash flow, this cushion is evaporating, and I know you are super bullish and you're looking at those cloud numbers. So lay out the case because the market today is showing a little bit of anxiety. I mean these were great numbers across the board, right? Including from Microsoft and Meta. I mean Meta's topline is growing more than 30% yet the market is sort of looking at this CAPEX question. So why isn't the market telling us that we should be more worried? Well, I think the market is bifurcated and I think it's got this problem right now where it wants everything to be zero-sum. It's okay to spend CAPEX if you're Google and you're growing 63%, but it's not okay to spend CAPEX if you're Microsoft and you're growing 40%. And then of course there's a big debate Deirdre on fungibility because OpenAI basically all of their spend right now is being heavily discounted. You saw how that impacted Oracle, we saw how that impacted Microsoft. Now concerns now around AWS and how they're sort of doing their various financing. And you can even see with Nvidia for instance with all this great CAPEX expectation, Nvidia is selling on this news. So there is a certain amount of the market that still is actually expecting perfection. But the real question that I keep asking and I keep looking at is: is the demand real and is the demand fungible? And so what we're seeing in the numbers is that the demand is very real. You have very sophisticated companies with very capable leaders that are looking at the market conditions and deciding overwhelmingly that it is existential to their businesses to be making these investments. Whether it's Zuck, whether it's Satya, Sundar, they all understand that right now the biggest risk in their business is not over-investing. And despite the fact that they do have to be very realistic and very precise in their ability to execute to not go into a problematic state with this free cash flow, the other side of this is if they under-spend they could genuinely put themselves at a disadvantage that they may not be able to recover from. And a case in point to this is AWS itself. AWS was the slowest to come out of the gate. It spent the least on Nvidia in that first wave of CPU to GPU era computing and it has been digging itself out of a hole despite from a larger base now for the better part of eight quarters. It had a CEO in Adam Selipsky that departed. I genuinely believe it was for mishandling the Nvidia relationship. And now they are basically defending their turf against very capable competitors including Google, who basically has taken advantage of having the capacity because they invested more than AWS early on and plus they got to building their own infrastructure stack earlier on. The case is compelling. The business is there and there's a bigger margin of error for these guys to be wrong over-spending than there is for them to be wrong under-spending. Okay, that margin of error point is really important. Dario Amodei has talked about this and he calls it the cone of uncertainty. Basically if you're off by a year, that can be disastrous. You can go bankrupt, especially for like an OpenAI and Anthropic where the business model is sort of the LLM, right? So you talked as well about demand. Certainly demand is there but is it efficient? I keep coming back to sort of Uber as a really important data point, right? The CFO said that they blew through their AI budget. Now I don't think that they're pulling back on AI spending, however how do we know what true demand is and whether it's really efficient? Right, we just had Aidan Gomez on talking about how he's moving to more efficient smaller models and how he thinks that's going to be a trend. Are the hyperscalers built for that? Yeah, you're asking the right question and you and I believe it or not actually agree on this more than we probably don't. The normalization of the revenues related to the model subsidies that exist, right? ChatGPT and Anthropic are clearly subsidizing model delivery. Gemini makes largely the usability of its models free, of course they can offset that with advertising. Meta is spending big. It is generating more than 60 billion dollars in ad revenue and genuinely claims that AI is making that more efficient and more productive. But having said that, you know we've seen this agentic inflection. I mean look at Qualcomm—it's up double digit percentage today on the idea that it has a custom XPU CPU. We are so compute constrained that any company that can meaningfully add to capacity right now will rip and run forward. We saw the same thing with ARM, we saw Intel. These—this is a clear indicator of a trend line that's going on and that trend line is agentic compute. Now to Aidan's point, and I think he was very salient in it, is companies are going to get to this inflection. Every employee has a hundred, a thousand, maybe more agents that are running 24 by 7 consuming tokens, taking actions, doing work and that all comes at a cost of compute. If you can serve that up with a smaller, more efficient model it could be a Cohere and it could be an open-source model. It could be a Gemma model from Google. I agree with a lot of what he said about Chinese models. I'm heavily bet against China's ability to infiltrate meaningful regulated industries. But the fact is these high-quality models open source I think are going to be very valuable because serving up these large frontier models at scale— Right, but they don't need highly regulated industries, right? I mean look at this data on OpenRouter. Their already adoption is just through the roof. Yeah, I think the adoption's consumer. I think it's micro-businesses, small businesses. I mean even a business—you know we built our own AI platform. We are in the industry and technology research space. We provide data to investment and to technology service providers and technology companies. And even as we built our solution, like we wouldn't even consider a model that had Chinese roots. It's just it cannot be done. But I think Aidan pointed out the arguments clearly. There are use cases there. I think they're very efficient in how they deploy technology. We've seen how successful they've been in drones, how successful they've been in robotics, in automotive. I do think in the AI wars they have a meaningful disadvantage with EUV, with the access to semicap equipment. And I do think they have to be more creative in how they distill models and they've also had to be extremely clever in terms of how they can build accelerators and chips that can be competitive. But having said that, I always say never underestimate China's resolve and their willingness to make things happen even when, you know, the resources are not easily available to them. But having said that, I genuinely don't believe that the US is not going to be capable. And by the way I'll put this out there for everybody is I think Nvidia is going to solve this problem. Nvidia has been very outspoken about open source. I think they're very well positioned and as everybody starts to compete with everybody—the thing is this market's so big that everyone wants to be like well the TPU is against the GPU and blah blah blah. I don't know that that's the case. But what I do think is the model, the infrastructure, the platform, the stack, the agent—the more you can centralize and build the full stack, the better off you are. Google's getting great credit for this right now; I think they're up 10% today on their print. The more you can serve it all, the better off you are. Nvidia's not going to get into frontier model delivery, they don't want to compete with OpenAI, they don't want to compete with Google, they don't want to compete with Anthropic, but they will deliver open source. And I think they could be the answer. And of course I still think Meta—we don't want to rule them out too soon. Right. The original sort of open-source pioneer. It is kind of amazing like how quickly things change, right? I mean Amazon was seen as behind because they didn't have their own frontier model, but now it seems like you just have to be able to serve every kind of model and that's what the market is valuing right now. Yeah, I think there's two points there. I think Amazon was seen as behind because they underinvested in compute, they tried to get into models and I think they realized that it's not the game they want to play. They still have some models, but I still don't think that's where they are going to differentiate. What they have is massive scale and an unbelievably large consumption base from their existing CPU era cloud business, right? Run rate north of 160—I mean they're nearing in on 200 billion dollars of run rate business there. That revenue stream for them is incredibly valuable. And what they needed to do was secure enough compute and they've been building these various relationships, secure enough compute, secure enough growth to invest in more compute to make sure that they can solidify. One thing that people are forgetting is I think there's about 2% of the population's paying for AI right now and it's like 20% of businesses are using it. Folks like us, you and me, we live in this every day, we're on X, we feel just absolutely overwhelmed bombarded and insulated by things AI. But the world is still very immature in terms of AI adoption. And so when we look at the size of the market, the 30 billion dollars of run rate revenue for Anthropic, this is still based on a very small fraction of enterprises actually using and paying for this stuff. So we are extremely early and I think that opportunity for an AWS that has, you know, hundreds of thousands of customers to start adding AI to the repertoire of what they're selling with traditional compute and storage buckets—I think that opportunity's huge for them and they were able to catch up even though they did have a very slow start. Right. Very similar to what Aidan was saying is just more means more. But let's talk a little bit about backlog, right? Because that was really like a data point for the bulls last night. Microsoft more than 600 billion dollars in backlog, Google nearly 500 billion dollars. How do we know it's real revenue back to sort of this question? I know and not double counting from circular AI deals. I know you're used to this question, but lay it out for me. Well, first of all the circularity should not directly impact the size of backlog, but I'm sure there's some interesting accounting and some bears that have probably figured out how to extrapolate that and I'd love to look at that map. But what I would say is these deals where, you know, AWS is going to invest in OpenAI and then OpenAI's going to buy compute—that one number for how much compute they're going to buy, I think there is a genuine concern about OpenAI's viability. If there's one company that I think is worth having a concern about it is them. I don't think this week's news was necessarily the case, but I do think it has been shown that model as a moat is not nearly as deep as compute as a moat. And so they're chasing to have enough compute to be able to continue to build models that are differentiated enough to secure their longevity while others—you mentioned the open source—but of course Anthropic I think proved its point pretty effectively over the last quarter and continued to offer disruptive competitive products. And of course Google—can never rule them out, they've done incredibly well. So what I think is the backlog that is related to OpenAI for all these companies continues to be discounted. So if it's Microsoft's number, Oracle's number, AWS's number—and I think you're seeing that reaction as Google doesn't have that at nearly the same degree as those other companies—and I think the market has been somewhat punitive. Having said that, the one argument I continue to make is that the fungibility—I think there was a Stargate project that got cancelled and you saw I think immediately Microsoft stepped in. And my point is that the amount of compute that people are concerned about—that OpenAI won't be able to pay—I just think that compute goes elsewhere if that does end up happening. In terms of the model serving revenue and the people they're willing to pay for frontier models, if OpenAI suddenly didn't exist, would the people paying for OpenAI pay for Anthropic or pay for Gemini like an advanced Gemini model or some other paid model? And what we're seeing is the answer is yes. ChatGPT has enjoyed the benefit of being first and has some really good models, but I don't think necessarily it is the same entrenched sticky sort of revenue that you'd say Google creates through advertising and offering a free service to people. So that's how I look at this. So you're kind of saying that if OpenAI collapsed the whole AI trade is not doomed. There's just so much demand that someone will pick up their enterprise customers, consumer customers, compute that they've already developed, right? Okay that's heartening as sort of more information comes out or more concerns come out I guess. Okay, last question for you because we—one company we haven't talked about is Apple, reporting after the bell today. Doesn't have model or compute as a moat and yet, I mean they haven't exactly been performing terribly despite sort of sitting out in AI. And there is this idea that maybe it's going to live on the edge on our phones and this is similar to what Aidan was talking about as well, bringing it all on-prem. And that's a big question for the infrastructure build-out. Yeah, you know I've been more of an Apple skeptic for the reason that it hadn't necessarily found an AI story. It definitely fumbled along the way—Apple intelligence hasn't done great. You know the Vision Pro has not—now though that's not AI, but that's their metaverse—they flopped as badly as Meta did there. But in this case they maybe got something right and what they got right was kind of what I mentioned about AWS but even more so, right, was that if you have a big enough installed customer base, being early wasn't necessarily a requirement to be successful because Apple has the experience layer. So what we genuinely believe here is that Apple's experience layer continues to be dominant. Now there's been stories about you know Qualcomm OpenAI partnering on a new device platform, these new agentic devices. China has been very outspoken about getting into this particular space. And Apple hasn't really had an answer for that yet, but if you still walk through an airport, you walk down the street, unfortunately if you drive down the road and look in the window at the driver next to you, they're all looking at an iPhone. So Apple has the platform that still is the consumption layer. Where are you using Anthropic? On my iPhone. Where are you using OpenAI? On my iPhone. Where are you using Google and Gemini, Meta? On your iPhone. So I think they've enjoyed that installed user base and the ability to distribute that they've always had that's given them the chance. But I do think there's an inflection here where if there is a new platform, this tarnished transformation—he needs to come in, he needs to be more innovative, he needs to think a little bit more disruptively, they need to get some AI leadership in that's been a revolving door there. Because they have delivered, but if you look at Google and you look at Nvidia's performance over the same period as Cook, he got cooked. And so they need to bring some of that innovation back. Right. We'll hopefully get some answers on the earnings call tonight and of course the CEO change will add more data to how we look at the AI trade and where Apple is. Daniel thank you so much for making the time, really appreciate you being here. Great discussion. I think the message from both Daniel and Aidan is demand—they agree that demand cannot be fulfilled, which is what we hear from a lot, yet some of the cracks maybe showing particularly on the balance sheet side. In any case, please join us again next week. We're going to be talking about the most interesting thing, the most relevant thing in AI. Thank you to Jasmine, to Sammy, to Robert, to Evan, to Divya, to all of those in the back making this possible and hope to see you next week. Thanks again.

Episode 43: Jensen Huang on Generative Computing, Re-industrialization, & Physical AI

Special Competitive Studies Project(91.9K)·42:36·14.5K views·2026-04-30
Jensen HuangCEO, NVIDIA
TL;DRThe shift from retrieval-based to generative computing marks a new industrial revolution that offers a historic opportunity for U.S.
re-industrialization, provided the nation overcomes "existential" fears and prioritizes the rapid adoption of AI across all sectors of the...

TL;DR

The shift from retrieval-based to generative computing marks a new industrial revolution that offers a historic opportunity for U.S. re-industrialization, provided the nation overcomes "existential" fears and prioritizes the rapid adoption of AI across all sectors of the economy.

The Shift to Generative Computing

  • Paradigm Shift: Computing is moving from retrieval-based (accessing pre-recorded data like photos or stories) to generative (creating new information/tokens based on context and intent).
      • The Token Economy: AI reformulates numbers (tokens) into text, images, or biology/physics data. Huang likens this to "Tang"—the data is in powder form, and the computer adds "water" to create the final output.
      • Compute vs. Storage: This new era requires massive "AI Factories" (computers the size of football fields) rather than just centers of data storage.
  • NVIDIA’s Role: NVIDIA defines itself as a computing fabric provider, building the systems, software, and algorithms that connect chips to diverse industries like drug discovery (Lilly) and heavy equipment (Caterpillar).

The Five-Layer AI Cake & Re-industrialization

Huang identifies five critical layers where the U.S. must maintain leadership: Energy, Chips, Infrastructure, Models, and Adoption.

  • Economic Opportunity: AI is a market force capable of driving the re-industrialization of the United States, creating high-skilled manufacturing jobs that do not necessarily require a master’s or PhD.
      • Investment: NVIDIA has committed half a trillion dollars in consumption to move its supply chain from the East back to the West.
  • The Energy Bottleneck: Re-industrialization requires massive energy to "transform atoms."
      • Grid Modernization: The U.S. needs more efficient power grids with better service-level agreements to handle the energy demands of AI factories.
      • Sustainability: Sustainable sources like nuclear, solar, and fusion are essential for long-term growth.

Agentic and Physical AI

  • Agentic Systems: The current breakthrough is "harnessing" large language models to ground truth, research tools, and memory.
      • Automation: Most software tasks are now automated, though this is leading to *more* hiring of software engineers to handle increased complexity and innovation.
  • Physical AI: The science of robo-taxis is "solved" and now enters an engineering phase.
      • Humanoid Robots: Huang predicts these are 1–3 years away from becoming mainstream.
      • Challenges: The primary hurdles are mechatronics (motors, hands, weight-to-strength ratios) and battery technology rather than just the AI models themselves.

Geopolitics, China, and Open Source

  • Export Controls: Huang argues the U.S. should not concede the Chinese market, noting NVIDIA's market share in China has dropped from high levels to nearly zero due to current policies.
      • Dynamic Policy: He advocates for a "dynamic" export policy that favors the American tech stack while ensuring American companies lead globally in market share.
  • Open Source Security: Huang contends that open source enhances safety.
      • Defensive Swarms: Protecting against a "super agent" cyber attack requires "massive swarms" of defense agents built on open-source technology.
      • OpenShell: NVIDIA created "OpenShell" (a reference to "OpenCLAW" guardrails) to provide a virtual sandbox for agents, ensuring data privacy and policy control.

The Labor Paradox: Tasks vs. Purpose

Huang addresses the "stigma" and fear surrounding AI-driven job displacement.

  • Task vs. Purpose: AI automates tasks (like coding/typing), not jobs (like innovation/problem-solving).
      • The Radiologist Example: Ten years ago, experts predicted AI would eliminate radiologists. Today, there is a shortage of radiologists because AI enhanced the field rather than replacing the human professionals.
  • Software Engineering: While AI can write code, the demand for code is infinite. Huang argues we don't just need a "billion lines of code," we need a "trillion," and AI enables humans to reach that scale of ambition.
"The first layer [of the cake] is energy, then chips, infrastructure, models—and the most important part for every country is the adoption of the technology. This is the layer I'm most concerned about. We cannot afford for another country to leave us behind." — Jensen Huang
"Being human means to innovate and solve problems. Coding is just a task, like typing. If we didn’t have to type anymore, we could go and do all the things we imagine in healthcare, science, and manufacturing." — Jensen Huang
"We are so cinematic, so science fiction in the way we describe AI that we're causing consternation and fear. Meanwhile, the rest of Asia is embracing AI with great enthusiasm. This is how we get left behind." — Jensen Huang
Hi, I'm Ylli from SCSP.ai. For my next episode of Memos to the President, I just hosted Jensen Huang for the second conversation in less than 12 months here in our office space in Crystal City. We talked about layers of AI, we talked about China and export controls, and we talked about AI adoption and open source. Take a look at it; it's an incredible conversation ahead of our biggest event, AI+Expo, which takes place next week, May 7th through 9th, at D.C.'s Convention Center. If you haven't registered yet, please do so. We expect 20,000 people and incredible programming. Register, but first, watch the episode of my conversation with the CEO of Nvidia, Jensen Huang. Welcome back. I'm here sitting down for the second time in less than a year with the founder and CEO of Nvidia, Jensen Huang. Welcome. "Very happy to be here." It's an honor to have you, Jensen. And, you know, it's incredible. I was watching our podcast from last July. So many things have happened. So many things have evolved in the AI space. But let's start by talking about the state of AI first. You talk about the AI scale versus computer scale and what that means today and how it's different from the computer age. Can you elaborate on that a little bit? We're in a new computing paradigm. Artificial intelligence, if you break it down to the basics of computing, is essentially retrieval-based computing. The way we used to do computing is essentially retrieval-based computing. We would pre-record information—take a picture, record a video, write a story, do a brief, and so on—and we put it online. That's why we would put it in a data center, a center of data. And then based on whatever retrieval method you use, if you're shopping, it's probably a recommender system; if you're doing search, it recommended a list and you point and click; or if you use YouTube, it just feeds you whatever it feeds you. And so, then once you select, it would go and retrieve that file for you. And if you were a shopper, then it's very likely that based on your previous preferences and whatever context it is—where you are and from what other link did you come from, right, all the cookies you left behind—it might use that information to recommend one of maybe 16 versions of ads that's already created for you. It's all retrieval-based computing. So far, all of the way we largely use computing, we create content and we retrieve content. Well, the way that we do computing now with artificial intelligence is based on your context, based on your request, based on your intentions. It would then generate from some initial seed of information—it could be grounded in some fact, it could be grounded in a piece of report—and it generates information for you for the very first time. And every single time it's, you know, largely different. And so it's generative computing. It used to be retrieval-based, now it's generative. The benefit, of course, is that every time you use computing based on your context, based on the changes of ground truth, and based on your intention, intelligence would be applied and provide you the information that best suits you. And so that—makes sense? Okay, that's the big breakthrough. And the reason for that is we need so much more computation instead of a bunch of storage; we need a lot of computers. That's the fundamental difference. Now of course, the big breakthrough is because of that, we have to learn how to perceive information of all kinds—it could be text, it could be video, it could be images—we have to reason about what it is that you're asking me, the context and your intent, and then I have to plan to answer your question accordingly. It could be providing you an answer, providing you a summary, or maybe writing you a brief based on all the things that I've researched, or even doing some shopping for you. And so, we went from retrieval-based computing to generative, and on top of that, we've now made it possible for us to do tasks. And so, artificial intelligence is really about that journey. Right. I want to ask you a personal question here. When was the first time you saw a chatbot and how did you react to that? The first time I saw a chatbot was shortly after Nvidia built Megatron. Before the first large language model was announced, Nvidia announced with Microsoft the first large language model. It was called Megatron. And Megatron was about 400, 500 billion parameters. The technology that we invented to make it possible is pre-training the models. And together between us and Microsoft, we pre-trained a really large model at the time. This is probably late 90—mid-91, late 91. Okay, so this was before ChatGPT. And the thing that was great about it, of course, was that it encoded and was able to memorize a lot of knowledge. But the thing that we didn't invent that OpenAI invented to make ChatGPT useful is the concept of alignment, reinforcement learning, and human feedback. And as a result, you know, you give it a prompt, the Megatron—you give Megatron a prompt, it would generate just a—it would start spewing off all kinds of things that it was encoded in its memory, and it was just nonsense. And it was really—it wasn't very useful. And it was—we were waiting for another great invention; it was reinforcement learning and human feedback. But the first time I saw ChatGPT, I was mind-blown. It was—yeah, I was so happy to see it. Yeah. We have a segment we do every week here in our office called AI in the Office. We record every week one of our staffers, what did they do with AI last week? What did you do with AI last week that really was interesting? Yesterday, I was using it to finish my shareholder letter. And so I'll outline something and then I'll say, "Go off and read everything that I've already read, I've already said, and all the things that I've already written—all the keynotes that I've already done—and based on this outline, just populate it with things that I've already said. Just give me a basic framework." And then from that, I might take it and refine that, or I'm like, "Oh, that wasn't very good, I'll rewrite it all together." And then I would give it to the AI again to ask it to make it tone-consistent or, you know, highlight some things that it could do a better job on. Going back to Nvidia, writing is so hard—I hate writing—but you know, with AI it was tolerable. Yeah. Nvidia is more than just a chips company. It builds an entire ecosystem. And when you look at what you do, you not only invest in building the ecosystem that got you here, but you also build an ecosystem going forward. So how would you describe in Washington, what does Nvidia do? What is that ecosystem that you have built? We build the computing infrastructure for modern AI. And so, if describing modern AI as the new computing paradigm, then we are the computing infrastructure, the computing fabric that makes that possible. A computer consists of the chips but also the systems, the system software, and the algorithms, where it's computing—it could be in a data center, it could be on-prem, it could be inside a factory, or it could be in a base station like the work that we do with telecommunications. It could be, you know, in a car. And so, it doesn't matter really where computing is done, but we create the computing infrastructure necessary to do that. Each one of these different applications also has different algorithms, and our job is to translate the intention of the science to best fit into our architecture. And so Nvidia is really this collection of software, middleware, and essentially the operating systems of these domains that makes it possible for people to do all this computing. And then we connect all of that to the world's ecosystem of partners, whether it's Lilly for drug discovery or Caterpillar in energy generation, construction, and heavy equipment. And the number of companies we work with in the United States is basically everybody. Yeah. You have talked recently about AI being a five-layered cake. So I want to get into each one of the layers, and I want you to tell us—we're in Washington—where do we stand in each one of the layers? What are the things we need to do? What are the things where we are ahead? AI is a new computing paradigm. It is also a new industry altogether. And the reason for that is because the way that AI operates is so fundamentally different than the way computers operate. It no longer stores the information exactly as you described it and retrieves it from storage, from a disk drive or SSD. We now generate it, and the generation output is numbers. And those numbers are essentially tokens; we call them tokens. No different than—well, tokens are just a floating point number or some number. And we take those numbers and we reformulate them into the output that you desire. And the output you desire could be text, it could be video, it could be images, it could be sound. You know, kind of like Tang—it's in powder form and we add water to it and it turns into orange juice. And so we reformulate it in such a way that you can use. Well, that token generation process requires really large computers. And computers need energy. And so, the way that this industry has formulated is one: energy on the bottom; a whole bunch of chips and systems, computers—and these computers are fairly gigantic. I mean, you know, they're the size of football fields. And there's a lot of them. And so the next layer up from that is what we call infrastructure. Infrastructure is land, power, and shell, but also the software for cloud services. And then on top of that is models. And on top of that is really the most important part for the United States and every single country: the adoption of the technology. And this is in fact the layer that I'm most concerned about. There's one area that really is worthy of us spending time to talk about: making sure that we are mindful about, on the one hand, making sure we have proper guardrails and keep people safe in the application of the technology, but ensuring that the United States is in the front, at the pioneering front of applying the technology. It drives so much productivity and prosperity and technology as well as economic leadership. We can't afford for another country to leave us behind. We were the frontrunners of applying technology in the last industrial revolution. We need to be careful not to be the last in this industrial revolution. And so the five-layer cake starts with energy, chips, infrastructure, models. And even models, we of course think about large language models, which is the thing that we can engage with easiest and most. But remember AI can represent information of any kind, and some of the most important information we represent isn't language and numbers but biology, chemicals, physics, articulation, and animatronics. All of these types of information are represented by these tokens. And it's really important to recognize that when we think about AI, don't just think about the chatbot and the services we're talking about; there's enormous industries that are adjacent to it that don't get the voice that some of the AI companies get, but they're super important to the future of our nation. And so the AI models and the applications are on top. Yeah. Can we talk a little bit about the energy? Obviously all these models require a lot of energy going forward. We have a lot of hurdles in that space from the grid to building the necessary pathways. We work a lot on fusion, but you know, solar—so there's a variety of ways to get to the energy needed. How would you assess our energy situation right now? Well Ylli, that's a really important question. I think first of all, you've gotta take a step back and ask ourselves: do we want to re-industrialize the United States? Do we want to bring back to the United States this entire sector of the economy, this entire sector of labor that we consider essential to having a properly shaped society and a properly shaped economy? We've become a nation and an economy where unless you get a four-year degree, unless you get a master's or PhD, you're going to get left behind. And that's—that's unfortunate and unnecessary. I think we can all agree that that's unfortunate and unnecessary. We have to re-industrialize this country. For the first time in a generation, we have a market force that's incredibly powerful to drive the re-industrialization of our country. AI will cause us to create several plants. The first plant is chip plants. We're the largest AI company in the world today. We committed half a trillion dollars of consumption so that we can bring the supply chain from the East into the West—back to the West—so that we can build chip plants and packaging plants and computer plants so that we can build all of the manufacturing necessary for Nvidia's AI supercomputers to be built here and used here. So the first plants are chips, the second plants are associated with the computers themselves, and the third is putting these computers into AI factories. Altogether, we're talking about trillions of dollars of manufacturing and high-skilled labor jobs. We're going to create enormous amounts of manufacturing opportunity here in the United States. That, I think, is the first thing we have to confront: Do we want to be that country or not? Do we want to be that kind of society or not? Do we want to be that economy or not? Now, if we decide the answer to that is yes, then we obviously need energy because you need energy to transform atoms in one form to atoms in another form. You guys understand what I'm talking about. You have to break covalent bonds and you have to make covalent bonds. And in order to do that, an enormous amount of energy is necessary to change the phase of matter. And so, the whole concept of manufacturing is about energy. And so, we need energy in this country in order for us to re-industrialize. And that's—but as you build these—we are—and you ask me a question, I'm sorry—what is the state of our capabilities? Clearly, we are behind because of all of the policies we've had in the past. All the concerns about climate change caused us to, of course, under-invest in energy. Now, we want to be conscious about the environment. Everything has to be balanced. And so now the question is what do we do going forward? The first thing that we can do is we can modernize the grid; we can make the grid more efficient. We over-provision in the grid, as you know. We have to make sure that our grid can take care of our society and all of our infrastructure on 12 of the worst days of the year; the rest of the time it is over-provisioned by enormous amounts. And so the question is what kind of service level agreements can we come up with so that the power utilities can provide excess energy when they can? And during the times when they can't, now we have to provide for subsequent backup energy. The backup energy can come from solar, it can come from nuclear, you know, all kinds of sustainable energy in the future. But we have an opportunity right now with this incredible market-driven force to take advantage of this opportunity to, one, make sure that the United States becomes a manufacturing nation again and create enormous amounts of manufacturing labor and high-skilled, high-paying jobs. And then the second is to use this opportunity to enhance the energy system of our country. In July when we last spoke, you talked about the next wave of AI being agentic AI. My goodness. My goodness. Yeah. Can you talk a little bit about how you assess the state of agentic AI right now? There's a lot of conversation yesterday we've had here and in town about the state of agentic AI—you know, the successes, the failures, and what's happening right now. You know, the big breakthrough from large language models to chatbots was reinforcement learning and human feedback. The large breakthrough from large language models to agentic systems is a system called "harnesses." The model itself advanced, no doubt—the pre-training is improved, the reinforcement learning has improved, and teaching it how to reason better and tool use—all of that has improved. However, the big breakthrough is the concept of harnessing these large language models so that they have a connection to ground truth so that they could do research, so that they could use the web browser, so that they could reason and have memory and improve themselves and communicate with others. And so these agents, in the last six months, made enormous breakthroughs. Almost all of them perform incredibly well. We're big fans of Codex; Codex and Cloud Code both are incredibly good. Codex 5.5 just came out—huge breakthrough, big leap—and Cloud Code is obviously incredibly good. The thing that we can now do is that the vast majority of software tasks are now completely automated. And meaning that we don't have to do the programming ourselves. The one interesting observation, which was the prediction, was because of agents coming out, all software engineering jobs would be gone. That the first thing you should do is, whatever you do, plan on being a lot of things in the future but don't be a software engineer. Well, turns out the number of software engineers we're hiring is increasing. Every company is increasing; the number of software engineering jobs is increasing. And the reason for that is this—it's a very big idea. This also happened in radiology. Ten years ago it was somebody predicted that the first job that's going to go, the first industry that's going to go, is radiologists; that job is gone forever. And the reason for that is computer vision was going to completely transform studying these images. The prediction was 100% right: AI has now permeated every aspect of radiology. The only thing that was wrong was the prediction: radiologists are in short supply. And so the same thing is happening in software engineering. The reason for that is this: in our jobs, the task that we do—in the case of software engineering, the task is programming. You could argue it's kind of a fancy version of typing. However, the skill, the purpose of the job, is not programming; the purpose of the job is not coding. The purpose of the job is to innovate, solve problems, connect with collaborators, find problems that exist and solve them, or find problems that nobody's even expressed—that's called innovation—connecting unrelated things, creating something new. That's the purpose of software engineering. And so our engineers—their purpose in life is to innovate, solve the problems, and move the company forward. It includes coding, but coding is not their job; coding is their task that some of them do in their jobs. As I was saying, the next wave you predicted was the physical AI. Ten months later from where we last spoke about this topic, where do you think we are right now in the physical AI space? One of the best breakthroughs in physical AI—the first, the easiest one—is self-driving cars. Robo-taxis are here. The science is now completely solved. Now it's a lot of engineering. And even the engineering is just around the corner. So we're going to have robo-taxis. Have you ever used one of them by the way? "Oh yeah, yeah, sure, sure." How was your experience using it? Excellent. Nvidia makes robo-taxis. We created this software called AlphaM-IO; it's the world's first thinking robo-taxi, the thinking car, meaning it can come up to a circumstance that it has never seen before and it would reason about it. The way it reasons about it, it decomposes it into, you know, more mundane things. Oh, I've seen this, I've seen that, and in composition I understand what's going on, and so I know exactly what to do. And so the reasoning system has really expanded the capabilities of these cars. So the robo-taxis are here. What about humanoid robots? Right around the corner. And the reason for that is if you could imagine this—you guys know this—if you could prompt a video generator, and so ChatGPT has a new excellent video generator, and you say, "I want to generate somebody picking up a coffee cup and taking a sip," it would generate my hand picking up the coffee cup, knows exactly where the coffee cup is, and picks it up. If I can generate a video doing that, why can't I generate the robot to do it? All right, and so, I think you could imagine based on this, the other capability must be around the corner. And so, do you think this is like three to five years, or one to three years? What's the timeframe you envision these robots really becoming more mainstream than we are seeing right now? The big challenge right now is partly the AI model, but a lot is associated with the Megatronics. The motors, the hands, the structure—you want to be on the one hand light, on the other hand it has to be strong. And so, you want it to be light because if something were to happen and it tips over, having something that weighs 80 pounds tipping on you is probably okay; having something that weighs 300 pounds tipping on you probably isn't. And so, there's a very big difference between the current state of the art versus where we need to be, and we're going to get there. And so material science matters, motors matter, battery technology matters, sensors, and so all of these things matter and then of course there's the AI itself. And when do you think Olaf will become available at Target? I'm not hoping for Olaf, but you know the other robots that I have on stage usually with me from Disney—you know, they're incredibly cute. Who doesn't want their own R2-D2 growing up with them? Let's move to another serious topic. We're in Washington, Jensen. We've gotta talk China and export controls. You have been a proponent of us selling chips to China. There's a big criticism, there's a big opposition to that point about us not enabling our main competitor to get ahead of us. How do you address that criticism? The easiest way to think about that is to remember that AI is a five-layer cake. And the United States, America, should win in every aspect of AI. We should be the world leader in energy production and energy technology production. We should be the world leader in chips, we should be the world leader in infrastructure, we should be the world leader in models, and we absolutely cannot afford to lose this one: to be the world leader in AI application and AI adoption. The first thing that we should do is we should enable and encourage every single one of the layers of that cake and all the industries associated. Go lead the world, go lead in technology, go lead in market share, go lead in economic share, and go lead in the diffusion of our technology across the world so that everybody depends on the American tech stack. And so whether it's energy export or it's chip export, or it's infrastructure export or model export or application technology export, let's make sure that we're the world leader in every one of them. Let's export like crazy. As we know, we would love to have an incredible trade imbalance—meaning extraordinary amounts of exporting. And so why shouldn't we have AI, also, be in that camp? Now of course, we also want to make sure that from a technology perspective that we favor the American tech stack—meaning whenever we can, let's favor the American companies that are part of that stack. And so we should always have the best technology first and most to American companies. And we do. And so, you can find this balance: on the one hand, provide the best and the most to the United States; on the other hand, ensure that American companies win around the world. Yeah. The argument, Jensen, there is that across the five-layer cake, there is one particular layer that it's too important because in the others China can get ahead: they have cheaper energy, they have incredible talent so they can build—I mean they build already incredible applications, and adoption is probably much more popular there than here. So the compute becomes the essential node according to Washington—or some people in Washington—that you can slow them down. Not completely a zero-sum game, but at least they cannot catch up with where we are right now because if they do catch up and if it surpasses, then they'll become a global leader on probably what we have agreed is the most important technology of our lifetime. Every layer is important. Every layer is important. And we should make sure that we can accelerate the United States. Those two conditions, I think, both we could agree on: every single layer is important. The chip layer obviously is important. If the chip layer is not important, why are we talking about chips all the time? And so obviously the chip layer is incredibly important. Nvidia had, you know, call it 90-somewhat percent of the world's market share today; in China, we have now dropped to zero. Conceding an entire market the size of China probably doesn't make a lot of strategic sense. And so I think that that has already largely backfired. Maybe it made sense at the time, but I think the policy really needs to be dynamic and it has to stay with the times. At this point in time, I think it would be fairly safe to say that having American chip companies and other companies in China makes a lot of sense. I think that with respect to the model layer, we have to make sure that we do everything we can to help our model companies have the best, have the most, and have it first. And we do. And so, you know, this is the largest market in the world, we're the fastest market in the world, and the tech companies here are agile and they're innovative. I have every confidence that we're providing all the best here anyway. And so, I have every confidence that AI model companies here in the United States will continue to stay ahead. Yeah. I mean another thing would be that they will pursue self-sufficiency regardless of our policies because they have done it in almost every industry and they're pursuing it also in your industry. "Sorry." Well, they—let's see. They have self-sufficiency and pretty significant leadership in many layers. At the energy level, I think we can all acknowledge whether it's the production of energy or the technology that we use, they are the world leader. And so we are the world leader in chips. In AI models, I would say that we are ahead, we're unquestionably ahead; they're close behind. They have just such an extraordinary number of AI researchers. For whatever reason—it's because of interest in science and math and the encouragement of the social fabric—they just have such an extraordinary number of science and math experts. And as a result of that, the number of AI researchers in China is quite extraordinary; it's one of their national treasures, if you will—their greatest natural resource. And so we have to be mindful that we continue to attract that natural resource to the United States. We have to make sure that we welcome them here, that they want to come here. I'm quite frankly kind of concerned about many of them deciding to stay or not being allowed to leave. And so I think that, at the model layer, our technology is advanced, but we do deeply rely also on the talent that is around the world. And then lastly, at the highest level, my greatest concern—I remain very concerned today—we are so cinematic, so incredibly science fiction in the way we describe AI that we're causing so much consternation and so much fear around the United States. Meanwhile, the rest of Asia is embracing and adopting AI with great enthusiasm. And so, this is—this is something we have to be quite concerned about. This is how we get generally left behind. Yeah. At the GTC you talked about open source, and you touched upon OpenCLAW and how popular it is. I still remember that graph you showed, the downloads and everything. Open source is really incredibly important for AI democratization and accessibility and whatnot, but at the same time it brings safety and security issues. How does Nvidia address these things? So first of all, open source enhances safety and enhances security. The way that you're going to deal with a super agent cybersecurity attack is through a massive swarm of models that you adopted from open source, that you've trained to be a defense. A massive swarm of defense agents. And so you're not going to defend against a super agent with another super agent; you're going to defend it with massive swarms. And so you use asymmetry to your advantage, and that requires open source technology for us to develop upon. The CrowdStrikes, the Palo Alto Networks, the Ciscos, and the Microsofts all rely on open source technology to do that. Open source also has the benefit of being completely developed in sunlight, and so we understand the technology and we can keep it safe. And so safety and security is frankly enhanced with open source. With respect to OpenCLAW, it was such an incredible phenomenon. The concern that we had—which is the reason why we created, we invented two technologies. The first technology is OpenShell. We call it OpenShell because it's a shell, because it puts a shell around a lobster, you know, OpenCLAW. And so, the shell keeps it in a safe cage, if you will. And that technology was built at Nvidia as a starting point; we've contributed to open source, and it's being adopted by so many different companies. And the basic idea is to give it a virtual environment, a sandbox, and we're mindful about the type of information it can access and the policies that the policy engines will be able to control. The information that it can send in and out could be mindful about the privacy of personal information that's sent out. It could give you access to certain information that you cannot send out. And so all the policies associated with the privacy of each one of the instances of the Claw are now captured in OpenShell. And so we created an environment that makes it a lot more safe. Doing all of this completely in open source enables all of the world's enterprise companies to be able to adopt the technology because they understand what's underneath. And working with the US government and working with cybersecurity companies around the world, you know, this is how you're going to keep these agents safe. You touched on this really important topic throughout your conversation: AI adoption and the fear out there. Last time when you and I talked in July, we were both upbeat what the trajectory of this technology is going. But today, there are several elements that I think are boiling out there: you have the political discourse, you have the data center conversations, you have the state-level regulations, and you have the layoffs. Whether or not you can blame it on AI or the other things that have been happening from the tech companies, I'm afraid that we're entering this phase now where people might build a stigma around AI, which I think is not helpful because then you will have as a consequence a lack of AI adoption. How do you assess this? I mean you mentioned this throughout your conversation today that this is a big concern. There are things that are said that are very counterproductive and, in fact, hurtful. On the one hand, maybe a scientist thinks that by warning people that AI is going to completely permeate and proliferate across radiology and therefore radiologists are going to get wiped out—on the one hand that might be considered warning and therefore helpful, but in fact the counter would have been hurtful. If we convinced everybody not to be radiologists and we now need radiologists, that actually is hurtful to society. It is hurtful if we convince all the young college graduates to not be software engineers and it turns out the United States needs more software engineers than ever; that's hurtful. And so we have to be mindful of how we communicate the importance of this technology and what it's able to do. We have to advocate for policy and advocate for guardrails on the one hand; on the other hand, scaring people with nonsensical things which are not going to happen—that this is an existential threat to humanity, that there's a 20% chance that it's existential—that's ridiculous. That it's going to wipe out 50% of new college grad jobs, that it's going to completely destroy democracy. I mean these kinds of comments are not helpful. They're made by people who are like me: CEOs. And somehow because they became CEOs, you adopt a God complex and before you know it you know everything. And so I think we have to be careful and really ground ourselves in talking about the facts. The facts are this: AI has created more than half a million jobs in the last couple of years. The facts are AI is our best opportunity to re-industrialize the United States and to bring manufacturing jobs back to the United States. The facts are that's going to generate hundreds of thousands of jobs and trillions of dollars of new economy back into the United States. The facts of the matter are companies that use AI have demonstrated the ability to grow faster. When they grow faster, they hire more people. Apparently, AI creates jobs. And so the question is: why is it that on the one hand I'm telling you AI creates jobs, while on the other hand they say it destroys jobs? Well, the very simple idea is this: let's pretend for a second that the total lines of code that we have to write in the United States is one billion lines of code. And that one billion lines of code was supported by 10 million software developers and about a trillion dollars worth of the economy, and those one billion lines of code are now going to be automated because most of it is going to be written by AI. Well, if our job is basically typing and one billion lines of code are now automated away, you would come to the conclusion a trillion dollars worth of jobs will be destroyed. But that's fundamentally wrong for two reasons. The first reason is the task of our job and the purpose of our job are related, not the same. If you apply that to me, you would come to the conclusion what Jensen does for a living is tap on phones and talk. And tapping on phones and talking, AI has done that just fine, and therefore my job should be gone, but I'm busier than ever. And so there's a fundamental difference between the purpose of the job and the task of the job. And so that's one. The second thing is: it is a fundamental flaw that we only need a billion lines of code written. We need a trillion lines of code written. We need way more code written than that because we have the imagination of solving problems, whether it's in healthcare or science or manufacturing and retail. In all of the different fields that we have, we have lots and lots of imaginations of all the things that we can do. If we just didn't have to type anymore, we could go and do those things. And so maybe it's just because in the last 50 years society has taken this one little device with a keyboard on it and it consumed all of our lives to the point where we just can't imagine living without typing anymore. And so we'll figure it out, you guys. I mean, the idea that being human means to hunch over on this little thing typing all the time—you know, 50 years before that, people didn't do that. In the future we're going to do less of that and we're going to do more of something else. As you know we have the Expo next week, and I think part of the reason why we do the Expo is because we want people to see the prospect of this technology. And at the Expo people have the opportunity to build robots and to see what universities are doing and tech companies are doing with AI. So unfortunately we will miss you next week, but I know we worked with your team to have Nvidia's presence there. It's a big event for us. "The biggest, yes." We have lots and lots of people coming; it's such an important work and I love that you're celebrating AI through the lens of healthcare, energy, manufacturing, retail, and quantum. Different fields of science and different industries and different professions. And in all of those lenses, I think the people that have engaged with it are so incredibly excited. All the engineers at Nvidia today use AI. AI basically does most of our coding, and yet we're hiring more engineers than ever, we have more challenges than ever, we have bigger dreams than ever, and our ambitions are greater than ever. And so, if you just said this to yourself: suppose we infuse AI into this country and as a result of that, we are doing things faster than ever before. Our ambition is greater than ever before. Our expectations are greater than ever before. How is that a bad condition for our country? That's exactly what we want: to be more ambitious than ever, to be faster than ever, to be better than ever. And so, I hope that we adopt AI irrespective of anything else. Whatever we do, help people understand how AI has been transformative to so many people and so many industries already in just the last six months. In just the last six months since it's been really useful. Prior to that it's been incredible but not useful. Now it's useful and incredible. And so, I really do hope that every industry, every company takes advantage of it. We're surely all in on it and we're supercharged by it, exhilarated by it, and our ambitions are greater than ever because of it. Jensen Huang, thank you so much for coming by. All the best and I'll see you again. Thank you Ylli. Thank you. Thank you so much, thank you.

Sam Altman in conversation with Patrick Collison

Stripe(103.0K)·58:20·29.9K views·2026-04-30
Sam AltmanCofounder and CEO, OpenAI Patrick CollisonCEO, Stripe
TL;DROpenAI is entering its third phase as a "mega-scale token factory for the world," aiming to become a low-margin, high-volume infrastructure utility that accelerates global economic output and scientific discovery.
Sam Altman predicts that AI will soon move beyond coding to...

TL;DR

OpenAI is entering its third phase as a "mega-scale token factory for the world," aiming to become a low-margin, high-volume infrastructure utility that accelerates global economic output and scientific discovery. Sam Altman predicts that AI will soon move beyond coding to automate the "drudgery" of all computer-based work, while significantly compressing the timeline for breakthroughs in material science and biotechnology.

The Evolution of OpenAI’s Mission and Strategy

  • Three Phases of OpenAI: Sam Altman outlined the company’s structural trajectory:
      • Phase 1: A pure research lab focused on the "crazy" goal of building AGI.
      • Phase 2: The development of a product-focused company (ChatGPT, APIs).
      • Phase 3: A global "token factory" providing massive-scale intelligence as a new public utility.
  • The "Infrastructure Play": Altman views OpenAI’s future as an infrastructure provider similar to Stripe, favoring a low-margin, high-volume business model aligned with customer success.
      • Switching Costs: He notes that as AI gets smarter, switching costs between models actually decrease, making high-margin "moats" harder to maintain and further incentivizing the utility model.
  • Massive CapEx Requirements: Altman describes the current build-out as the most expensive infrastructure project in human history.
      • Demand vs. Price: He believes demand for intelligence is effectively uncapped, provided the price continues to drop and speed increases.
      • Bubble Concerns: While journalists frequently call a "CapEx bubble," Altman argues that the current scale of demand makes the massive investments look reasonable.

Product Frontiers: From Coding to "General Drudgery"

  • The Coding Threshold: The recent "parabolic" growth in AI utility was triggered by models hitting a reasoning threshold that made them effective for coding.
      • Codex and OpenClaw: These tools represent a shift where the AI becomes the primary interface for the computer.
      • Automation of Drudgery: The next major unlock isn't a specific domain like spreadsheets, but the automation of "switching between apps" and "copy-pasting"—the small tasks that break creative flow.
  • "Revenge of the Idea Guys": In the startup ecosystem, technical talent remains vital, but the barrier to entry has lowered.
      • Founder Traits: Altman is now more willing to fund "idea-driven" founders who deeply understand their users but may lack traditional coding skills, as AI handles more of the technical execution.
  • Headcount Projections: Despite the massive scale of the business, Altman expressed a desire to keep OpenAI’s headcount extremely lean, ideally only doubling from its current size over the next five years.

Science and the OpenAI Foundation

  • Accelerating Discovery: Altman believes the most important contribution of AI will be accelerating science, potentially achieving a decade’s worth of progress in a single year.
  • The OpenAI Foundation: This non-profit arm will be one of the largest foundations in the world, focusing on science and AI resilience.
      • Material Science: Altman identified this as the most underrated domain for AI impact, noting its potential to revolutionize catalysts and physical materials.
      • Biotech and Health: OpenAI is supporting efforts like the Arc Institute to tackle complex diseases (e.g., Alzheimer’s) through foundation models like Evo-2.
  • Energy Breakthroughs: Altman made the bold prediction that the world may see a profitable nuclear fusion reactor within the next five years, driven partly by the massive energy demands of AI data centers.

Management Style and Philosophy

  • Hands-Off Leadership: Altman describes himself as a non-hands-on manager who hires great people, gives them a high-level goal, and stays out of the way.
      • Context Gathering: He maintains a "pulse" on the company by communicating with hundreds of employees daily via Slack/text to gather diffuse context.
  • Managing Elite Talent: The "magic" of OpenAI was getting highly capable, often iconoclastic individuals to work together long enough to reach breakthroughs. This was achieved through a shared, deep conviction in "scale" and concentrating resources on a single research program.
  • Iterative Deployment vs. Power Concentration: Altman defended OpenAI’s decision to release tools like ChatGPT early and often.
      • He rejects the "ivory tower" approach where a small group of experts controls the technology, arguing instead for democratized access so the world can build on it.
"We have to figure out how to build this like mega, mega scale token factory for the world... we need to make that as smart, as cheap, as abundant, as easy to use as possible." — Sam Altman
"I think we're going to try this new experiment where we start sending like an FTE to work... hands-on with the CEO of a company to automate their job as much of it as they can." — Sam Altman
"The most important contribution of AI... is that if we can start to discover new science at a much faster rate... life gets better because we understand science better." — Sam Altman
Please welcome back Stripe co-founder and CEO Patrick Collison. Good evening, folks. I really hope you've enjoyed the announcements and the sessions over the course of the day thus far. I am extremely excited about this interview. As you know, I was due to be interviewing Greg Brockman, who was a very early Stripe employee and then a co-founder of OpenAI. But AI is a dynamic space and there is a lot happening, so we've made a slight substitution. Instead, I'm going to be interviewing somebody that I've known for a long time. I was just counting backstage, and I think I've known him for 18, maybe 19 years. He was actually one of the very earliest investors in Stripe—I think Stripe's second or third investor—and he also co-founded OpenAI in 2015. Please welcome to the stage, Sam Altman. All right, I think we have some Codex fans in the audience. Love to hear that. Sam, how's the week going? It is so fun, but it is a busy week. I'm happy to be here. This was an unexpected surprise. Well, thank you for joining us; we appreciate it. We opened this morning by saying that we've kind of arbitrarily decided that the singularity started on January 1st, and thus, today is day 119. What do you think of that? It does feel like we are somehow in the takeoff. Day 119 feels like a reasonable enough guess. Yeah, I won't fight it. Did you feel this? We've started to see a bunch of our metrics inflect as of late last year and the beginning of this year.
