Things were kind of doing well, but somehow the shapes of the curves changed and really went parabolic. Is that matched in what you guys see? Was there some trajectory change? I do think the models got really good, especially for coding, but really good in general starting late last year and very early this year. In my own experience of using the technology and seeing what other people are doing with it, there is this sense that every week is now a little bit different than the week before. Just a lot happens very fast. It seemed to all correlate with the models hitting some threshold. Why did coding models suddenly start to click over the last couple of months? Was there a research trick, or was it that you just got enough code data in the pre-training? It's a great question. We've wondered a lot about why several people crossed that threshold at the same time. I'm sure it's a number of factors, including raw reasoning horsepower. It was enough of a feedback loop of people using it for code to figure out where it was good and where it needed to improve. I think it was all of these things. Also, like many other endeavors, once you know something is possible, it's much easier to go do it with vigor. It seems like Codex is kind of having a moment right now. It really crossed some subjective threshold for me with the latest app updates in 5.5. It is quite hard to say why right now and not a little bit sooner, or why not the next model. One of the things I have learned about the history of all the things we've put out is it is very hard to say why this particular thing was the thing that worked. This goes back to ChatGPT. Why was GPT-3.5 the thing that got over the threshold where most people went from saying "not that impressive" to "gonna change the world"? Why not one model earlier or later? I really can't explain it. You just kind of feel it. I've had two inflection points with Codex. One was kind of with GPT-5.2 and then a really big one in the last few weeks where it's like, "Okay, this is going to be the primary interface to a computer for me." Is everyone using it for coding, or are you starting to see usage diffuse into other domains? The most adamant users are still using it for coding, but there's been this tidal wave of people coming into Codex recently. I'm really trying to understand what has happened to cause this. The depth of what people are starting to use it for has surprised me. Certainly, our ambition is for it not just to be about coding, but to be about all the work you do in front of a computer. I would say we're maybe 10 percent of the way there for the non-coding stuff, but now that we see what's happening and we have a real user base using it in these other ways, I think we'll get good at it very fast. What do you think will be the next domain that subjectively feels like it has this big unlock after coding? Is it going to be spreadsheets, or performance reviews? I do think coding is a little bit special and these models are a great fit for it. The world needs so much more code than currently gets written. There may be no other domain that is quite like coding, but there will be a lot of others that I think are close. The next coding-like thing that I think will happen is not any specific domain, but the realization of how much time people waste trying to use a computer. There is an idea that you can do a huge percentage of your day in a very different way. You don't realize how much time you spend clicking between messaging apps and copying and pasting stuff. Responding to very boring things that you could clearly automate once is the key. The degree to which most people will realize they can sit back and watch an AI do most of their drudgery is going to surprise people. In my own experience trying to work that way, it actually gives me much more enjoyment of work. I didn't realize how much the little stuff drags me down or gets me out of a happy flow state. So the subjective quality of life improvement is huge. Are you an OpenClaw user? I am. Any OpenClaw users here? We have some good news for you all coming. You can just tell them. OpenClaw has been one of my biggest "this is magic AGI" moments ever in the field. I remember the first time someone told me about it. They were trying to explain it and I was like, "Okay, that all sounds cool, but I can make a lot of that work." Then it was a real reminder of what happens when the models cross some threshold. When the product designer gets a handful of critical ideas really right, it's a much more magical experience than it sounds like. I find I've been an attempting OpenClaw evangelist, trying to describe that experience to others, and I find it difficult to communicate. It sounds kind of prosaic: a stateful ChatGPT session that can also make some use of tools. What do you use OpenClaw for? If we scrolled your message thread, what would we see? This is a very embarrassing thing to admit. You're in the perfect place. The thing I always try first with a new kind of AI system is to try to build a better home automation interface because it never works. It's never as good. OpenClaw was the first time that I was able to get a setup that I was happy with. I also built a messaging app that I had always wanted to work. I've since switched it to something I built with Codex, but OpenClaw was the first time I was able to automate the unpleasant task of waking up and going through a drowning amount of messages. That should have been doable with previous systems, but it's hard to explain what it's like when it all actually just works and you trust that it's going to work. I was testing the new Link CLI that we just launched today and I asked my agent to buy itself a gift—anything on the internet for under 20 dollars. It chose to buy itself an HTTP zine from Gumroad. Wow. There is all of the stuff that feels strange, no matter how convinced you are intellectually that this is not a real thing wanting a real gift for itself. No matter how much you're convinced that this is a weird emergent behavior and you're not supposed to read into it, there are things that feel a little strange. We're going to have a party for GPT-5.5. I wasn't quite sure what to do, so on a whim, I asked 5.5 what it would like for a party for itself this morning. It gave this beautiful set of things, including what it would want for the flow of the party and what it would not want. It suggested we should do it on May 5th because that would be funny. It wanted only a short little toast, and it wanted the toast not to be by itself, but by the people that built it. It wanted a big central suggestion for 5.6. It wanted me to feed those suggestions into it. Now there's real moral pressure on you, but it was a strange thing. I want to ask you about OpenAI itself. It is now a 10-year-old organization. It somehow feels like so much longer than that, but yes, just over 10. A long 10 years. I can't remember pre-OpenAI life that well at this point; it feels like it's been so long. You have a singularity looking forward but also a singularity looking backwards. What's the craziest OpenAI story that's never been told? It sounds so prosaic relative to the crazy drama that's happened, but there was this period after we had finished training GPT-4. There were about eight months before we released it. There was this eight-month period inside of OpenAI where we were all using this thing. We kind of knew that it was dramatically better and different and was going to unlock a bunch of things in the world. No one outside the company or almost no one outside the company knew about it. We'd walk the halls sometimes and wonder if we were engaging in collective psychosis. We wondered if we had just totally whipped each other into this frenzy. There was no feedback to keep us in check or sane from the outside world. It doesn't sound that weird relative to crazy board drama or the Elon trial, but living through it was an unbelievably strange time. What's the Sam Altman management style? If I'm working for you directly, what does that look like? I'm definitely not a hands-on manager. I'm very much of the style that you get great people, give them a very high-level thing to point at, and try to let stuff just happen. I think there have been two main phases of OpenAI and we're heading into a third. The first one was when we were only a research company. We were trying to figure out how we were going to build AGI at a time when it sounded completely crazy and we really had no idea what to do. Then there was a second phase where, in addition to continuing to do that, we had to figure out how to build a product company. Now we have a third phase. We have to figure out how to build this mega-scale token factory for the world. I think of what we're doing as building sort of a new utility. People are going to want to use a lot of tokens and a lot of intelligence in all sorts of ways. We need to make that as smart, as cheap, as abundant, and as easy to use as possible. That will require pretty deep full-stack integration and a massive infrastructure build-out. The thing that I didn't really appreciate between the phase one and phase two shift was how much my management style had to change. Running a research lab and running a product company are two extremely different things. I suspect this third phase is going to be very different yet again, so I've been reflecting on how I'll have to change. I think it's not going to be a natural fit for my management style. I either have to find someone great to hire, or figure out how to do things in a different way, or build an AI that can manage this new thing. When I interviewed Jensen here two years ago, he told me about his 60 direct reports. Do you have any unusual practices like that? The closest thing I have is that I probably talk to a few hundred people at the company a day via Slack or text. It's very quick, one or two messages, not done by an agent. I actually do it myself. The context I get from that sometimes is very helpful in these diffuse ways. I find there's an interesting watershed of pre-Slack organizations and post-Slack organizations, and they're truly quite different. I hate Slack, but I can't imagine having to still communicate via email or whatever we used to. There's a view that the AI labs are going to progress up the stack, gobbling up the value chain voraciously. There is a fear that there will be this incredible positive feedback loop and a hegemonic force that we should all be getting very concerned about. What's your view? I think some of them do want that, but we don't. One of the things that I have always admired about Stripe is it is very clear that Stripe is aligned with its customers. We make more revenue, we charge our customers more. Thank you, by the way, for the partnership with Stripe when ChatGPT launched. It was extremely critical, and I don't think anyone else could have scaled that quickly. But we scaled, we pay you more money, it's very aligned and we're all happy. You just provide a layer of infrastructure for the internet. I don't know exactly how to do this yet, but I would like to get to a model for OpenAI that is similar. I would like us to be an infrastructure provider. I'd be happy for us to be a forever low-margin business as long as we can be huge and growing fast. I would like us to supply an intelligence meter that companies can buy to automate things or build products. People can take it with them and we find ways to align ourselves with the success of the entire economic engine of the world. I believe that will work. I believe that switching costs of AI are going to make it hard to have huge margins anyway. You've seen recently how easy it is for people to switch from our competitor's coding product to ours. This is a consequence of AI getting smarter; it gets easier to just say, "Hey agent, go do this thing for me." So many people have been explicitly critical of OpenAI for procuring so much compute. You were quite noteworthy for stipulating what sounded like preposterous figures for the magnitude of the build-out required. Obviously, the preposterousness of those figures now looks less tenuous by the day. Thoughts on compute CapEx and the build-out? It's going to take a lot of money. I think this will clearly be the most expensive infrastructure project that the world has ever undertaken. The revenue is ramping to meet it, so people feel better about that. Also, the efficiency gains that we've all been finding are incredible. We're going to get way more out of each GPU than I thought we were going to. As it has often been remarked, the demand goes up more than linearly as you drop the price of each unit of intelligence. This is especially true if you can drop the price and the speed with which you get it back. The question now of "what is enough" is something I don't have a good answer to. In some sense, I think demand for intelligence at a low enough price is effectively uncapped. We're not going to build the Dyson sphere and then just cover it with data centers, but maybe we do. Space data centers? Good luck with that. I don't even think he's that serious about it. I don't myself think that we're in a CapEx bubble. I'm not an expert in this, but the figures I see relative to the magnitude of the demand look reasonable to me. People love to proclaim bubbles. I can't articulate why, but intellectually I kind of get it. It feels fun and it feels smart. Journalists, in particular, love to talk about bubbles. So there's ample desire to write about this whenever anything looks a little bit silly. Clearly, sometimes it's right. But how you can discern between the amount of time someone calls a bubble and the amount of time you're actually in a bubble, I have never figured out how to do. In my previous career, I was an investor, so I was quite interested in trying to see if I could come up with some sort of framework for this. I never was able to figure it out. I read what smart people had said at different points in history and they called it exactly right, but they also said it 10 more times in the 10 previous years. Your business OpenAI depends in a very significant way on super talented people. These effective people are not in every case super easy to work with. Some are wonderful people, and some are the most fantastic collaborators, but some are very iconoclastic and strong-willed and they get easily irked. How do you think about managing elite skill here? Do you tolerate prima donnas? Someone working on a book about OpenAI once told me that I figured out how to get a lot of people, who all thought they were the only capable person, to work together long enough to figure out the breakthroughs. What's the trick? A lot of pain. I think we had very deeply shared convictions even when people didn't like each other. We collectively believed in scale and concentrating resources on this one thing. We thought getting this right was important enough that people were going to put aside various personal conflicts. One of the most unusual things about OpenAI was that at the time we trained GPT-3, the vast majority of our compute was going into this one single research program. We would talk to people that we were trying to recruit from DeepMind, and they would say that's insane and that it was going to create a terrible culture. John and I have been doing this thing at Stripe since 2010. You and Greg started out in 2015 and you've ventured through many trials and stratospheric successes. It's not easy to work successfully with a co-founder for a decade. Why has that partnership worked? How have you made it such a success? Obviously, you and John knew each other for longer than Greg and I did, but Greg and I did know each other for a long time before OpenAI. I think having the shared history really helps. One of the things that I had observed at Y Combinator was that one of the biggest predictors of success was how long the co-founders had known each other. The teams that came together seven days before applying to YC on a co-founder matching site rarely worked. It was not impossible, but it was rare. We had known each other for a while and had a sense of shared values. We were clear on what we wanted to do. We had this deep mutual respect and complementary skill set that has just worked really well. I'm extremely grateful. I think having to go through any startup experience without a co-founder you have a deep connection to is really hard. I've watched people do it, but it's very hard. There's this entire ecosystem of companies building on the platform. It's an interesting moment given the ability to build products at unprecedented rates. You are one of the most prolific and successful startup investors ever. Have the traits that make founders successful changed in this era? There was a time when we used to make fun of the "idea guy." These people wanted to start a company and say, "I have the best idea, I just need a coder to build it for me." It was kind of always personally annoying to me because it's like saying, "I have a great idea for a song, I just need that guy with the guitar to make it for me." YC had a version of this where teams without technical founders were difficult to make work. All of a sudden, it's like the revenge of the idea guys, which is actually awesome for the world. For a long time, the most important ingredient we looked for was technical talent. Now, people who deeply understand their users and can't code at all—I want to fund those people. How does one think about startup investing these days? You have a couple of years potentially to AGI or the singularity, and then you have funds with 10-year time horizons. How does that all fit together? I think to do anything at this point on a 10-year time horizon requires a real suspension of disbelief. And yet, that's probably the right way to live your life. I don't think it works to say there's this singularity in three years or five years and we can't see past it, so we're going to do nothing. You have to live as if stuff is just going to keep going in an understandable way for a long time. How far ahead does OpenAI plan? I mean, we find 20-year power and land agreements for infrastructure. For the product, I think we have a clear vision of what things can look like in two years, and then it gets much hazier after that. There was a narrative that GPT wrappers were undifferentiated and flimsy and would be swept away by model improvements. Now it feels like that narrative is flipping. Instead of talking about wrappers, we talk about harnesses, and harnesses are seen as having significant heft. What's your view on their durability? I have kind of had the same view all the way through, which is you as a business want to be on the side of hoping that AI gets smarter. In the early model days, if you were a GPT wrapper patching a weakness in the current model, when the next model was much better, you were kind of sad. If you were doing something that benefited from intelligence, you would be happier. I think the same thing applies to harnesses. The right way to think about this is that the data center, model, and harness is just one cluster out of which comes usable intelligence. There are so many things to go build where you're just happier for that whole cluster to get better. When you look at the organizations making the most effective use of AI today, what specifically are they doing that's different? Tobi Lütke of Shopify was the first CEO I knew that just said we are going to be all-in on AI in the way we run our company. He got his hands dirty just building AI automation of everything. It was not a token leaderboard or some gamified thing. It was the CEO saying we are now going to put AI into everything we do, and then really holding people to it. That energy has now been done by other people, but when the leader of a company really holds people to it and ideally does it themselves, that has worked very well. We're going to try this new experiment where we start sending an FTE to work hands-on with the CEO of a company to automate their job. That will have a nice fractal effect throughout the company. A second thing is being uncomfortably permissive with data access. There are huge reasons not to do this, and I'm stopping short of a recommendation, but it's what I've seen from the most effective companies. It's easier for small startups than companies that have a lot of sensitive data. Saying you are going to record your meetings and let the AI have access to the code base and every Slack message is very powerful. I don't know how the world is going to decide the tradeoffs on data privacy versus AI efficiency. I think there is some regulation that's going to have to change for that, but it is so powerful. Where is open source AI going? Does it have a future? For sure. Right now people want smarter and cheaper frontier intelligence, but there is also a lot of demand for open source. I know science is something you're very excited about. OpenAI recently made a grant to the Arc Institute. Can you speak a little bit to AI as applied to science and how you think about grant-making in the context of the OpenAI Foundation? I hope that this will be the most important contribution of this technology. If we can start to discover new science at a much faster rate, life gets better. Starting with the models of a few months ago, but really now with 5.5, the models have gotten smart enough that excellent scientists are saying they are able to figure out better ideas. The pace of science is going to increase. Eventually, we'll have automated labs and robots. If we can start doing a decade of science in a year, the compounding effect will be extremely great. This will be one of the big areas of focus of the OpenAI Foundation—money, expertise, and technology to accelerate science. I think this will be the biggest foundation in the world. We're really focused on science and then AI resilience, which means helping the world through this transition. We were thrilled to support Arc. I think it's clearly the best AI and bio effort. If we can make even a small contribution to treat diseases, we will be very thrilled. I thought that was going to take longer, and looking at the incredible work the Arc Foundation is doing, I now think maybe it won't be that far off. This is your interstitial ad for the Arc Institute. There is an Arc Institute booth downstairs. The Arc Institute is an organization we started four years ago. Its goal is to produce the first cure for a complex disease in humans, like most cancers, autoimmune diseases, or neurodegenerative diseases. Humanity has never cured a complex disease, not one. We started Arc Institute with this goal, and Alzheimer's is the first complex disease that we're targeting. Our hope is that with new genome engineering technologies like CRISPR and amazing advances in AI, we'll be able to make some meaningful progress. The early results are very encouraging. The Arc Institute is currently looking for a CTO. We had one CTO do a sabbatical at Arc last year, and he helped us train Evo-2. But we're looking for a full-time CTO and we thought that person might be in this audience. If you know somebody for whom that sounds of interest, go check out the Arc stand downstairs. That was good. It's so much better that you label that as the interstitial ad rather than just doing it. You were the second investor in Stripe. YC and Paul were in that Palo Alto kitchen. We were two pimply teenagers proposing building a financial services institution. Why did you invest? Honestly, because you and John were two of the most impressive founders of any age that I had ever met. I clearly was right about that. I had known of you on the internet. I was struck that you were solving a problem for yourself and that it fit a trend. I really believed commerce was going to move online in a huge way. If you can find really smart founders and a market that's going to be big, you should just invest. What's your advice for Stripe on navigating the AI era? I remember when investors would outsmart themselves saying Stripe's going to be a commodity. It turns out that if you make a great product, people are still going to need to take money in the future. They are probably just going to stick with you if you're a good ecosystem partner. Every company needs to get more efficient with AI. But this whole mindset that every company is going to go away and everything is going to be completely different—maybe it will be agents that need to handle payments instead of consumers, but money's going to have to move somehow. Adopt AI especially internally, and use AI to build better products, but don't assume that the entire socioeconomic system completely reconfigures. I think the world has gotten a little bit delusional about this. If we index OpenAI headcount to 100 today, what do you think OpenAI headcount is in five years? I would love it to be 200. Apart from AI, what technologies excite you? I'm obsessed now with data center infrastructure. There's so much cool stuff to do there. I think there will be amazing new technologies at the physical layer, including energy and robots. That stack is probably what I think about the most. The world is finally making a little progress on brain-machine interfaces. I'm excited about that, and I'm excited about progress in biotech. I'm excited about new kinds of computer interfaces. We are in an insane era right now where we're stuck with these old devices and old operating systems. When do you think we'll have the world's first profitable nuclear fusion reactor? It depends how far electricity prices get pushed by data center demand. Maybe sooner than we thought. I'll guess in the next five years. That's a bold prediction. Hypersonic commercial air travel? I don't know, more than 10 years. Are there any domains of science and technology not in the discourse that you think will be accelerated by AI? Material science. It's not a cool thing and I think people underestimate how much of the world is materials. It's a beautifully AI-shaped problem, getting new catalysts. I expect rapid progress there that will impact all of our lives. How do you hope your involvement changes the trajectory for the world? I believe in democratization and personal agency and that everybody deserves a really great life. The most controversial decision we made in the history of OpenAI was what we now call iterative deployment. A lot of the thinking when we released ChatGPT was that this is insanely dangerous to do. People thought only a small set of people should know what's coming. That sat very poorly with me. I believe it is extremely important that we avoid that kind of power concentration and that we build this for the world. By enabling people to explore this very wide opportunity space, we will give the world a gift. The world will build a much bigger gift on top of it. I am a believer in entrepreneurship and that people are mostly good and do amazing things with tools. My biggest contribution will be pushing for this to be a democratized technology. Sam Altman, thank you very much. Thank you for joining us.

Hassett on Powell Staying at Fed, Inflation and Oil Spike

Bloomberg Television(3.1M)·12:32·4.7K views·2026-04-30
Kevin HassettDirector, National Economic Council
TL;DRNEC Director Kevin Hassett anticipates a "sea change" at the Federal Reserve under Kevin Warsh’s expected leadership, advocating for interest rate cuts to fuel a productivity-led growth cycle while dismissing current energy spikes as temporary disruptions that do not warrant rate hikes.

TL;DR

NEC Director Kevin Hassett anticipates a "sea change" at the Federal Reserve under Kevin Warsh’s expected leadership, advocating for interest rate cuts to fuel a productivity-led growth cycle while dismissing current energy spikes as temporary disruptions that do not warrant rate hikes.

Federal Reserve Leadership and Independence

  • Leadership Transition: Hassett expressed disappointment in Chairman Jerome Powell’s recent comments but signaled that the administration is focused on the expected confirmation of Kevin Warsh as the next Chair.
      • "Sea Change": Warsh is viewed as "fresh leadership" that will restore institutional independence and shift the Fed's current trajectory.
      • Confirmation Outlook: Hassett described Warsh’s confirmation as a "lock."
  • Fed Independence: The administration maintains it respects the Fed’s autonomy despite public criticisms from the President.
      • Inspector General (IG) Role: Hassett defended the Justice Department’s request for the Fed’s IG to review internal matters, arguing it is a "completely respectful" use of the Fed’s own internal oversight.
      • De-escalation: The administration hopes for a de-escalation of tensions once the transition in leadership occurs.

Economic Outlook and Monetary Policy

  • "Blockbuster" Economic Data: Recent indicators show a robust economy, characterized by high capital spending and historically low unemployment metrics.
      • Capital Spending Boom: High import levels are being driven by capital goods used to build domestic factories, rather than perishable consumer goods.
      • Employment Strengths: Jobless claims are at their lowest since the 1960s, and government employment is at its lowest level since WWII.
  • The Case for Rate Cuts: Hassett argues that interest rates should be reduced to "supercharge" the economy, specifically targeting the housing market.
      • Residential Investment: Noted as a current weak spot (down 8%) due to interest rate sensitivity; lower rates would stimulate new construction and associated consumer spending.
      • Productivity Buffer: Like the 1990s "Greenspan era," Hassett believes a "productivity boom" will keep core prices under control despite high growth.
  • Inflation vs. Energy Shocks: Hassett warns that hiking rates in response to a "temporary spike" in energy prices would be a "policy error."
      • Core vs. Headline: The administration views core inflation as stable and a better predictor of future trends than the volatile topline inflation influenced by oil.

Energy Markets and the Iran Conflict

  • Strategic Pressure on Iran: High oil prices and $6/gallon gasoline in California are attributed to the geopolitical conflict with the Iranian regime and its nuclear ambitions.
      • Sanctions Impact: Hassett noted that Iranian production has previously been squeezed from 5 million to 1 million barrels per day under strict sanctions, and the administration intends to maintain pressure to stop their nuclear program.
      • Economic Blockade: Reports suggest Iran is facing hyperinflation, food shortages, and business closures as a result of the current blockade.
  • Domestic Mitigation Efforts: The White House has implemented several measures to shield Americans from price spikes:
      • California Drilling: Reopening offshore drilling in California that was previously shut down by the state.
      • Logistics & Standards: Implementing Jones Act waivers and adjusting ethanol standards to increase summer fuel supply.

International Relations and European Critique

  • Tensions with Germany: Hassett addressed recent friction between the President and the German Chancellor regarding Iran policy.
      • Policy Critique: Hassett characterized German energy and economic policies as "unwise" and "anti-free market," suggesting these policies have left Europe overly vulnerable to Middle Eastern energy shocks.
      • Growth Stagnation: He noted that European economies are currently struggling to achieve even 1% growth and urged partners to help resolve the Iranian nuclear threat to stabilize the global market.
"I think when Kevin [Warsh] gets in there, it’s going to really be a sea change at the Fed that we’re all looking forward to." — Kevin Hassett
"It would be a policy error for the ECB or the Fed to hike rates into a temporary spike in energy prices... The best forecast of future inflation is not topline inflation, but core inflation." — Kevin Hassett
"Our expectation is that things are going to get better very, very quickly if we can just get the Iranians to finish the negotiations and realize that it’s in the interest of their own people to stop things that could turn into serious hunger events." — Kevin Hassett
Let's talk about this economy in just a moment. I think we need to follow on from the Federal Reserve and the news conference with Chairman Powell just yesterday. Stepping aside as the Fed chair, but staying on as a governor. Kevin, was anyone in the administration given a heads-up ahead of time before you heard that announcement? You know, I've... communications with the Federal Reserve and the administration are still open when there are policy concerns, but I can't really discuss that. I can say that I'm disappointed in what Jay said, but I'm going to sort of stay out of it as we await just a couple weeks Kevin Warsh's confirmation. I think when Kevin gets in there, it's going to really be a sea change at the Fed that we're all looking forward to. Kevin, in the meantime, the President has articulated his disappointment like you just have. Previously, the President suggested that if the Chairman stayed on beyond his time—not sure what he meant specifically by beyond his time—that he would fire him. Is the President intervening? Are there certain options to intervene? You know, I think that in the end, our hope is that Chairman Powell understands that the Justice Department has stood down. They've just asked the Inspector General at the Fed to look into this, which is something the Inspector General is probably doing anyway. And so, I think that it's time to de-escalate and move on. Kevin, do you think the President has sufficiently de-escalated to allow him to move on? Well, you know, he doesn't think so, and and I think that in the end, he'll study the matter and we hope he comes around. The Treasury Secretary says it's an insult to the other Republican nominees on the board, the likes of Governor Waller, Mickey Bowman—people that you and I know fairly well. Do you believe it's also an insult, and does it complicate the way this particular institution will operate in the months and meetings to come? Again, I don't want to start getting too much into having the White House argue with the Fed right now while we're waiting for Kevin Warsh's confirmation, which looks like it's a lock to me. The bottom line is that Kevin Warsh is going to go in there, he's going to be new leadership, he's going to be fresh leadership, and we fully expect that that's going to help everybody see how important the independence of the Fed is. Now, as a logical matter, if you just sort of say, hey, we'd like the IG to look into something, the IG is the Federal Reserve's Inspector General, and so it's completely respectful of the independence of the Fed. We do respect the independence of the Fed, and and I think that the best way to show that it's not a partisan organization is to move on. There has been a pretty aggressive push to reduce interest rates. At the same time, this Federal Reserve is shifting towards articulating a more symmetrical reaction function. Not there yet, but getting closer to it, Kevin. Supporting that is the economic data from earlier on this morning. Do you think there's space just to wait to see to work out how things turn out before thinking about reducing interest rates? Right. Well, well, first of all, like, let's let's talk about the economic data for a second because it was just kind of a whole bunch of blockbuster numbers. The 2% number, actually, you know, for me being the capital spending guy—I used to be the capital spending guy at the Fed when I was a young economist—the thing that was amazing, as you know and you highlighted, was the capital spending boom. And that one of the things that sort of dragged it down from a really high number was that the quality of the imports has skyrocketed. And so almost half of the imports were capital goods which were coming in to build factories in the US. Typically in the past, you know, our imports are things like consumer goods that are perishable. And so, so the bottom line is that it's really strong data. We got additional claims the lowest they've been since the 1960s. We got government employment the lowest it's been since the Second World War. We've got all these positive movements in terms of fiscal responsibility and economic growth moving forward. And so then, but we do have this temporary disruption with energy markets, which is very serious. As you mentioned, the energy prices are high, gasoline prices are high. But you know, my read of the academic literature on what you do when you have an oil shock like this is not that you raise rates into it, especially given how I think we're highly confident that this is going to be a temporary disruption. Futures markets agree with that as well. And so I think it would be a policy error for, you know, the ECB or or the Fed to hike rates into a temporary spike in energy prices. And their own, you know, economic studies sort of suggest this is true, because if you look at it, what they want to do is make sure that future inflation goes back towards the target. The best forecast of future inflation is not topline inflation, but core inflation. And core inflation in the last CPI number was fine. The PCE kind of crept up a little bit, but it doesn't tell the story of the topline. And the topline is the kind of thing that I think someone could overreact to. It might not be appropriate, Kevin, to have a rate hike at this point. But do you agree with your colleague Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, who came out and said it might be appropriate to stay on hold for a while as this does progress and works itself out? Again, I am highly confident that the productivity boom is going to keep core prices under control, and that the... you know, my old friend Alan Greenspan in the 90s saw that because of the computer revolution that we could have high growth and it wasn't a Phillips curve kind of thing that you needed to respond to, as he let us have four or five of the best economic years that we've seen in my lifetime. And I think that we have that opportunity again and it would be a shame if we squandered it. So you think that it is appropriate to currently cut rates, despite the increasing concern by some of the Fed members that we are entering a potentially inflationary moment? Do you think it's inappropriate to go to something more of a symmetric type of approach? Well, they they've been sort of saying we're leaning towards cutting, and then there was some dispute about what's going to happen at the next meeting. But but I think that getting interest rates down could really supercharge the economy from where we are, but not be super inflationary. And so you saw that the weak spot—the weak spot in the GDP report—was residential investment, which was down 8%. Residential investment is kind of the most interest-sensitive thing there is, and I think that Fed policy has a chance of moving the yield curve in a way that could really help home buyers. And and one of the things that I remember from the 90s is that when we want to have sort of low inflation, high growth, then one of the things that happens is you get a lot of construction, residential construction. And when you have residential construction, it has a big positive effect on GDP because you have like an empty house that has to get filled with a washer-dryer and a TV and carpet and furniture and everything else. That's much different than if you have like an old house where you're moving from one old house to another. And so new construction is really the thing that could lift this to 4 or 5% growth, and it's being held back by high interest rates. What's the pathway to get there, given the fact that the longer-term yield curve is seeing some of the highest rates that it has this cycle? 5% in the 30-year yesterday for the first time in a while. And frankly, a lot of people in market think that if the interest rate were cut at the front end, that would only exacerbate the creep higher in yields at the long end. How do you see that? Yeah, I mean, there are times when that's true, but not a time when we're in the middle of a a huge positive supply shock. And so I think a little bit of movement, and Kevin Warsh, hopefully soon-to-be Chairman Warsh, has described how one can do that and maybe play with the balance sheet a little bit at the same time and move things in a direction that helps America's homeowners. And and I think that if we could get that last little bit of the economy going on all cylinders, then we'd really, really be back very close to talking about the golden days. Kevin, we have triple-digit crude this morning, WTI much higher. And actually what you're seeing across America, especially in a place like California, they've actually breached $6 a gallon when it comes to gasoline. I know the White House has some measures. They've extended the Jones Act waiver. But what else are you thinking about? Well, we've already done a whole bunch of things including, you know, basically opening up the the drilling that offshore drilling in California that was shut down by the state. And that's actually, you know, alive and pumping out barrels. The bottom line is that that we're very convinced that this is, you know, a suicidal Iranian regime that was set on really big disruptive things in the region that would be perhaps permanently harmful. And that when we had them signal in their negotiations that they were just about ready to have 11 nuclear bombs, then the President decided to take action to defend the entire earth, for goodness sake. And so right now the thing that I look at it in in terms of the economic effects of the war is how difficult it is for Iranians right now. As if the government doesn't care about them at all, and and so they're having a hyperinflation, they're running out of food, they don't have any distilled product to drive around in their cars, they're shutting down businesses, even the the Persian rug dealers are shutting down. It's time for that government to just see that they shouldn't aspire to have a nuclear weapon, and if they just give that up then we can go back to having normal relations, one could hope. Right, but what could this government do to shield Americans from higher gasoline prices? Are things like an export ban on the table? No. What we've done is we we've as you know we've done the Jones Act, we've opened up drilling in California, we've, you know, pursued like changing the ethanol standards so that the corn ethanol can be used more in the summer. We've we've taken every single step that we can, and we understand that that you know, it would have been a lot worse if we hadn't done that. But the fact is that our expectation is that things are going to get better very, very quickly if we can just get the Iranians to to like finish the negotiations and realize that it's in the interest of their own people to stop things that could turn into serious hunger events and so on. Kevin, unfortunately—and you and I are probably in agreement here—unfortunately they've got a track record of doing a lot of things that aren't in the interest of their own people. In the meantime, there seems to be a preference to maintain the blockade and put the squeeze on them. How close are we to them breaching storage capacity, so to speak? Do we have a decent gauge of where things are at inside the country? You know, like like there it's a well-to-well thing. But the basic idea is that if you shut down a well, then the pressure can build and then the, you know, oil can kind of squirt everywhere, and then when you open the well up again, it you get a lot less oil out of the well. That's the sort of theory of what could happen. And it's certainly possible because they're running out of storage that they're going to have to shut in wells. The one thing I can say is that their production went from 5 million barrels a day to 1 million barrels a day because of President Trump's, you know, policies in the previous term when he really put in tough sanctions on Iran to try to stop the nuclear program. And after Joe Biden came in, he just stopped the sanctions, and the energy production went back up to 5 million barrels a day over a couple of years. And so the Iranians, you know, they had it took them a couple of years to get things fixed, probably with new wells and so on, because it can harm wells. But but we have an experience of them being shut almost down to nothing and then getting back up. Kevin, are partners stepping up to help? We're just hearing from the President here out on social media, so I'm sure this is the first time you're hearing of it. I'll read it for you: "The Chancellor of Germany should spend more time on ending the war with Russia and Ukraine and fixing his broken country, especially immigration and energy, and less time on interfering with those that are getting rid of the Iranian nuclear threat..." "...thereby making the world, including Germany, a safer place." I believe the German Chancellor earlier this week suggested that the president was being humiliated by the Iranians. Just what is the relationship like, Kevin, with our European partners at the moment? Oh, you know, I think that the European partners are feeling stress because their very unwise energy policies have exposed them much more to energy shocks from the Middle East. And it looks like the German Chancellor is losing his cool a little bit, and I would suggest that, you know, he take a deep breath and recognize that the best way for Europe to get its economy back on track is to help us resolve this problem. And then also examine their own policies, which are so anti-free market that their economies are, you know, happy when they get 1% growth. I think Germany's a great country, I lived there for a time, and I think there's a heck of a lot of potential for that country, but they sure aren't achieving it right now, and it's not just because of oil.

President Trump signs an executive order expanding savings

C-SPAN(1.8M)·11:33·365 views·2026-04-30
Donald TrumpPresident of the United States
TL;DRPresident Trump signed a landmark executive order establishing the "Trump IRA" program, providing millions of low-income Americans access to low-cost retirement accounts with a federal matching contribution of up to $1,000 per year.
The "Trump IRA" Initiative: Beginning...

TL;DR

President Trump signed a landmark executive order establishing the "Trump IRA" program, providing millions of low-income Americans access to low-cost retirement accounts with a federal matching contribution of up to $1,000 per year.

Expansion of Retirement Savings (Trump IRA)

  • The "Trump IRA" Initiative: Beginning next year, all Americans can access low-cost IRA accounts via trumpira.gov, modeled after the Thrift Savings Plan (TSP) currently available to federal employees.
      • Federal Savers Match: Low-income Americans (specifically those earning below $35,000) are eligible for up to $1,000 in annual federal matching funds deposited directly into their accounts.
      • Long-term Projections: The administration estimates that a 25-year-old investing $165 per month with the federal match could accumulate approximately $465,000 by age 65.
  • Phase Two Goals: Economic advisor Kevin Hassett noted that future legislative efforts will aim to expand this match to middle-income earners who lack employer-sponsored 401(k) plans.
  • Current Participation: The President noted that related retirement initiatives have already seen 5 million sign-ups, significantly exceeding initial expectations.

Economic Indicators and Manufacturing

  • Market Growth: President Trump highlighted that the stock market has set 59 all-time record highs during his tenure, adding an estimated $9 trillion in value to the U.S. economy.
  • 401(k) Comparisons: The President contrasted his administration's performance with the Biden administration, claiming the average 401(k) rose by $24,000 in his last year compared to $875 under his successor.
  • Manufacturing Resurgence: The briefing emphasized the return of automotive plants from Canada, Mexico, Germany, Japan, and South Korea, as well as U.S. leadership in AI development.

Energy and Infrastructure

  • Bridger Pipeline Authorization: The President signed a presidential permit for the Bridger pipeline, a trans-border project described as similar to the Keystone XL.
      • Strategic Goal: The permit is intended to enhance North American energy dominance and security by streamlining the movement of oil and gas.
      • Efficiency: The President argued that pipelines are a safer and more efficient alternative to transporting fuel via truck or train.

Federal Contracting and Administrative Reform

  • Shift to Fixed-Price Contracts: An executive order was signed to move federal contracting away from "cost-plus" models to a "fixed-price" default.
      • Accountability: This change aims to eliminate incentives for cost overruns and align government spending with private-sector practices to reduce fraud and abuse.
  • Surgeon General Nomination: The President officially withdrew the nomination of Casey Means and formally nominated Dr. Nicole Saphier for the position of U.S. Surgeon General.

National Proclamations

  • National Fallen Firefighters Memorial Weekend: A proclamation honoring the sacrifice of firefighters who have died in the line of duty.
  • National Foster Care Month: A proclamation recognizing the efforts of foster and adoptive parents, a key priority area for the First Lady.
"For millions of Americans who lack employer-sponsored plans, this will be really revolutionary because they'll be covered. Nobody thought that was possible."

— President Donald Trump

"What this executive order does is it establishes a fixed-price contract as the default in most federal contracts. This aligns the federal government with what would typically exist in the private sector and we believe will continue to drive down fraud and abuse."

— Will (White House Staff)

"We're going to expand this program... to middle-income people... so that every American [has access]. Most high-income people have an employer that gives them a 401k with a match, but low-income people or Uber drivers... they don't have access to that."

— Kevin Hassett

These things that we do are very important. This afternoon I'm thrilled to sign a historic executive order expanding access to high-quality retirement savings accounts for millions of Americans. In my State of the Union earlier this year, I promised to make the same types of retirement accounts enjoyed by the federal employees available to all Americans. So that's what we're doing. It only seemed fair. It's a tremendous—it's really a tremendous situation for all Americans. That was really much like "promises made," as I say, "promises kept." I'm thrilled to be joined by incredible people that I know and are warriors. Jason Smith—these are Congress people—Brian Steil, Mike Carey, Lloyd Smucker, Bill Huizenga, Ryan Mackenzie, John McGuire, Zach Nunn, Derrick Van Orden, Jeff Crank, Nick Begich, Monica De La Cruz, and Mike Waltz. Warriors, every one of them there. Great people. They fought very hard for what we're about to sign. And since my election, the stock market has set 59—this is in a little bit more than a year—59 all-time record highs, adding $9 trillion in value to really the value of our country, if you think about it. Under four years of Biden, the average 401k rose at a rate of just $875 a year. Last year under Trump, the average 401k balance was up $24,000. So $875 versus $24,000, I would say that's pretty good. This is really incredible; the numbers are incredible. The numbers being invested in our country are more incredible than anything. You see it; there are plants being built all over, all over the USA. We have auto plants, not just AI. AI is doing great; we're leading China by quite a bit. But we have auto plants being built all over the country. They're coming back from Canada; they're coming back from Mexico, Germany, Japan, South Korea. Wherever they build autos, they're coming here. Nobody ever thought they'd see that. The union loves me and I did very well with the autoworkers, but they like me a lot more now than they did when I was just talking about it. Beginning at the start of next year, every American will be able to go to trumpira.gov and open a new low-cost IRA account. You'll then be able to access the same type of retirement accounts that federal employees enjoy through the Thrift Savings Plans, which are incredible. As part of the federal savers match program, low-income Americans will be eligible to receive up to $1,000 per year in matching funds deposited directly into their accounts. Great thing. For millions of Americans who lack employer-sponsored plans, this will be really revolutionary because they'll be covered. Nobody thought that was possible. For example, if a 25-year-old who is eligible for the savers match program invests just $165 a month, under the matching federal contributions, they will have an estimated $465,000 in their account by the time they're 65 years old. In other words, they'll be rich. And there's something awfully nice about that. But this is only the first step. I was talking to Kevin Hassett a little while ago and the people standing right behind me; we're going to take it to the next level. And the next level—Kevin, you're going to mention something—but the next level will be necessary to have congressional approval. What you just heard, we don't need congressional approval for. And it'll never be terminated because to terminate it, if somebody else is in office, you'd have to take that money away. You'd have to really take away something that's so positive. I don't think any politician would do it. But to take it to the next level, we need congressional approval, which should be very easy to get. It should be bipartisan. Normally it would be bipartisan, but today nothing's bipartisan, right? I mean it should be something that everybody supports. So Kevin, before we start, maybe you could just explain what we were talking about for the next level. Right, so for the next level, sir, what Chairman Smith and I are already talking about—where I've been talking with Mr. Smucker about this for a number of years—is that we're going to expand this program that you're creating here. This is for low-income people, but we want to expand it to middle-income people and maybe above as well so that every American has access. You know, most high-income people have an employer that gives them a 401k with a match, but low-income people or Uber drivers or something don't have access to that. They don't have access to the match. What you've done here is you've given the match to low-income people with incomes below 35,000. But we think that there are a lot of people even who make more than that that don't have many assets for retirement, and so we're working with Congress to significantly expand this program. We are looking forward for legislation this year to expand this. And also the Trump accounts—you remember with Michael Dell, Susan, and the group that we had—are setting records. The numbers— We have 5 million signups right now, sir. 5 million signups in a short period of time. It is far above what we thought even possible. So congratulations to everybody that got that started. Michael Dell put in $6,250,000,000; that's a lot. He put in a lot of money and he's going to do more too. But they were very responsible. So I just want to let you know it's like a record. Nobody—you are even surprised by that number, I think, right? Right. I said I don't know if we can do that kind of number, but it's really way beyond what anyone thought. It's great and we're going to be doing the same thing, I guess, in a certain way for older people. We're going with a plan that, if it works, has to get approved by Congress, but we think it'll get approved. It'll be fantastic for the country. Let's go, Will, do you want to start? There are some other things and we thought we'd let these great people from Congress and beyond—or below, I guess I could say that, right? So first, sir, this is the IRA executive order that you just mentioned. This establishes the retirement accounts with the federal match for eligible people, as you said in your speech and as Kevin explained. This is a big step forward. It's going to provide access to high-quality retirement accounts to a lot of Americans who don't currently have access to such accounts. Okay. There it is, just nice. Great thing, thank you very much everybody. We'll do some that aren't associated with this and then we'll talk about this with the press. We'll ask some questions of the Congress people, okay? Because they were really responsible for this. Alright? Yes. Who would like to hold it? Okay, good. Popular guy. Okay, let's go. This is a presidential permit authorizing the Bridger pipeline, sir. This is a trans-border pipeline similar to the old Keystone XL pipeline. It'll significantly expand our ability to move oil and gas around North America. It's a huge deal in terms of long-term energy dominance and energy security. This is slightly different than the last administration. They wouldn't sign a pipeline deal and we have pipelines going up. And by the way, they're way underground, they're not a problem, nobody even knows they're there; it's so crazy. But they wouldn't approve anything having to do with a pipeline and they're great as opposed to trucking it or training it. They were—they're great. A lot of jobs too, a lot of jobs. Okay, very good. Next. This is an executive order on federal contracting, sir. Many federal contracts currently operate on a cost-plus basis. This provides oftentimes weird incentives for contractors, leads to cost overruns, and costs the federal government a lot of money. What this executive order does is it establishes a fixed-price contract as the default in most federal contracts. This aligns the federal government with what would typically exist in the private sector and we believe will continue to drive down fraud and abuse in federal contracting government-wide. Would anybody like to object to this? Certainly not. You fought for this one too. Okay, thank you. Next. You previously announced that we were withdrawing Casey Means as the Surgeon General, sir, and appointing Dr. Sapphire—or nominating Dr. Sapphire in her place. That on the left is the withdrawal, which will later be transmitted to the Senate, and on the right is the new nomination form for our new Surgeon General, Dr. Sapphire. She's with Fox—was with Fox—is she with Fox anymore, Peter? But she's great. She'll be the Surgeon General. I believe she's at Memorial Sloan Kettering in New Jersey, sir, and she's an outstanding doctor. She is. I just don't know how Biden used the autopen. There's just so many things to sign. It's here; please take this. It's crazy. Oh, all four years was crazy. Okay, let's not bring it up. She's going to be great. Surgeon General. Thank you. This is a proclamation declaring this weekend to be National Fallen Firefighters Memorial Weekend. As you know, firefighters do an incredibly difficult, dangerous job and help keep us all safe. This is a proclamation honoring their sacrifice and remembers those who have fallen. Right. Everybody likes that. You bet. Okay, firefighters. That one deserves... Okay. Lastly, sir, this is another proclamation. Your administration, particularly the First Lady, have made foster and adopted care major priorities. This is a proclamation declaring National Foster Care Month, honoring those who have participated in foster programs, who have helped raise foster children, and speaking about the many things that your administration, including the First Lady's office, have done on that set of issues. Yeah, it's been a big thing for the First Lady, so this is in honor of our First Lady. Well thanks. I spent time in foster care. Oh, that's great.

Full Trump press conference discusses Iran war, World Cup, attempted shootings and more

USA TODAY(7.5M)·59:28·3.5K views·2026-04-30
Donald TrumpPresident of the United States
TL;DRPresident Trump signed a historic executive order establishing "Trump IRAs" to expand retirement savings access with federal matching for low-income Americans, while simultaneously announcing an overhaul of federal contracting and providing updates…
on the "military...

TL;DR

President Trump signed a historic executive order establishing "Trump IRAs" to expand retirement savings access with federal matching for low-income Americans, while simultaneously announcing an overhaul of federal contracting and providing updates on the "military decimation" of Iran’s nuclear and defense capabilities.

Retirement Savings and Financial Initiatives

  • TrumpIRA.gov Launch: Starting next year, Americans can open low-cost retirement accounts modeled after the federal Thrift Savings Plan.
      • Federal Saver's Match: Low-income earners (under $35,000) are eligible for up to $1,000 per year in direct federal matching funds.
      • Long-term Wealth: Trump estimated that a 25-year-old investing $165/month with the match could accumulate $465,000 by age 65.
  • Phase Two Expansion: Economic Advisor Kevin Hassett and Chairman Jason Smith are working on legislation to expand these matches to middle-income Americans and "Uber drivers" who lack employer-sponsored plans.
  • Trump Accounts Success: Trump reported 5 million signups for related financial products, noting that Michael Dell contributed $6.25 billion to support the initiative.

Economic Policy and Federal Efficiency

  • Federal Contracting Reform: An executive order established "fixed-price" contracts as the default for the federal government, replacing "cost-plus" models to drive down fraud, abuse, and cost overruns.
  • Energy Dominance: Trump signed a presidential permit for the Bridger Pipeline, a transborder oil and gas pipeline described as a successor to the Keystone XL project.
  • Economic Indicators: The President highlighted 2% GDP growth, a $9 trillion increase in stock market value since his election, and IRS data showing average tax returns are up 11% due to the "Big Beautiful Tax Bill."
  • Tax Policy: Reiteration of "no tax on tips," "no tax on overtime," and "no tax on Social Security" for seniors.

Foreign Policy and Military Operations

  • Iran Conflict: Trump described a highly successful 45-minute military operation involving B-2 bombers from Missouri that "obliterated" Iran’s nuclear capacity.
      • Military Impact: He claimed Iran’s navy is "underwater," drone factories are down 82%, and missile factories are down 90%.
      • Diplomatic Stance: Despite the military action, Trump stated Iran is "dying to make a deal" because their economy is crashing under a total blockade.
  • Venezuela Partnership: Described a "joint venture" style relationship where Venezuela is producing more oil than it has in decades, benefiting both nations.
  • NATO Relations: Trump threatened troop withdrawals from Germany, Spain, and Italy, criticizing them for failing to assist in the Middle East and "making a mess" of Ukraine.

Personnel, Security, and Political Strategy

  • Surgeon General Shift: Formally withdrew Casey Means and nominated Dr. Nicole Saphier (formerly of Fox News and Memorial Sloan Kettering) as the new Surgeon General.
  • Assassination Attempt and Security:
      • Trump praised the Secret Service's performance during a recent attempt on his life but noted he is considering wearing a bulletproof vest, despite concerns about looking "20 pounds heavier."
      • He criticized Democrats for wanting to "defund" law enforcement, including ICE and the Secret Service.
  • Legislative Strategy: Trump called on Senate Republicans to "blow up the filibuster" immediately to pass the Save America Act and voter ID laws, warning that Democrats will do so the moment they regain power to add 21 Supreme Court justices and grant statehood to D.C. and Puerto Rico.

Notable Quotes

"Beginning at the start of next year, every American will be able to go to TrumpIRA.gov and open a new low-cost IRA account... In other words, they’ll be rich. And there’s something awfully nice about that." — President Donald Trump
"We have already been talking... about expanding this program... to middle-income people and maybe above as well, so that every American... has access to the match." — Kevin Hassett
"If we terminated the filibuster, we would have a 50-year run. You’d stop cheating... they’re professional cheats and they’re great at it. That’s what they’re best at because you can’t get elected with their policy." — President Donald Trump
"Iran killed 42,000 people over the last two months, protesters... they are in very bad shape. They’ve got nothing going, including leadership. Their first-string leaders are gone. Their second-string leaders are gone. Now we’re dealing with the third string." — President Donald Trump
Well, thank you very much, everybody. We very much appreciate it. This is a big afternoon. They all are in a way, because all of these things that we do are very important. This afternoon, I'm thrilled to sign a historic executive order expanding access to high-quality retirement savings accounts for millions of Americans. In my State of the Union earlier this year, I promised to make the same types of retirement accounts enjoyed by federal employees available to all Americans, so that's what we're doing. It only seems fair. It's a tremendous... it's really a tremendous situation for all Americans. That was really a case of promises made, as I say, promises kept. I'm thrilled to be joined by incredible people that I know and the warriors: Jason Smith—these are Congress people—Brian Steil, Mike Carey, Lloyd Smucker, Bill Huizenga, Ryan Mackenzie, John McGuire, Zach Nunn, Derrick Van Orden, Jeff Crank, Nick Begich, Monica De La Cruz, and Mike Lawler. Warriors, every one of them. They're great people. They fought very hard for what we're about to sign. And since my election, the stock market has set 59—this is in a little bit more than a year—59 all-time record highs, adding $9 trillion in value to really the value of our country, if you think about it. Under four years of Biden, the average 401k rose at a rate of just $875 a year. Last year, under Trump, the average 401k balance was up $24,000. So $875 versus $24,000, I would say that's pretty good. This is really incredible; the numbers are incredible. The numbers being invested in our country are more incredible than anything. You see it. There are plants being built all over, all over the USA. We have auto plants, not just AI. AI is doing great; we're leading China by quite a bit. But we have auto plants being built all over the country. They're coming back from Canada, they're coming back from Mexico, Germany, Japan, and South Korea. Wherever they build autos, they're coming here. Nobody ever thought they'd see that. The union loves me, and I did very well with the autoworkers, but they like me a lot more now than they did when I was just talking about it. Beginning at the start of next year, every American will be able to go to TrumpIRA.gov and open a new low-cost IRA account. You'll then be able to access the same type of retirement accounts that federal employees enjoy through the Thrift Savings Plans, which are incredible. As part of the Federal Saver's Match program, low-income Americans will be eligible to receive up to $1,000 per year in matching funds deposited directly into their accounts. It's a great thing. For millions of Americans who lack employer-sponsored plans, this will be really revolutionary because they'll be covered. Nobody thought that was possible. For example, if a 25-year-old who is eligible for the Saver's Match program invests just $165 a month, under the matching federal contributions, they will have an estimated $465,000 in their account by the time they're 65 years old. In other words, they'll be rich. And there's something awfully nice about that. But this is only the first step. I was talking to Kevin Hassett a little while ago and the people standing right behind me; we're going to take it to the next level. And the next level, Kevin, you're going to mention something, but the next level will be necessary to have congressional approval. What you just heard, we don't need congressional approval for. And it'll never be terminated because to terminate it, if somebody else is in office, you'd have to take that money away. You'd have to... you'd have to really take away something that's so positive. I don't think any politician would do it. But to take it to the next level, we need congressional approval, which should be very easy to get. It should be bipartisan. Normally, it would be bipartisan, but today nothing's bipartisan. I mean, it should be something that everybody supports. So Kevin, before we start, maybe you could just explain what we were talking about for the next level. Right. So for the next level, sir, what Chairman Smith and I are already talking about... I've been talking with Mr. Smucker about this for a number of years, is that we're going to expand this program that you're creating here, which is for low-income people, to middle-income people and maybe above as well, so that every American... You know, most high-income people have an employer that gives them a 401k with a match, but low-income people or Uber drivers or something, they don't have access to that; they don't have access to the match. What you've done here is you've given the match to low-income people with incomes below 35,000. But we think that there are a lot of people even who make more than that that don't have many assets for retirement. And so we're working with Congress to significantly expand this program and looking forward for legislation this year to expand this. And also the Trump accounts, you remember with Michael Dell, Susan, and the group that we had, are setting records. We have 5 million signups right now, sir. Five million signups in a short period of time, far above what we thought even possible. So congratulations to everybody that got that started. Michael Dell put in $6,250,000,000. That's a lot. He put in a lot of money and he's going to do more too. But they were very responsible. So I just want to let you know, it's like a record. Nobody... you are even surprised by that number, I think, right? Right. Yeah, that's fantastic. I don't know if we can do that kind, but it's really way beyond what anyone thought. It's great. And we're going to be doing the same thing, I guess, in a certain way for older people. We're going with a plan that, if it works, has to get approved by Congress, but we think it'll get approved. It'll be fantastic for the country. Let's go. Will, do you want to start? There are some other things, and we thought we'd let these great people from Congress and beyond... or below, I guess I could say that, right? So first, sir, this is the IRA executive order that you just mentioned. This establishes the retirement accounts with the federal match for eligible people, as you said in your speech and as Kevin explained. This is a big step forward. It's going to provide access to high-quality retirement accounts to a lot of Americans who don't currently have access to such accounts. Okay, there it is. Just nice. Great. Thank you very much, everybody. And next, we'll do some that aren't associated with this, and then we'll talk about this with the press. They'll ask some questions of the Congress people, okay? Because they were really responsible for this. All right. Who would like to hold that? Okay, good. Popular guy. Okay, let's go. This is a presidential permit authorizing the Bridger Pipeline, sir. This is a transborder pipeline similar to the old Keystone XL pipeline. It'll significantly expand our ability to move oil and gas around North America. It's a huge deal in terms of long-term energy dominance and energy security. Slightly different than the last administration. They wouldn't sign a pipeline deal and we have pipelines going up. And by the way, they're way underground. They're not a problem; nobody even knows they're there. It's so crazy, but they wouldn't approve anything having to do with a pipeline, and they're great. As opposed to trucking it or training it, they're great. A lot of jobs too, a lot of jobs. Okay, very good. Next. This is an executive order on federal contracting, sir. Many federal contracts currently operate on a cost-plus basis. This provides oftentimes weird incentives for contractors, leads to cost overruns, and costs the federal government a lot of money. What this executive order does is it establishes a fixed-price contract as the default in most federal contracts. This aligns the federal government with what would typically exist in the private sector, and we believe will continue to drive down fraud and abuse in federal contracting government-wide. Would anybody like to object to this? Certainly not. We fight for this one too. Great. Thank you. Next. You previously announced that we were withdrawing Casey Means as the Surgeon General, sir, and nominating Dr. Saphier in her place. That on the left is the withdrawal which will later be transmitted to the Senate, and on the right is the new nomination form for our new Surgeon General, Nicole Saphier. Nicole. Yeah. She was with Fox. Is she with Fox anymore, Peter? But she's great. She'll be the Surgeon General. Well said, sir. I believe she's from Memorial Sloan Kettering in New Jersey, sir, and she's an outstanding doctor. She is. She's going to be great as Surgeon General. Thank you. This is a proclamation declaring this weekend to be National Fallen Firefighters Memorial Weekend. As you know, firefighters do an incredibly difficult, dangerous job and help keep us all safe. This is a proclamation honoring their sacrifice and remembering those who have fallen. Great, all right. Everybody likes that. You bet. Okay. Firefighters. That one deserves it. Okay. Lastly, sir, this is another proclamation. Your administration, particularly the First Lady, have made foster and adoptive care major priorities. This is a proclamation declaring National Foster Care Month, honoring those who have participated in foster programs, who have helped raise foster children, and speaking about the many things that your administration, including the First Lady's office, have done on that set of issues. Yeah, it's been a big thing for the First Lady, so this is in honor of our First Lady. Well said. I spent time... I spent time in foster care. Oh, that's great. That's great. That's not as good. Thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you, Mr. President. So do you have any questions? Maybe we discuss the first signing first. You have anything on that? Mr. President, how is this going to help small business owners? Do you think this is going to inspire more people to go into small businesses that might be reluctant earlier for an IRA, to have their IRA? Is this going to help give them... Well, I'll tell you, small businesses now are thriving and as you know, more people are working right now than at any time in the history of our country. We're doing great. And there's more investment being made in the United States than any country at any time, at any time in history. A lot of that is AI and a lot of that is, maybe in a certain way, more importantly, auto plants are all coming back to our country. They're coming back from different countries where they left us for Germany, as you know, Japan, South Korea, Canada, Mexico. And they're all coming back at levels that nobody's ever seen before. And tariffs did it. I did it. Maybe the election did it, but tariffs did it. And if they build here, they don't have to pay tariffs. And we have Germany... a lot of companies are coming in from Germany, from Japan. Japan just announced $10 billion more in plants going to be built between Toyota and Honda and all the different companies. They're pouring into our country, and South Korea, Canada... they're coming in. I don't know what they're doing, but I can tell you they're coming in tremendously from Canada. There's no reason; they don't want to pay the tariff. And because of that, they're investing at levels never seen before. For 30 years we virtually didn't build an auto plant in this country. Now we're doing more than we've ever done. So I'm very proud of that. AI is going wild, we're doing great. And we're letting them build their own electric plant; we're actually making them build their own electric plant so they don't take... it takes tremendous amounts of energy to have a successful plant. All of the new ones are building their own electric plant, like a utility, and any extra they'll either give it or sell it for a good price to local communities and to the grid. So it's going to be fantastic. But we have tremendous amounts of factories being built in our country now, more than we've ever had at any time ever before. And we have the biggest investment being made in our country in the history of our country—in the history of any country. There's never been a country that has the kind of investment coming in that we have right now. And these things are being built right now, so you're not going to see the results... I mean, you'll see construction results. You notice the construction workers are through the roof. But when they open, the numbers are going to be staggering. Do you have a question? I certainly do. Thank you, Mr. President. You had some good economic news: GDP 2% growth, new orders for business equipment surged to the highest level in six years, and IRS data shows that the average federal tax return is up by more than 11% when you compare it to the same time as last year. Do you believe that this is evidence that the big beautiful tax cuts that you signed into law last year are working? And do you think the economy is going to continue to hum as the year progresses? Yeah, thank you. So the biggest tax cut in history is in the great big beautiful bill. And I think, you know, I see it. I actually see it reported where people are shocked at how much money they're making. They can't believe the refunds that they're making. $5,000, $6,000, $7,000, $8,000, $9,000. I saw one last night on television: $11,000 more than she thought she was going to get. And it's no tax on tips and no tax on overtime, no tax on social security for our seniors... nobody can believe it even, and the numbers are far greater than they thought. People that thought they were going to make an extra $500, it turns out they're making an extra $5,000. So it's been really great, yeah. And that's going to carry forward. Look, the country's doing really well. And that's despite a military operation. I don't call it a war, because you'd rather have a military operation. We're really... I mean, Iran is dying to make a deal. I can only tell you that. I don't want to get into the details, but they cannot be nuclear, other than that. But their navy's gone, their air force is gone. Every ounce of any form of equipment practically is gone. You know, their drone factories are about 82% down and their missile factories are almost 90% down. And many of their missiles have been knocked out. They used some, but we knocked out more than they used. And it's pretty amazing what's happened. They want to make a deal.
