But despite the fact that we are in what some people would call a war, we just hit a new high today on the stock market. We have S&P through the roof. They're all through the roof. And you know, when we hit 50,000 on the Dow and 7,000 on the S&P, I said to myself, we got to do something about Iran. They want to have a nuclear weapon. You can't let them have a nuclear weapon. And I hated to do it to my people because they said 50,000... Peter, you know this, that 50,000 wouldn't be obtainable in my four years or anybody's four years. And we hit 50,000 my first year, the end of my first year. We hit 7,000 on the S&P; they said that would be even less obtainable and we hit that at the end of the first year before the year was up. And then I said to Kevin, "Sorry about this Kevin, but we have to put out a fire." And the fire is taking place in the lovely country of Iran. And they want to have a nuclear weapon. And if we didn't use this beautiful... see that plane right there, that beautiful B-2 bomber? It looks small there, but it's actually quite large. It doesn't look very powerful there, but it's actually quite large. We ordered 25 more of them, the updated version, all built in America, which is very nice, right? But if we didn't have that B-2 bomber, if we didn't go in and do what we did, we obliterated that nuclear capacity of theirs. And so they never... they didn't get their nuclear bomb. If we didn't do that attack... it should have been done long before I came along by other presidents or by another country or something. But it should have been done long before I came along. But they are in very bad shape. They've got nothing going, including leadership. I mean, you know, their leaders are gone. Now they have a whole new set... their first string leaders are gone. Their second string leaders are gone. Now we're dealing with the third string. But we'll see what happens. It's been an amazing period of time between Venezuela, where we're doing great. We're working very closely with them. They're doing more oil now than they've done in many, many decades actually. And they're making more money and so are we. You know, it's like a joint venture to be honest. And we've paid for the attack many times over. The attack—something you've never heard before probably. I always used to say to the victor belong the spoils, but we've paid for the attack, which is always expensive, but it was less than a one-day attack. It was actually a 45-minute attack. And that's a good... you know, they have a good military in Venezuela. But they did not expect that. Other than that, when these suckers come at you at 2,500 miles an hour at 25 feet off the ground, you say, "This is not what I bargained for. This is just no good." Anyway, Venezuela was amazing, but now we're doing essentially the same thing, though it's larger. We can't let Iran have a nuclear weapon. Their economy is crashing; the blockade is incredible. The power to blockade is incredible. They're not getting any money from oil. And hopefully it can be worked out very soon. Yeah. Mr. President, Iran is proving to be very stubborn. The talks seem like they are stalled. Are you getting antsy to break the ceasefire? Well, I mean, I don't know what stubborn is because really nobody knows what the talks are except myself and a couple of other people. They want to make a deal badly. And we have a problem because nobody knows for sure who the leaders are. It's a little bit of a problem. You know, the leaders have been wiped out along with their military—almost all of their military. I mean, think of it: 159 ships was their total navy; 159 ships are gone. They're underwater; they're way below sea level. Sea level is a big difference; it's pretty deep waters over there too. So I don't know that we need it; we might need it, we might not need it. And on Iran, a little while ago, the President of FIFA said that when the World Cup is here in the US, the team from Iran will be playing in the games. Well, if Gianni said it, I'm okay. Are you joking? Did Gianni say it? He did. Gianni Infantino, that's a piece of work. What if they win? Well, if they win, we'll have to worry about that. That's not... I'm going to have to worry about that one. Look, you know what, let them play well. Gianni's fantastic, you know, he's a friend of mine. And he talked about it and said, "You do whatever you want; you can have them, you don't have to have them." They probably have a good team, I don't know. Do they have a good team? Do you have any idea? I have no idea. It would be hard to believe, actually. But I think let them play, right? Mr. President, something a little bit different, but what is your take on these reports that your show, The Apprentice, is going to come back... could come back on Amazon with host Donald Trump Jr.? Well, I've been hearing it. Look, we had a great success—14 seasons—and The Apprentice was a tremendous success. So I've been hearing that a little bit. So we'll see what happens. He's a good guy. He'd probably be good; he's got a little charisma going. You need a little charisma for that sucker. So we'll see what happens. Yeah, they told me about it. We'll see. Mr. President, thank you. There's been some talk about changes to your security after what took place... Changing my security. Well, they did a pretty good job actually. You know, they stopped the NFL running back. I mean, he was like a running back. In fact, if he ever got out, they're probably going to sign him. He was a speedy guy. But he was stopped. They had many levels in front of him before he would even come to us. You know, you could be a baseball team and win 19 to nothing and somebody could say you should have won 21, or you should have gotten 25 runs, or the team was no good or something. So I can only say that I know a lot of those people at Secret Service and they were good and they were forceful, and I saw the guys in front of me; they were powerful-looking guys. I said we could send them to Hollywood; they could make movies. I think that there's always room for improvement, right? And he got into the building. The problem is the... I know that's what I do, I build buildings like that, much nicer actually, but I build buildings like that. And you know, when you have 1,000 rooms sitting on top of a ballroom and you have the elevators coming down right near the entrance, it's a pretty tough situation. Now, right outside, we have something that's on time, on budget—actually ahead of time and ahead of budget depending on finishes. You know, finishes are a big difference between marble and onyx in price. But it's right on budget, right on time. And that's built to the highest standards of security. No units on top, no hotel built on top where an elevator comes right down through the middle of it. It's a tough location. So I think... I know the people at Secret Service, I think they're great. And I think that Congress has to take care of those people. You know, they're what we're talking about: ICE, border patrol, Secret Service, everyone is... the Democrats essentially want to defund the police, just so you know that's right. They want to defund law enforcement. It's crazy. These people have gone crazy. I heard Hakeem Jeffries—a low-IQ person, he's a very low-IQ—screaming today that the Supreme Court is illegitimate. I mean, that's a dangerous statement. Then if I say something modest, they say, "Oh, he's a king, he's a king." They're sick. But Hakeem Jeffries said the Supreme Court is illegitimate. That's a rough statement. If I made that statement, it would be the biggest story out there; bad things would happen. Yeah. One of the ideas... the average price of a gallon of gas is now $4.30 in this country. And you know what? We're not going to have a nuclear weapon in the hands of Iran. The gas will go down as soon as the war's over; it'll drop like a rock. There's so much oil; it's all over the place, sitting all over the oceans of the world. And it'll go down. What won't happen is Iran having a nuclear weapon and using it; then the whole world is a different place. You're not going to have to pay a little bit more for gasoline. The oil will go down rapidly as soon as the war is over. It's good to see you, sir. I've got two questions. The first one, following up on Daniel's question: I've described you as the peacemaker. You're achieving peace around the world. Due to these latest policies—big beautiful bill, no tax on tips, no tax on social security, no tax on overtime, now the Trump account and then the IRA account—could we describe you as the wealth maker? Well, I'm helping people out. It's a nice question, actually, but I'm helping people out. These people are with me; these are great people. And feel free to speak up if you'd like, because they wanted this to happen and we got it done. And they're Republican people, I think in all cases, right? It's crazy. We need it to be unanimous because the Democrats will vote against anything no matter how good. If they had their favorite thing in the world and we happened to want it... But we found a new strategy. We're going to oppose every single thing that we want, and we'll get it unanimous. So I'm going to come up and oppose everything that we want, Brian, from this point forward, and they'll vote for it. Good plan. Second question: you created Space Force towards the end of your first administration. You just had the Artemis 2 crew in here. I know it's a big accomplishment. What do you see moving forward in term of space exploration? Well, doing Space Force, I think, is going to go down as one of my more important things. It's turning out, even with these military operations we have right now—when you look at Venezuela or the decimation of Iran—that a lot of that took place so effectively because of what's coming out of space. When I did Space Force, I'm very proud of it. Space was like we were third. China was first, Russia was second. Now we've leapfrogged; we're way ahead of China, we're way ahead of Russia. And we made some great choices. The first person that headed Space Force and the couple of really great people after that were always very important. And a new person is heading it now. I think he could have a chance to be as good as anybody, as good as maybe better, but we've had great people heading up Space Force. It's been very important for what we're doing with respect to the different places. And as far as peace, I settled eight wars. And people try and dispute it, but then they go off for them and almost in every case they sent letters thanking me and letters to the Nobel Committee. I don't care about that. Maria was very nice; she gave me her Nobel Peace Prize because she said she didn't deserve it. She was very nice, but I settled eight wars. And in every case, the prime ministers or presidents wrote letters thanking me. In the case of India... look, the biggest one would have been Pakistan-India. The Prime Minister of Pakistan said I saved from 30 to 50 million lives, but it could have been more than that. Two nuclear nations were going at it. Eleven airplanes were shot down. They were in the first week of what would have been a very bad war, and I got it solved. You know how I got it solved? By the use of tariffs. I said I'm going to charge you tariffs if you guys keep fighting, and they said, "Oh, please don't do that." You just lifted tariffs on whiskey, is that correct? Yeah. What I'm doing is... for as long as I've been here, they've wanted to... you know, Kentucky is a great place where I also won, Jason, by a lot. Kentucky with the bourbon and Scotland with the whiskey have had trade for years and a lot of restrictions. It really stopped them. I don't know if you know, it's a barrel trade. They use the barrels from the one to take care of the other, and the barrels are only good for one unit. They can't be used twice, but the once makes it a better substance. I'm not a big drinker, so forgive me, but it makes them a better substance and a better taste. They dealt for years—Scotland and Kentucky. And I just took all the restrictions off, so Scotland and Kentucky can start dealing again. I did it in honor of the King and Queen who just left. They're heading back and it was a big deal. They've been trying to get this for a long time. Mr. President, you talked about possibly pulling out some troops of Germany. Would you be considering the same thing for Spain and Italy? Yeah, probably. Right, probably will. Why should I? Look, why shouldn't I? Italy has not been of any help to us and Spain has been horrible, absolutely horrible. You know, it's NATO. It's not even the fact that they're bad; it's one thing if they said nicely, "Okay, we'll help," but the help's a little slow. But the level... and we help them with Ukraine. You know, they made a total mess out of Ukraine. And we helped them. Ukraine has nothing to do... we're an ocean apart. It has to do with them; it's like their front door. We helped them and Biden gave them $350 billion, which was insane. It's one of the reasons the war went on. But when we needed them, they were not there. We have to remember that. And so if we ever have a big one... because we didn't need any help with Iran. We had Iran right from the first day. It was over. And now it's even more so; we're so locked and loaded. But we didn't need the help. To a certain extent, I asked them—I didn't need the help—but I said, "Yeah, we'd love to have you help," because I wanted to see if they'd do it. In all cases, they said, "We don't want to get involved." And you know the amazing thing is, they use the Strait of Hormuz, we don't. We don't use it; we don't need it. We have a lot of oil. And they use it, and you would have thought they would have said, "We would love to help you." But they didn't. And Germany, I mean, he's doing a terrible job. He's got immigration problems, energy problems, he's got problems of all kind and he's got a big problem with Ukraine because they're in that mess. He criticized me for doing the whole thing with Iran. But I said, "Would you like to have a nuclear weapon in the hands of Iran?" He said, "No, I don't." I said, "Well then I guess I'm right." He didn't have any answer to that. The bottom line is for this world, you cannot let Iran have a nuclear weapon. And if you agree that Iran can't have a nuclear weapon, which most people agree, it should have been done a long time ago. But if you agree they can't have a nuclear weapon, then what I did has been executed perfectly because our military has decimated them. Mr. President, two questions for you. You have called this the "new regime" before in Iran, but I speak to Iranians on a daily basis and they tell me that these are the same people that have murdered them and repressed them. And second of all, how come Secretary of State Marco Rubio is not involved in these conversations? Oh, he's very much involved. Marco is totally... he's negotiating with them all the time. No, look, Iran killed 42,000 people over the last two months—protesters. They were going to kill eight women whose lives I saved, and I appreciate that they didn't do it. I asked them not to do it, and they didn't. They were getting ready to hang the women—eight beautiful women that just caught the imagination of a lot of people. It was inconceivable. They were going to be executed that evening, this was a week ago. And I got on the phone very quickly and I said, "Don't do it. The whole world is watching, don't do it." And they didn't do it. I guess four were released and the other four are going to be released in a month. They were ready to be executed that night. So they take a tough... you know, they knocked out the wrestler, they killed the wrestler's teammates. He was a world champion wrestler. They killed him because he protested and he spoke. He spoke negatively about the regime and they executed him along with his friends. They killed 42,000 people. So when you say they say I do the same, I don't do the same. I'm trying to save that country in a certain way. Nobody's ever seen anything like it. Think of it: they killed 42,000 innocent, not weapon-carrying people for protesting. The number they say is a lot higher than that. They're a rough group. But we have decimated them and their economy is a disaster. So we'll see how long they hold out. We'll see. Saudi Arabia is pulling the plug on LIV Golf. Once that tour is gone, do you think the PGA Tour should welcome the defectors back with open arms? Well, I do. It's a great question. In fact, if I had time, I'd love to watch television today because the PGA Tour is playing at Doral, the Cadillac Championship. And I said, "Who's winning?" Jordan Spieth is in the lead. Oh, he's a great guy. Good putter, to put it mildly, right? He's a great guy. No, they're at my tournament, the PGA. In two weeks, LIV is going to be at my course right here on the Potomac. Beautiful course. No, I'd love to see LIV, but I do believe that all of the great golfers should be playing against each other. I want to see Rory playing Bryson DeChambeau, I want to see Big John Rahm playing Scotty, who's so great, right? Scotty Scheffler is great. And I want to see him play John Rahm and Bryson and all the different guys. They have great players on the LIV. It's almost like people want to see that. That's why the Masters was so good, because you saw everybody together. Patrick Reed played great—he's on LIV and he played great—and they all did. They're great players; I play with them. When I think I'm a good player and then I play with them, I don't think I'm that good, you know? But there's something nice about all of the players playing together. Now they'll all be accepted by the tour because they're great. You can't have the best player that they're boycotting. Now they may do something a little bit, but they'll all be back on tour and it'll be great. I don't know what's happening with LIV, but they are playing in my course in two weeks on the Potomac. Right now they're playing at Doral, which I own, and if we end this early I'll be able to watch for about three minutes before I get back onto the economy and every other subject, including Iran, which we're doing so well with. A question on clarification of the Trump IRAs versus a regular IRA. Does it come down to the difference of a federal match? And secondly, do you have any reaction to Federal Reserve Chair Jay Powell staying on the board of governors? No, I don't care if he stays on. I just want to make sure that Kevin became the head. It's very unusual that they stay on, you know. I predicted he would because, who's going to hire him frankly? So I predicted he would stay on. But you know, he's a negative force. The thing that angered me about him more than interest rates was the fact that he could allow a building to cost billions of dollars more than it was supposed to. I could have built that building or renovated and fixed that building for $25 million. They're going to spend maybe $4 billion and it's not even close to being open. You know, Kevin—who's going to be fantastic—I don't know if he's going to have an office. I'm going to have to put him right here. I'll cut the Oval in half; I'll give him half of the Oval. No, I'm very happy about Kevin. I think Kevin's a star. And the other Kevin, could you answer her question? Sure. The match is a super important part of it, and it's one reason why we're calling on our friends here to potentially expand it. Right now the match is capped at 35,000, but there's a whole academic literature that this match has a big effect on people's willingness to save. And so the differences really are the match. We're creating the TrumpIRA.gov website so it'll be really, really easy for people to have access to private companies that follow the TSP rules that are really low cost. Finally, you noticed that Michael Dell has given money, promised money for the Trump accounts. We've also made it clear that at the Trump IRA, charities can contribute to other people's accounts. Imagine this will boost the stock market and economy further. Did you want to mention phase two? We're doing phase one today because I can do that without Congress, but phase two is going to be something that would be unbelievable. We'll be going back to Congress to ask for this. Right. The basic headline is that if you look at the huge success of Australia in their retirement savings accounts, we're working with the Treasury Secretary and members of Congress to come up with something that's pretty close to the Australian system. Or better. No, they had a couple of little glitches that we'll get rid of. But there's absolutely no contemplation of harming Social Security in any way. This is meant to be an add-on to that. It'll be very exciting; it's going to be great. We're going to move pretty fast. There's all these missing scientists who had access to classified material. A bunch of them have turned up dead. Have you been briefed yet about whether or not these cases are connected? Yeah, I have. So far, they're individual cases. We have a lot of scientists. When you see ten, it's a lot, but it's a very small number compared to what... we have hundreds of thousands of scientists. Some of them that we looked at are very sad cases. In some cases some were sick, some left this earth self-inflicted, some had other things. So far, we're finding that there's not much of a connection. We'll let you know. We're going to be doing a full report on it. It's very serious, though. Mr. President, have you been informed if the Secret Service agent who was shot on Saturday, if that was from friendly fire or if it was from the suspect? Well, they say it was not friendly fire. That's what I heard. They said it wasn't friendly fire. It was actually reported. Do you read the reports? Because it was actually reported. I read through the DOJ memo, but I don't believe it said that the agent was struck by... I'm just saying they said it wasn't friendly fire. It wasn't us. Is there talk about you potentially wearing a bulletproof vest moving forward, given you have now been shot at so many times? I don't know if I can handle looking 20 pounds heavier. These guys, they look so... some of these guys are physical specimens. You don't want to be near them. No, if you want to gain 20 to 25 pounds you get a vest. And believe me, frankly the vest did an amazing job because it took a bullet close up. And he didn't even want to go to the hospital. We sent him just in case, but he didn't want to go. The vest totally protected him. Still a lot of power behind that shot though. That's like getting hit by Mike Tyson. But it's amazing. I've been asked about that. And I guess it's something you consider. In one way, you don't like to do it because you're giving in to a bad element, so I don't know. But I have been asked about it. How concerned are you about it happening again? I don't think about it. If I did, I wouldn't be doing a very good job here. I'd be thinking about nothing but that. I really don't think about it. If I did, I wouldn't be effective. Okay. Mr. President, real quick, you posted on Truth Social calling on Senate Republicans to blow up the filibuster. Why don't they do it? Why don't they do it? I have no idea. You know why? I guess because they have three or four that are hardline no to everything. And we have a very small majority. John Thune's a good man. I said to him, "John, just go take the vote, let's expose who the people are." But you know, if we got rid of the filibuster, we could vote on the Save America Act, voter ID... you know, I love this guy. He's great, even though I don't love his girlfriend too much, okay? I won't tell you who the girlfriend is, but I like him. He's great; it's great that he hasn't changed. But I say look, Brian, I think here's the problem. It's not even imaginable to me why they don't. And people are angry about it. Republicans are angry about it. The filibuster should be terminated. If the Democrats ever get a shot, they will use it in the first minute of the first hour and they will execute all of the bad things. Twenty-one justices—they really like 21. I heard 13, but they really want 21 Supreme Court justices. And they really want to have D.C. and Puerto Rico become states. That would mean an automatic four senators go into the Democrat column. And there's not even a chance that they don't do it and do it immediately. With that knowledge, why wouldn't you do it as a Republican? Unless you have bad motives almost. But if we did it, we could pass one bill after the other. We could pass laws and acts and things that we never even dreamt of passing. And you know what else? We wouldn't lose for 50 years. If we terminated the filibuster, we would have a 50-year run. You'd stop cheating. I mean look, they're professional cheats and they're great at it. That's what they're best at because you can't get elected with their policy. You can't get elected with men in women's sports, with transgender mutilation of our children, or with open borders for jails and mental institutions to be dropped into our country. Think of it: 11,888 murderers come into our country and nobody even talks to them about it. We got a lot of them out because of ICE, which they don't want to fund. They're defunding our police. When they defund ICE and border patrol and Secret Service, they're defunding our police just as much as an officer on the street corner. So why the Republican Senate doesn't just sit in there and just do it and take a negative vote and do it again and again like the Democrats do... they go again and again. I'm negotiating a deal with Iran and every three days they put in a thing that the war should stop. People ask me, "How the hell do you negotiate like that?" You're destroying them. Every day I read about how well they're doing militarily. They got nothing left; they're done. And yet I read in the New York Times and on that stupid CNN—which I only watch because you have to watch a little bit of the enemy—that they're winning. You have to be smart. And if you see CNN, you'd think they're winning the war. If you read the New York Times, it's actually seditious in my opinion. You read some of these columnists and... but it all starts from the top. It's a terrible thing. So we're negotiating with them and they read how well they're doing. They're sitting in a cave and all their leaders are dead. Missiles are all over the place. They have no navy, they have no air force, they have no nothing. We can fly right over the middle of Tehran without being shot at because they have no anti-aircraft; they have nothing. And they're reading that they're winning the war. They're trying to figure it out. They think we're crazy. But I'm supposed to be negotiating and they make you negotiate from weakness, not strength. But the difference is, I don't care. And everybody knows the facts. We are decimating that country. I got a call today from another country in the Middle East saying, "Sir, please don't hit them anymore, they're decimated." And yet the New York Times will tell you that they're winning. It's so sad. I hate to even say that because when I say that, people out watching will say, "Oh, maybe they are winning the war," just by the fact that I'm saying it. It's so ridiculous. We have the greatest military in the world and one thing that's come out of this and Venezuela... I rebuilt the military in my first term and did a good job. And also the first year of this term. We have the greatest military like no other. When we attacked Venezuela, their equipment... none of it worked. It was all good equipment but it was discombobulated. You know what that means? They pressed the buttons and nothing happened. They heard that weird buzzing sound from way up high. It's amazing how great our military is. It's like the B-2 bomber right there. Late at night, eight months ago, they went into Iranian territory and Iran was waiting for them. And they were firing at the wrong targets; they didn't see them. With no moon, dark at night, each plane had two bombs—14 bombs. Every one of those 14 bombs went right down an air chute. Think of it: they followed the beam right into the chute and every single one of those bombs—very powerful, 200,000-pound bombs—went right down an air chute. They got faster and then the first one was a perfect hit. He said "skedaddle" and that plane turned over on its side and went back to a place called Missouri. Do you know Jason? God's country. Well, it went back; it wanted to say hello to a very powerful Congressman from Missouri. So every one of those bombs hit, and yet before they landed CNN came out with a report that maybe the damage wasn't that great. The damage was far greater than we said; it was obliteration. When the Atomic Energy Commission went there one week later, they said "Trump was right, it was obliteration." And they still haven't been able to do anything with the site. That stuff is buried. And the reason I took such—I was angry about it was because it's disrespectful to those pilots and those incredible people, including the people that maintain the planes. Those planes had to fly for 37 hours without stopping. They got the tankers; they had three tanker lifts, meaning they had to get refueled up in the air. They never landed. They went all the way from Missouri to here and then back. Some went to Guam, which there are those that say that was a way of camouflaging the whole thing because they followed the ones from—they thought the ones from Guam were not coming, they were going to take it easy at Guam. That didn't work out too well. But that was amazing, and the New York Times and CNN, they said well maybe the B-2 attack wasn't so great. Even to this day I hear it because we want to get the nuclear dust that's way down there. You need excavators and everything to get it, but we want to get it; we'll take it one way or the other, we'll get it. They'll either give it to us or we'll take it. But I think it's very disrespectful to our incredible military when they say maybe they missed the target. Every single bomb hit the target. Then on top of that, we shot from submarines 30 unbelievable missiles that also hit the target, on top of that. It was complete obliteration and that was confirmed by Atomic Energy. But even the other day I saw on CNN, "well I don't know, they want the stuff so therefore the bombs didn't hit." Yeah we want the stuff, it's way buried. But we want to get it, we will get it, won't even be hard. But they have to respect our military; they have to. If it were a miss I tell you, you know, it would be fine, it would have done a lot of damage, but these bombs went down the air chutes. They'll never build air chutes again in Mount—you know, they built these air chutes to get a little air up there, but they'll have to find a different way because it didn't work. But our military is amazing. There's never been anything like what we have, the most powerful military on Earth, and it's too bad we have to use it, but we're doing really well, so we'll see how that all works out. Thank you very much, everybody. Thank you guys. How do you like the new floor? So we had all—what I do best is build. That's what I do best. I think I say to people, "am I a better builder or politician?" And most of them say politician, but that's okay. What I do best is I build. The ballroom will be just like that, so I went out and I have a special black granite. Granite is the most powerful stone there is. Marble is much weaker than granite. I like marble more because marble can be more beautiful, but it's a much weaker stone. When you look at gravesites and you look at a marble plaque a hundred years old, you'll see oftentimes you can almost not read it. When you look at a granite plaque a hundred years old, it looks like it just got put there yesterday. You know that's a test, but we have the finest granite anyone's ever seen. It's, it's called flawless granite; there's very little of it. And I replaced broken slate that was put here many years ago. I saved the ramp because it's in honor of FDR—FDR right up there, he had that built as a ramp. They said, "oh sir, we'll straighten it." I said, "no, you have to leave the ramp." It was built—that ramp was built for FDR with the wheelchair because, you know, it's a not pretty good slope, but a lot of people say, "why is it a ramp?" They say because of FDR, they say I get it. But we saved that. But we replaced the broken slate that was—as slate, by the way, is meant for a roof, it's not meant for a sidewalk and we replaced it with granite, the highest-grade granite. It's valued at—you know they value stones in terms of that—that's valued at one million plus. That means one million plus years. A marble will oftentimes be valued at 200 years, 300 years, just giving you a little lesson in stone. Somebody will say he went off on a tangent. There's something wrong, like I did with the pen. I gave you a little thing on the pen, that that pen is better than the one that costs 2,000 that they're handing to everyone. See this one, I can hand to these guys; that's worth three bucks, okay? Maybe five. So anyway, but anyway, I always feel guilty, but they said he went off on a tangent about the... If you don't find it interesting, leave during, but the granite... in fact if you can, let them leave through that door. It's just completed. We had it completed for King Charles. On time, on budget. I paid for it, so you know, it's very expensive, I want to tell you that. He loved it, he loved it. He'd seen some nice stone. The Trump Renaissance, this is a Trump Renaissance era. Well, we're fixing the White House. But it was sort of terrible; people would walk to the Oval Office over broken slate. Again, slate is used on roofs, it's not used where people walk on it, and it's been walked on for a long time in many, many broken pieces. So I said to do it right. Now the other thing I did, if you want to handle it... all of the columns, they had 200 years of paint on them and they looked terrible, and you know, they were chipped and broken and when you have a lot of paint... and I stripped the columns back down into the original stone and painted them, and you'll see it. But you may want to—not final touches, little touch up here with the paint, but a little hit by a couple of these heavy stones. You know they're very heavy, thick granite. But that granite is valued at over a million years. So in other words, it's a lifetime, one million plus, that's your max. Why don't you walk out there and take a look? I think you'd get... and the company that did it, if you look at the setting, each point is perfect. So take a Peter, take a look, tell your father if I did a good job. Thank you. Thank you, press. Thank you, press.

Stripe Sessions 2026 | Indexing the economy

Stripe(103.0K)·30:15·5.1K views·2026-04-30
Patrick CollisonCEO, Stripe John CollisonPresident, Stripe
TL;DRThe global economy is undergoing a structural "replatforming" around AI, characterized by a surge in high-revenue solopreneurship, "global-day-one" scaling for startups, and the emergence of autonomous agentic commerce supported by stablecoin infrastructure.
SaaS...

TL;DR

The global economy is undergoing a structural "replatforming" around AI, characterized by a surge in high-revenue solopreneurship, "global-day-one" scaling for startups, and the emergence of autonomous agentic commerce supported by stablecoin infrastructure.

Current Economic Indicators and Myths

  • SaaS Resilience: Despite a $1 trillion market value loss in the software sector in January 2026, Stripe data shows SaaS payment volumes remain higher than pre-sell-off levels, indicating fundamental revenue strength.
  • Rational Markets: Outsize valuations have shifted strictly toward the most profitable companies, correcting the "dot-com" style irrationality where unprofitable firms were highly rewarded.
  • Tariff Pass-Through: While trade volumes remain stable, the downward price trend in durable goods (typically driven by manufacturing productivity) has reversed as businesses pass cumulative tariff costs to consumers.
  • The "K-Shaped" Myth: Contrary to popular narratives of a wealthy-only recovery, Stripe data shows the spending gap between high-income and low-income consumers is actually shrinking.
  • Labor Market Cooling: Recent unemployment upticks are attributed more to interest rates and "pandemic hiring hangovers" than to AI displacement.

The Explosion of Economic Dynamism

  • Solopreneurship at Scale: There is a massive re-acceleration in US business formations, driven primarily by "non-employer firms." Nearly 5 million Americans now earn their living as solopreneurs generating over $100,000 in revenue.
  • Accelerated Growth: The Stripe Atlas program recently hit 100,000 incorporations. The "Class of 2026" startups are tracking to five times the revenue of the 2025 cohort at the same stage.
  • Global-by-Default Playbook: Modern startups no longer wait to expand. The median top-100 AI startup on Stripe earns most of its revenue internationally and sells into 55 countries within its first year.
  • Coasian Shift: Based on Ronald Coase’s theory of the firm, AI is lowering the "coordination costs" between companies. This leads to a future of fewer people per firm, higher output per firm, and more coordination via efficient external markets.

The Rise of Agentic Commerce

  • Evolution of Autonomy: Commerce is moving from "Level 1" (software filling out forms) to "Level 2" (reasoning assistants like Wayfair’s shopping bot) toward "Level 5" (fully autonomous agents).
  • Machine-to-Machine Payments: Autonomous agents are already executing micro-transactions. John Collison demonstrated an AI agent researching energy markets and autonomously purchasing four cents worth of proprietary data to complete its task.
  • Infrastructure Requirements: To move agentic commerce into the mainstream, three layers are required:
      • The Economic Layer: Agents must be able to pay and businesses must accept those payments.
      • Micropayment Viability: For tiny, frequent transactions, stablecoins are becoming the essential infrastructure due to near-zero transaction costs.
      • The Trust Layer: Systems to manage permissions and verify agentic identity.

Strategic Moats in an AI Economy

  • Complementary Assets: As intelligence becomes cheap and abundant, its "complements" become more valuable. Key high-value assets now include:
      • Energy and Hardware: Massive demand for data center power is driving value for gas turbine manufacturers (e.g., Siemens Energy).
      • Proprietary Data: Companies like Reddit and Stripe (Radar) are monetizing data that was previously a "dormant asset" on the balance sheet.
      • Real-World Execution: Traditional firms like John Deere are maintaining moats through "complex interactions" between software and physical hardware (tractors in 130 countries).
  • The Productivity Lag (Solo Paradox): Historically, transformative technologies like electricity (1882) and computing (1987) took decades to show up in productivity statistics because the economy had to "digest" and redesign itself. Collison predicts the AI "digestive" period will be significantly shorter than the 30 years required for electrification.
"The markets are being tediously rational. The most profitable companies are the ones being rewarded with outsize valuations." — John Collison
"It used to be that young companies sold mostly at home, and only ventured out to the open sea of global commerce once they'd gotten big. Now, it's the newborns on Stripe who are globe-trotting their way through the long tail of markets." — Emily Sands
"Transformative technology looks for a long time like it's not doing much if you're looking at the economic gauges... but these are the early indicators of an economy that's replatforming itself." — John Collison
John Collison: Good morning. It is great to be back at Moscone. You can almost feel the tectonic plates shifting underneath us. Yesterday, Will walked you through the 288 new products that we're launching to help you as the economy replatforms around AI. But today, I want to take you to my happy place: getting deep into the economic data. I have a few big trends that we're seeing that I want to talk you through. But first, let's take stock of what's happened since we were together here at Sessions last year. Since we last got together, it's been a little wobbly. In January, the software sector lost a trillion dollars of market value in under 30 days. You're all familiar with the worry: AI makes software more abundant, more substitutable, and potentially less sticky than prior models had assumed. But interestingly, this is a prospective worry. It's not revenue softness that's happening right now. SaaS payment volumes on Stripe are actually a good chunk higher today than before the sell-off. So SaaS is still growing just fine. What does seem to have come back into fashion, though, is profitability, and not just in software but across the entire equity market. This is a chart showing how much market cap has historically accrued to the most profitable and the least profitable companies in the index. You see in the middle here, this is the dot-com boom, where the markets went a bit mad for a moment and briefly the least profitable companies were worth more than the most profitable ones. But if you go forward to today, now, despite all the talk about bubbles, it's the opposite. The markets are being tediously rational. The most profitable companies are the ones being rewarded with outsize valuations. We heard a lot about trade policy over the past year. Believe it or not, it's only been a year since Liberation Day. It feels like eight years, but it's only been one year. And the common wisdom by now is that the tariffs were, kind of economically speaking, a nothingburger; you know, the dog that didn't bark. And you can make that case. Trade flows ended the year only a little bit below 2024 levels. But there were higher prices to absorb as a result of tariffs. And what happened is, early on you had businesses eating the higher costs. But now, quarter by quarter, they're starting to pass them through. We can throw up durable goods prices here. This is how they behave intra-year in normal years. You might be wondering why there is this downward trend. It's because you have deflation in durable goods. Manufacturing productivity generally increases, global trade adds competition, and your flat-screen TV gets cheaper or your washing machine gets better for the same price. So you usually, in most years, get this gentle, predictable slope downward. And you see this only goes up to '24. If we add '25, it’s totally different and bucks the trend. And if we add '26, it's only three months in, but the same thing is happening and even more so. Our view is that tariff costs are still working their way through to the consumer. The story is not written yet. Another phenomenon you might have heard about is this K-shaped economy, the idea that wealthy consumers are holding things up and they're an increasingly large share of the spending. Maybe you saw this go viral on Twitter. This is the new United 787-9 seat map. And you can see that most of the cabin is now dedicated to business class seats. Or you can look at Delta's ticket revenue. They reported that premium seat revenue is now bigger than economy for the first time. This stuff gets a lot of retweets, but we don't actually see it in Stripe's data. If we look at the ratio of high-income to low-income consumer spending in our data, you'd expect this chart to be going up. But it's the opposite. The line is trending gradually downwards. The gap between high-income and low-income spending has been shrinking. So we don't actually see, when we look for it, this overreliance of the economy on high-income consumers that everyone has been talking about. The other major economic topic is the worry that AI is taking all the jobs. And if you look at the numbers, it's true that the labor market is cooling slightly. US unemployment rates are ticking up about a percentage point over the past three years. But you can ask the question: how much of that is actually AI? Part of it is the delayed pandemic hangover. If you look at the hiring rate, companies really binged on hiring in '21 and '22, and they're still unwinding that. Tighter immigration played a bit of a role. And then, of course, you have interest rates. The Fed's latest forecast for the end of 2026 is half a point higher than they predicted at this time two years ago. So money is tighter, and tight money slows hiring. So AI is in the mix, and it's presumably going to have a big future impact, but it's one force among many and probably not the dominant one in the labor market numbers you've seen today. Now, you're probably wondering what we're seeing at Stripe and how it corresponds to what's going on in the wider world. Stripe now processes almost 2% of global GDP, so we have a really useful window into the forefront of the internet economy. There are three areas that I want to dive into deeper with you. The first is that we're seeing a structural increase in economic dynamism. More firms are getting started, they're keeping headcount lean for longer, but they're scaling revenue faster than we've ever seen before. To explore this more, I'd like to call on some help from an actual economist, Stripe's Head of Data and AI, Emily Sands. Emily Sands: Thanks, John. Thanks, John. There's plenty of talk about AI not showing up in the macro data yet, like the labor stats we just saw. If you're as AI-pilled as most of us in this room, that probably creates some cognitive dissonance. But here's the thing: AI actually is showing up in the macro data. You just have to know where to look. Here's US business formations over the past 20 years. You can see a huge bump during the pandemic, which is not surprising. But you can also see it re-accelerating now. Basically, all of the recent growth is coming from what the Census Bureau calls non-employer firms, or what we all call solopreneurs. That's the blue line here. Now, these didn't used to be considered serious businesses. But AI means more of these businesses are getting to real scale. This is the number of solopreneurs in the US doing over $100,000 in revenue. Yes, solopreneurship is now how close to 5 million Americans earn their living. And we're fortunate to have many thriving solopreneurs building with Stripe. It's a great crew. This isn't just an American story. New business registrations are up 40% in Australia, up 70% in Finland, and up 80% in France. So the surge in dynamism is happening across advanced economies. At Stripe, we're seeing this firsthand. John Collison: Yeah, Stripe Atlas is the simplest way to get incorporated in the US. And last week, we celebrated our 100,000th Atlas-founded business. Somewhat topically, yes, 100,000. Somewhat topically for this talk, that business is Amperical, which is building AI software that optimizes profitability for battery energy storage systems. Sounds like something we're going to need. And I actually think Rachana, the founder, is here today. But the point is, we hit 100,000 Atlas incorporations way earlier than we expected. The number has exploded. There's more of them and they're scaling up like crazy. Emily Sands: They really are. So in aggregate, Atlas companies incorporated in 2025 are raking in twice as much revenue as the class of '24 was by this point. Nothing shabby about a doubling. But it's the class of 2026 that's really cooking. So let's zoom in a little bit and you can see that just a few months into the year, the class of '26 is tracking to five times the revenue of last year's cohort. These are very unusual growth rates. And they're driven in part by a generational shift in how companies grow internationally. So one simple way to see this is to ask across Stripe how many companies are earning most of their revenue outside their home country. Five years ago, that was 11.6%. Since then, it's doubled. And they aren't just selling in the obvious places. Among companies making most of their money cross-border, around a quarter are making most outside the top 10 global markets. It's a real long-tail story. The international pecking order has also flipped. It used to be that young companies sold mostly at home and only ventured out to the open sea of global commerce once they'd gotten big. Now, it's the newborns on Stripe who are globe-trotting their way through the long tail of markets. Take the top 100 AI startups on Stripe. The median earns most of its revenue internationally and sells into 55 countries within its first year of existence. Emergent Labs was founded in the US in 2024, but already nearly 70% of their revenue comes from abroad. They're doing material business in many markets. No less than 16 countries drive at least 1% of Emergent's revenue. So this is a totally new startup playbook. You launch globally on day one, you keep headcount very lean, and you automate aggressively. But not every business can be a solopreneur or a top AI startup. So what does all of this mean for the median firm? Despite the fact that one of us has a PhD in economics and the other dropped out of college and now hosts a podcast in a pub, John is going to teach us a little bit about economic history. John Collison: I think you might be surprised to learn just how many economic theories have been devised over a pint, Dr. Sands. Okay, some economic history. Are we ready? In 1931, a 21-year-old British student took a road trip across depression-era America. And back then, as now, a bunch of young people were pretty into communism. Central planning had this real intellectual prestige at the time. A lot of smart people thought that top-down coordination might be the way to do things, much better than messy, chaotic markets. And the student noticed something interesting. Within a firm, resources aren't allocated by prices. There's no little market inside the company. Companies themselves are centrally planned. And so he wondered, if markets are so efficient, why do we keep building little islands of central planning inside markets? His name was Ronald Coase. And his answer was that firms exist because coordinating inside a company is often actually cheaper and easier than coordinating through markets. And that answer won the Nobel Prize 60 years later. They don't rush into decisions over there in Stockholm. I know what you're thinking. John's little economic story is heavy on story and light on economics. But here's where we're going. What's the Coasian reading of AI? In the near term, the within-firm effect is the most obvious. Companies have shared context and systems of record and aligned incentives. And all of that allows for easier coordination with AI. You're probably feeling this in all your own companies already. But in the medium term, external markets are likely to get even more efficient. Agents are already great at discovery. They make it trivially easy to integrate a new piece of software. They make contracting much more straightforward. And as we'll discuss in a minute, they can transact much more frequently and for smaller amounts than humans can. Together, all of this should bring coordination costs between firms down by quite a bit. Now, some of these changes will take a little while to play out, but some of them are already here. So, on net, with AI, we expect fewer people per firm, more output per firm, just more firms, and more coordination happening through market-like mechanisms. Coase would have liked that idea. He always thought firms were an inefficient setup. The more we can do via markets, the better. An indie hacker from another era. Yeah, thanks Emily. Okay, so that was the explosion in business dynamism that we're seeing. The second trend I want to talk about is how commerce itself is becoming agentic. At Stripe, we think about this in increasing levels of autonomy in the purchasing flow, from simple help all the way up to significant agency. But you might be wondering, where have we actually gotten to in 2026? Well, level one is already here. This is software schlepping through forms on your behalf. The in-app checkout experience we're doing with Meta is actually a good example of this. So maybe you find something, maybe in an ad, you express interest and the agent has your details already and it can complete the checkout for you. Super handy, really convenient, but not exactly science fiction. I mean, you mightn't even think of this as agentic even though strictly speaking the software is doing the purchasing for you. It is your agent. Level two is the shift from plain old keyword search that we've had for decades to a shopping assistant that can actually reason within constraints and find products accordingly. Think about when you do some shopping on ChatGPT. Imagine, totally hypothetically, you say, "I need a birthday gift for my brother. He's 38 and has kind of weirdly at this late age gotten very into calisthenics, but he already actually has a lot of calisthenics gear. What's a non-obvious gift that I could get for him for under $100?" Hypothetically. It's not obvious what keywords you would put in to get these results. Wayfair does something similar. You can describe a room or a style or a feeling and the agent rummages through the catalog for you. And you're still in control here; you're making the buying decision. You don't have some awful vase you didn't want showing up unexpected. But you have better ways to find what you're actually looking for. Most people say they already shop this way; I mean you've probably all already done this. But what does it take to get to levels three, four, and five, where agents are making and executing purchasing decisions with real autonomy? Well, one way to peer into the future is OpenClaw. And there, the demand for autonomous commerce is really palpable. This is the cumulative downloads of payment-related skills on ClawHub: 125,000 in 12 weeks. And this is despite the fact that OpenClaw is still pretty hard to use for regular folks. So the question isn't whether there's demand; there is. It's how do we get what's already live at the frontier to go mainstream. Will talked yesterday about how we need the economic infrastructure for AI. Agents need to be able to pay, businesses need to be able to accept payments from those agents, and the whole thing needs a trust layer. And Stripe is working hard on getting all of this deployed. But there's actually one corner of commerce where things are already moving quickly, which is software buying from software. Let me show you what I mean. Previously, yesterday you saw buying stuff, but here just imagine we have an agent that we want to help us do some research. So I have a question I've been wondering. Hey Claude, how is AI demand affecting commodity prices and supply and demand for different energy sources? You guys are probably still typing to your Claude, but you can just talk to it. So what's going on here? You've probably heard so much about how the AI buildout is this massive capex boom. We're building all these new data centers and at various points in the past it's actually been power constrained. And so, we need to plug these data centers into something, but for many, many years we haven't actually expanded the US grid. Now we're adding all this new demand and the electricity grid is a market; it's got a supply and demand equilibrium. And so just when you have this equilibrium that's existed for many years and then you plonk this new demand along and cannonballs into the pool, just what happens? And so I asked Claude to go research this and it's going off and it's finding things. And here, okay, it said its picture is striking. It is sort of... I'm going to need some commodity and equity data to ground this analysis. Alpha Vantage has what I need; I'd like to buy this stuff. Total is four cents, yes, I thought this was within our budget. Yes, please and thank you. Okay, and it is blanching. So what did you see here? The agent analyzed my question, it looked for the relevant sources, it found a paid source, and now it's off buying and downloading that data autonomously. And you're probably used to your AI doing lots of thinking and building, but then it's asking you to carry out the grunt work. It wants you to do the deployment or the checkout flow or the signup. But where we're rapidly headed is the agent doing that work for you. In his demo yesterday, Will used the Link CLI to pay the API provider. Here, as you can see from some of the Tempo requests at the top, we're actually using the Tempo CLI because my agent has a stablecoin wallet. Machine payments can use fiat, but for these tiny purchases, for micropayments, you need a different type of infrastructure. You need stablecoins, which have near-zero transaction costs making it viable for the first time. So it's blanching away here. See, this is where you really need a fellow with a guitar. Just wrong timing. But while, and we did splurge for fast mode for you guys, but it's still blanching away there. And so I just thought I'd show you some cool tabs while we're waiting. This is from Works in Progress, Stripe's magazine about progress. You've probably seen it at the cafe out there and everything. And just to the discussion of all these new energy sources, we have this cool article about how Britain made a lot of progress and then forgot it all in nuclear. What else do we have? We have the Guindex, the first real-world application of AI, where an AI agent called every pub in Ireland to have a real-time tracking of the cost of a Guinness. So, finally something useful. It's still... okay, we're done. So Claude has given me my output; it's open in my browser. And so you see here again, I gave it a single prompt here, just my one-word question, and it spat out this report. And what's it saying? US data center electricity demand is projected to nearly triple by 2028. Hyper-scaler capex expenditure surged 62%. Natural gas prices have surged 104%. You know, natural gas is actually picking up a lot of this. So anyway, super interesting. We don't have time; I would love to just actually read all this. I'm not going to read all it in front of you, but I will be interested to read it later. You guys will probably be interested to read it later. And so what I can actually do is go back to my Claude and say, "Hey Claude, publish and sell this report, price it as you see fit for other agents and humans to find and buy it." Great. So it's off working and, you know, you saw our vibe deploying yesterday. And so it's going off and making a website where any of you can buy it. Actually now that I say that I should maybe check the licensing terms for this Alpha Vantage data set. Do I actually have the rights to commercially redistribute the final report? Phew, okay. Yes, I checked the terms of service, blah blah blah blah blah, we're fine. Okay, so it's doing its thing there. But while we wait for the report to get published, what should you take away from this? Well, agentic commerce, again, it is here. And we think there'll be a really big first mover advantage or an early mover advantage. It's one of the reasons we're moving so quickly at Stripe to enable you to do this. If your product or your platform can possibly support machine-to-machine payments, we think you should build for it now. And it's still going, it's blanching. And again, previously you had to go get API keys or go poke around in the Vercel interface or anything like this. Again, now thanks to Stripe Projects, it can orchestrate all of this for you. We've got other good tabs here. These are some of the companies that already support agentic commerce. This is Parallel and Browserbase for agentic web browsing. You have Postalform, which means your agent can mail a letter for you. So, if you want some compatibility between the new way of working and the old way of working. Really interesting question, which is my third topic: in a world where intelligence can do all of that, and everyone has access to that kind of intelligence, what actually becomes more valuable? There's an old rule in economics: when something gets cheap, its complements get more valuable. So, when containerized shipping collapsed the cost of moving goods, ports that could handle large container ships became much more valuable. So what are the complements to AI? One, certainly, is energy. We're going to need a lot more energy for AI's power to power data centers. In the meantime, we're going to need a lot of gas turbines, which you can read about in the report. You can see the order volumes taking off here. It's also reflected in the market value of the handful of companies that make them. This is Siemens Energy, one of the big gas turbine makers. But a less discussed complement to AI is proprietary data, which gets much more valuable when you can let super intelligent agents reason over it. One way you can tell data's getting more valuable is that companies that used to give it away for free have stopped doing that. This chart shows the percentage of various parts of the internet that have been shut off to AI crawling. And instead, those companies are starting to monetize it. Reddit has always had a ton of data; you know all the comments in the subreddits were always there. But before AI, it was a dormant asset on the balance sheet, and today their non-ad revenue, which comes from data agreements, is $35 million a quarter. We see the same dynamic with our own data; take Stripe Radar. Stripe Radar has always been able to reason across Stripe's entire corpus of data, but in recent years as the AI models have gotten better, the underlying data is then more valuable. Network effect is another one we should talk about. Buyers and sellers still need places to meet, and if switching costs are low, the network effects are even more important. To understand this better, we took a look at the public take rate for 10 top marketplaces. What you see is take rates flat for a few years and then you get this bump up during the pandemic, and then a steady increase over the past three years as marketplaces see higher returns to better AI techniques. The last complement I want to call out is just companies that have figured out the complex interactions between software systems and real-world execution. I think we'll see that that is an enduring moat. Take John Deere. If I asked you to name an AI beneficiary, John Deere might not be the first name that you'd think of. But they've spent years integrating GPS guidance and machine vision and sensor arrays into their equipment. And the defensible part, the moat here, it's not the AI. Lots of people could build the AI. It's having the tractors in the fields across 130 countries. So those are five things we think probably increase in value alongside AI, and therefore create even more durable competitive advantages in the years ahead. For all of you, AI should change how you think about your own competitive advantages. You might previously have built the best software in your space, but you might be finding that software is not the name of the game anymore. What you do have is powerful proprietary data, interesting network effects, real-world operations, and tools that took a decade to get right. All sorts of advantages that hold their value or even become more valuable in a world of abundant intelligence. So as we wrap up, will you indulge me in just a little more economic history? I can't—I can't resist. In 1882, Thomas Edison lit up 82 customers in Lower Manhattan using six dynamos. Finally, electricity in Manhattan! And for decades after, even as electricity adoption grew, productivity growth barely budged or even slowed down as the railroad investment boom started to wear off. And the economists were confused initially. I mean, we had this awesome technology in electricity; why wasn't it showing up in the statistics? And the problem wasn't the technology; the electricity did work. It was the economy that had to digest it. You see, factories had been built around steam: the shafts and the belts and the floor plans. It was all wrong for electricity. And it wasn't until we redesigned factories from scratch that the productivity gains finally appeared. People sometimes forget that this took a full 30 years from 1882 all the way to the late 1910s. And then in the 1910s, in that single decade, the growth rate of output per worker more than doubled. But there was this big lag. We saw the same phenomenon again with the birth of computing. In fact, economists have since dubbed this whole phenomenon the Solo Paradox, after Robert Solo's 1987 quip that computers were everywhere to be seen except in the productivity statistics. And he was right. They weren't to be seen, and they wouldn't be until the mid-1990s. Transformative technology looks for a long time like it's not doing much if you're looking at the economic gauges. You're kind of sitting there and you're tapping the gauge: is this thing on? I think this is what we're actually watching in real time with AI. You see the seeds of this in the phenomena I already mentioned. The minimum efficient size of a serious business is collapsing. Solopreneurs are scaling to seven figures and beyond. Agents are buying from agents. Companies are launching globally from day one. But none of that fits in the old model. These changes might yield productivity dividends tomorrow because we have to digest them. But they're the early indicators of an economy that's replatforming itself. The businesses you're all building now, they're not a footnote to AI history; they are the AI history. They are the story of the economic and productivity gains. Electrification took 30 years to reorganize the economy, but I suspect we won't need to wait anywhere near as long as that for AI. Thank you so much for being here. I hope you're getting a ton out of Sessions. Enjoy the rest of your morning, and I will see you back here for our final fireside with Daniel Gross and Nat Friedman this afternoon. Thank you. Gracias. Go raibh maith agaibh. Obrigada. Dankeschön. Mulțumesc. Merci. Shukran. Dhanyavadagalu. Arigatō gozaimasu. Terima kasih. Khàawp-khun. Gamsahamnida. Xièxiè. Thank you.

Robotics' End Game: Nvidia's Jim Fan

Sequoia Capital(163.0K)·20:03·26.5K views·2026-04-30
Jim FanLead, Embodied Autonomous Research Group, NVIDIA
TL;DRJim Fan argues that robotics is undergoing "The Great Parallel" to the LLM playbook, transitioning from language-heavy models to World Action Models (WAM) trained on massive egocentric video datasets and neural simulations, aiming to pass the physical Turing test within 2–3 years.

TL;DR

Jim Fan argues that robotics is undergoing "The Great Parallel" to the LLM playbook, transitioning from language-heavy models to World Action Models (WAM) trained on massive egocentric video datasets and neural simulations, aiming to pass the physical Turing test within 2–3 years.

The Great Parallel: Copying the LLM Success

Jim Fan proposes that robotics is currently "speedrunning" the same four-stage evolution that defined Large Language Models (LLMs) between 2020 and 2026.

  • The Four Stages:
      • Pre-training (2020/GPT-3): Learning the "grammar" of the world; for robots, this is simulating the next physical state.
      • Alignment/Fine-tuning (2022/InstructGPT): Shaping the simulation to perform useful, specific work.
      • Reasoning (2024/o1): Using Reinforcement Learning (RL) to surpass simple imitation learning.
      • AutoResearch (2026/Expected): Accelerating the loop through autonomous agents and swarm collaboration.
  • The Shift to World Action Models (WAM): Fan argues that current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are "head-heavy" on language but "physics-light" on verbs and spatial reasoning.
      • DreamZero: Nvidia’s new model that "dreams" a few seconds into the future, decoding next world states and motor actions simultaneously to solve zero-shot tasks.
      • Physics Emergence: By predicting pixels at scale, models like Veo-3 naturally learn gravity, buoyancy, and visual planning without explicit coding.

Scaling Data: From Tele-op to Egocentric Video

The "bottleneck" in robotics is the data flywheel. Fan identifies a shift away from traditional, non-scalable collection methods.

  • The Death of Tele-operation: Traditional tele-op is capped by the 24-hour physical limit of a robot/human pair and is often inefficient and slow.
  • Sensorized Human Data:
      • Universal Manipulation Interface (UMI): Wearable robot actuators that allow humans to collect data without the robot body in the loop.
      • DexUMI: An exoskeleton designed by Nvidia that maps human hand movements to 5-finger dexterous robot hands, enabling 1:1 data collection.
  • EgoScale (The FSD Equivalent): This model uses human egocentric (POV) videos as its primary "diet."
      • Scalability: Nvidia pre-trained EgoScale on 21,000 hours of human video with 0% robot data, using only 4 hours of tele-op for final alignment.
      • The Scaling Law: Fan identified a clean log-linear relationship between pre-training hours and validation loss for dexterity, similar to language model scaling laws.

Environments as Data: Neural Simulation

To reach the scale of 1 million environments, robotics must move beyond physical hardware into digital space.

  • The Equation: Fan asserts that "Compute = Environment = Data." The more compute available for simulation, the more data the model generates autonomously.
  • Real-to-Sim-to-Real: Using consumer devices like iPhones as "pocket world scanners" to turn physical objects into interactive 3D assets inside physics simulators.
  • DreamDojo (Neural Simulators): A purely data-driven simulator that takes actions as input and outputs RGB frames and sensor states.
      • It bypasses classical physics equations and graphics engines entirely, learning the "mechanics" of a robot from data.

The Robotics Roadmap to 2040

Fan outlines three major achievements remaining on the "technology tree" for robotics to reach its endgame.

  • The Physical Turing Test (2–3 Years): Reaching a state where a robot's labor is indistinguishable from a human’s in terms of energy in vs. unit labor out.
  • The Physical API: Creating standardized command-line interfaces for robot fleets, enabling "lights-out factories" that function as "printers of atoms" to output fully assembled products from markdown files.
  • Physical AutoResearch (By 2040): The final stage where robots design and build the next generation of themselves, far surpassing human capabilities.
"The main diet for robotics will be egocentric videos... We're able to break the curse of 24 hours per robot per day." — Jim Fan
"Our generation was born too late to explore the earth and too early to explore the stars, but we're born just in time to solve robotics." — Jim Fan
"If you believe in robotics, robotics will believe in you." — Jim Fan (referencing Ilya Sutskever)
And up first, I'm delighted to introduce my friend Jim Fan. Jim leads the embodied autonomous research group at Nvidia, otherwise known as Nvidia Robotics. I think that robots are just one of the most thrilling things that's going to happen. A car basically is a big robot, but I'm excited for robots that can go "beep boop" and lift things for us. Jim was a standout at last year's AI Ascent, and we're delighted to have you back. Thanks everyone, thanks. So it was a summer day in 2016, actually right in this office that we're sitting in. There's a guy in a shiny leather jacket, you know, big biceps, hurling in this large metal tray. On this large piece of metal, he wrote, "To Elon and the OpenAI team, to the future of computing and humanity, I present you the world's first DGX-1." That was the first time I met Jensen. And as any good intern would do, I rushed to get in line to sign my name on it. So can you spot it, my name? It's here. And can you spot another? That's Andre right there. So Andre, we're going to the computer history museum. I feel like a dinosaur. You know back then, I had no clue what I was signing up for. And then no one can describe what happened next better than Ilya himself: "If you believe in deep learning, deep learning will believe in you." And oh boy, did deep learning believe in all of us big time. Three step functions, six years—that's how it all took to bring us here today. The first tick: GPT-3, 2020, pre-training. Next token prediction is really about learning the rules of grammar, the shape of language. It's about simulating how thoughts and code and strings in general should unfold. 2022: InstructGPT, supervised fine-tuning, aligning this simulation to do useful work. OpenAI o1, 2024: reasoning using reinforcement learning to surpass imitation learning. And finally, AutoResearch, 2026: accelerating the whole loop beyond what's humanly possible. So as Andre said, all the labs are getting to the final boss battle. It's a lot more complex at scale, of course—you don't just have a single train.py file to tune. But doing it is "just engineering" and it's going to work. You spin up a swarm of agents, you have them collaborate to tune smaller models, you promote the most promising ideas to increasingly larger scales, and humans contribute on the edges. So for LLMs, they are in the thick of the endgame. And honestly, I'm very jealous. Look at how happy Andre was, big smile on his face. The LLM folks are having the party of their lifetime. They're speedrunning AGI on mythical creatures literally called mistrals. So why can't robotics get a piece of that fun? So as any self-respecting scientist would do, I copy homework and I give it a new name. I call it "The Great Parallel." So instead of simulating strings, can we simulate the next physical world state? And then we could align through action fine-tuning onto a thin slice of that simulation that matters for real robots. And we let reinforcement learning carry the last mile. And that's it. "The Great Parallel," copying the LLM success. If you can't beat them, join them. So please join me in a new episode: "Robotics: The Endgame." And sorry, I just couldn't resist. Nanobananas are too good. Thanks, Demis. So how do we play the endgame? It boils down to two things: model strategy and data strategy. Let's look at the model first. The last three years were dominated by VLAs or vision language action models. Models like GROOT and Pi fall in this category. So we assume that the pre-training is done by a VLM, and we simply graft an action head on top of it. But really, if you think about these models, they are LVAs: language is greater than vision is greater than action because the most amount of parameters are dedicated to language. So language is the first-class citizen followed by vision and action. And by design, VLAs are great at encoding knowledge and nouns, but not so much at physics and verbs. It's kind of head-heavy in the wrong places. This is my favorite example from the original VLA paper: "Move Coke can to Taylor Swift." Yes, it has not seen Taylor Swift before. Yes, it's able to generalize. But this is not quite the pre-training ability that we're looking for. So what's the second pre-training paradigm? And I always thought that it would be something glorious. Unfortunately, it turns out that this is AI video slop, as we call it. You know, I can watch these cats playing banjo on security cam all day. It's peak internet. But really, look at this. No one can take this seriously until we realize that these video models are learning to simulate the next world state internally. So these are some rollouts from Veo-3. You can see that the models pick up gravity, buoyancy, lighting, reflection, and refraction, all by themselves. None of this is coded in. Physics emerge by predicting the next blob of pixels at scale. And even visual planning emerges. Look at how Veo solves these mazes. It solves them by running simulation forward in pixel space. And draw attention to the lower right corner here. This is my favorite example. Let's watch, and you blink if you miss, how Veo-3 solves this one. It's super smart. You know, Veo-3 figures out that if you're not looking, geometry is optional. I call this physics slop. So how do we make these world models useful? Well, we do action fine-tuning. We align this superposition of all possible future states and collapse that onto a thin slice that matters for real robots. Introducing DreamZero. It's a new type of policy model that dreams a couple seconds into the future and acts accordingly. And you know that motor actions, they are high-dimensional continuous signals. So that looks just like pixels. We can render it at the same time as we render the videos. So DreamZero jointly decodes the next world states and next actions. And as a result, it's able to zero-shot solve tasks and verbs that it has never seen in training. And as the robot executes, we can visualize what it is dreaming about. And the correlation is very tight. If the video prediction works, the action works. If the video hallucinates, the action fails. So once again, vision and action are now first-class citizens. And we have a lot of fun with DreamZero. So we just roll the robot around in our lab and then type random things into the prompt box. And of course, DreamZero is not going to get all of these tasks 100% robust, but it's kind of like GPT-2. It's trying to get the shape of the motion correct in every case. DreamZero is our first step towards open-ended, open-vocabulary prompting for robotics. And we call this new type of model "World Action Models" or WAM. So let's all take a moment of silence for our dear friend VLA. They've served us well. Rest in peace. Long live world action models. And next, data strategy. This is Nvidia's chief scientist Bill Dally operating tele-operation inside our lab. Given his salary, I think this is by far the most expensive tele-op trajectory ever collected in our dataset. The past three years have been dominated by tele-operation. It's the golden era, right? VR headsets, extremely optimized latency for streaming, and these complex rigs that look like medieval torture devices. You know, so much investment in industry, so much pain and suffering. And yet for tele-op, it's upper-bounded by 24 hours per robot per day. That's the fundamental physical limit. And actually, who am I kidding? It's more like 3 hours per robot per day, and only when the robot god is merciful, because they throw tantrums all the time. So how can we do better? Well, how about this? You just wear the robot hand on your own hand. So this is called UMI, or Universal Manipulation Interface, and it's a deceptively simple idea. You wear the robot actuator on your hand and directly collect the data as humans, while getting the rest of the robot body out of the loop. Yet I would say UMI is perhaps one of the greatest papers ever written in robotics data, and it spawned two unicorn startups. On the left-hand side is Generalist, improving this design so you can wear the gripper here. And then on the right-hand side, Sunday made these three-finger data gloves. So last year, we took it one step further. We designed this exoskeleton that has a one-to-one mapping with five-finger dextrous robot hands, and we call it DexUMI. Let's look at it in action. On the left, the human direct collecting the data is always the fastest. On the right, look at how difficult tele-op is, right?
