Yeah, it was really nice knowing you guys. This is actually my last Stripe session. This is the last time you will ever let me on this show again. Take it- take it all in. So, I- I mean, there are a bunch of different predictions in here. The stablecoin market's worth 300 billion, some people are calling for 2 trillion over the next three years, four years, five years. But all of that is very macro level. I want to hear about what you're actually seeing in terms of adoption, new use cases, glimpses of- of, you know, new areas to deploy stablecoins, and then just what the big market movers are. Totally. I mean, we- I mean, our- so our business, you know, we shared this in the annual letter last year, grew 4x last year. Our business continues to grow enormously, like we grew 20% over the last month alone. So, you know, we certainly don't see stablecoin slowing down. And I do think total supply is, you know, flat. But these things always go through- Well, yeah, if you want to look at when- when you have a correction in in general digital asset prices, then trading activity at times falls and then it looks like you have a slowdown in adoption. But then you actually have to look at how people are actually using stablecoins for non-trading related activity. Exactly. Like stablecoin transaction volume is growing enormously, the number of transactions are growing enormously, and we certainly see that, you know, the number of folks building with stablecoins is growing enormously. Like we have folks from, you know, a bunch of banks are leaning into stablecoins. Pretty much every fintech that you could think of that is meaningful is doing something with stablecoins, and probably doing it with us. I mean, we obviously announced yesterday with- with Meta paying out folks in stablecoins. So, I do think that this like the hyperbole around stablecoins is dying down a little bit, and that creates the opportunity for some of this- this like fud, but, you- you know, it- it at the same time we're like, you- you know, there there was this thing I think Henry said it yesterday, which, which is funny, of in like the crypto world where for a long time people were pointing at every problem and being like, "Crypto solves this." And now they're no longer pointing at every problem and saying crypto solves this, but- But it actually is. You guys are happy to solve problems with crypto. Yeah, like very, very quietly under the hood it is solving a number of problems like banks for cross-border payments, we're seeing it for working capital needs, we're seeing it for just infrastructure for cards and infrastructure for global expansion. So it's- it's- it's certainly not slowing down. So there's like some very big meaningful categories, the cross-border payments one's very tractable, huge. Yeah. Is there a- a coming intersection of like vibe coding and stablecoins that you're seeing glimpses of? I'm just thinking about like- this like- there was this moment when iPhone games became fairly easy for like a kid to build in their bedroom. And you got Flappy Bird, and I think it was 99 cents. And I'm wondering if- if micro payments, stablecoins, there's some sort of intersection there where you get vibe coding and you get some sort of new flourishing like ecosystem of like- of like smaller transactions. I mean, people don't love micro payments, but there's- there's something that people I feel like could play with if they're spinning something up in a- in an afternoon. Totally. I mean, I- you know, one of my- one of the reasons why we started Bridge is- I was reflecting on the fintech space generally. This was like in 2020 or so. Yeah. And I realized, or one of the realizations that I had was, that the cards were basically just the entirety of fintech. Yeah. Everything was- like, you know, and people talk about platform shifts, like AI is this big platform shift and mobile was a platform shift, but in financial services platform shifts take a little bit of a different shape. And cards were this new form factor, and cards combined with the internet was like 90% of all or- You're talking about like credit cards from like the 70s. That boom. Yeah. Because we rode that boom for so long. There was a time when- when people would pay by doing like reverse debit and there were so many hacks on top of the credit card. Totally. It was a new platform, it was a new primitive on top of which all these new financial services got built. And cards were like growing meekly for a while and then the internet came along, and they were like uniquely well-suited. Obviously Stripe, this is what we do, but uniquely well-suited to solve payment problems on the internet, and then they just took off. It became reflexive, you know, more people were paying on the internet so more people had cards so more people paid in person so then more people bought stuff on the internet and then and then it just took off. But it was this like brand new platform shift in payments tied to an external platform shift. Our- our view was that stablecoins represented another platform shift in payments, and I think AI could be the corresponding one that helps drive adoption of stablecoins. And I think that all the payment problems that we're solving now are like fiat payment problems, and there's big markets and so on, but I think the much bigger market will be, you know, the new means of payment that are going to be required in these agentic services. Like in, you know, just simple things like the incrementality of payments is going to be very different, very small. The velocity of payments is going to be very different, very fast. The reversibility of transactions, like cards are uni- are are not well-suited in a situation where you don't want transactions to be- to be reversed or disputed. And so I think there's a lot of benefits to stablecoins but we're like at the very, very early stage, so you're kind of squinting and like looking like way down the tunnel and like trying to predict what- what will happen, but it feels like it's there. Can you explain the tension between the traditional banking sector and the stablecoin world? Oh, man. Like what are you saying if- if there was a, you know, someone with a bank and they say, "I make my money by taking deposits and I'm heavily regulated, I have to cover those costs with- with- you know, with interest, and you're going to be able to do it without as many regulations, you're going to out-compete me. Should I jump on the stablecoin boom? Is there a world where we work together? How should the traditional banking system interface with the stablecoin world?" I mean, it is, I- I would say, complicated. Okay. Do you spend much time in DC? I- I went earlier this year and, it- it was actually like very- it was like very heartening. I don't know if y'all have spent- spent time there, but like I hadn't been to DC since I was in high school and like a- a high school trip. I grew up in North Carolina so we could like take the school bus up there. And I went into, you know, like you're working at like a tech company of something like Stripe, and it feels like impossible to understand all the ways in which Stripe is growing, and then when you zoom in, it's like literally just like one developer coming to Stripe and signing up and like, you know, one person selling in. And then I went to DC, and DC feels like this incomprehensible like complexity of decision-making and stuff, and then we just met with a bunch of folks and you realize it's just like one person talking to another and like them trying to learn, and then they're going to talk to another person, and they were like genuinely curious. And these folks are like vot- you know, debating stuff on like agriculture and like, you know, airline safety, and then coming in and talking to me about like the minutia of like stablecoin regulation, and they were like pretty well-versed. So I think that like, one, speaks to how- how high on the docket it got for them, and- and like how important it is. And it has like, you know, I spent time there beginning of the year. We've spent more time recently as like Clarity Act has come about, and we think that, you know, it has certainly put the banks and the more stablecoin-friendly folks at odds. But my experience has been that that is not universally the case. Like there's a whole group of banks coming together. There's multiple groups of banks coming together to launch stablecoins. There are multiple community banks that we're actually working with on different stablecoin initiatives. The- the one sort of outlier is- is some of the other folks that have more directly, you know, JP Morgan has their own stablecoin and like their own thing that they're trying to push forward. So there is some competition and- and that sort of thing there, but I- I hope that we can all like work together, but it certainly has been contentious at times. Give me a price prediction. 2030, if I put $1,000 in stablecoins, how- how high can it go? What do you think's possible? Oh, man. I- I mean, I think you are going to be exactly as wealthy then as you are today. That's fantastic. Oh, oh! With- with inflation, with inflation. Advice to founders that are, feeling that want to start a company today but maybe feel like they're too late is part of this AI boom because I feel like when you started Bridge there had been- there was big stablecoin companies that had been created to, I'm, maybe you felt at times that am I too late? Like, clearly you were very- you were very early, and the timing was perfect because you got to like sit out a terrible bear market, and then you and then you were like, "Wow, I'm a genius." That's certainly not what everyone told me. Many people in the midst of it told me I was wasting- wasting my time. Yeah, I would- I would say, I mean, the lived experience for me and, like, you kind of see this now in some of these hotter spaces, is that folks kind of build what they think others want them to build. Like think about something in AI, you know, you're building this thing for this market that is not like a thing that you deeply believe in, and so you're, you're building what you think an investor wants to fund, or what you think other people in the industry will think is interesting. And what happened for me is like, I was building something that I deeply had conviction should exist, and that is what enabled me to persevere through like some really dark times and build a company that, you know, ended up, you know, hitting the- the sort of timing situation at like the perfect, in the perfect moment. But like literally, you know, if I didn't have that conviction, I would have pivoted, you know, a year before. You know, I mean FTX, I mean literally, you know, Terra Luna blew up, then FTX blew up, then SVB went under. I mean, it was like one stablecoin went under or the whole crypto market was done, and then USDC de-pegged. And so we got- Yeah, that's why it's not funny when you make this joke, John. Yeah, I mean we got booted off like four banks before we got started. I- I assume stablecoins are going to stay stable, but I understand there is risk. There is a new technology. Can you, help me understand the interaction of like AI coding agents, AI code review, in stablecoin software development? Historically, I have always had the perception that anything in crypto is not just higher stakes but more technically complicated. It's more like, I don't know, writing a compiler than, you know, a front-end website. And so it's high stakes, LLMs do make mistakes, at the same time the new models, Mythos 5.5, like super-human, and so there's probably a way to integrate that successfully. What- what works? What are you hearing or seeing that- that makes sense? Are we still in like the Centaur era where you need a great software developer working alongside AI? Is this something where you can just throw an- an agent at it and it's, you know, oh, there's no more risk anymore, it's not going to hallucinate? I mean, I would say for us, it- it kind of feels like we are one or two steps behind where, like, you know, it feels like some of the folks are who are in pure software, where they're like, "Oh, I'm just going to, you know, have this long-running agent vibe code an entire browser and ship it in a week and be fine." We're nowhere close to that. Like we could- we can, you know, there are big parts of our business that are pure software in a ton of ways, like think about like transaction monitoring or stuff like that. And- but the risk from a regulatory reason of like having things off and then, you know, missing things, and then not being able to appropriately justify because someone vibe coded a thing, it's just not- not possible. So the ways in which we use it end up being different. And another like very quick example is like, we're standing up a National Trust Bank. As part of standing up a National Trust Bank, there's a bunch of requirements. One of which is that every- every commit has to be approved by someone. Wait, what? So- so like you literally cannot have- Over for- fast takeoff, takeoff's cooked. This is the most bearish- Still got in humans. Still got in humans. Is- is FDA for AI? This was something somebody was saying. It is like, you know, the- the Jevons paradox of employment. We're just going to have to hire like more and more and more code reviewers. Everyone's- Good point. Yeah, it's an interest- I was talking to someone, a small business owner who was, very, very fired up on vibe coding. He told me specifically he hadn't slept in four nights. I was asking the- the product that he was working on, he was like, "This is something I've thought about building forever." I was like, "Well, have you ever thought about like rebuilding the sort of like vertical SaaS fintech platform that you run your whole business on?" And he was like, "Nah, 1,200 a year, it's like they give me so much. It's like it would never make sense to..." And so it's like the one piece of like core technology that he runs his business on, he even- he- he's so fired up on vibe coding he's like, "I'd never rebuild that, would never make sense." And- and so there's two things: there's like a value equation, and then there's like a potential risk kind of cost equation. And I feel like with stablecoin specifically, the potential cost in- in crypto, like the potential cost is so astronomical where like saving a little bit here to then introduce like a hundred million dollars of- of risk and- Yeah, but- but there's a bunch of other things that are amazing like for us. We are- we have like re-architected a lot of our- a lot of our infrastructure to be- like before, you know, one of the- one of the hard things in crypto is adding a bunch of different blockchains. Like every- a lot of blockchains can kind of be the same, but many are very different, and so it's super tedious to add a bunch of- a bunch of different blockchains. We, or the same thing is true of like adding new payment rails. So, you know, you might add a US bank, and then you need a redundant US bank, and then you need a European bank, and then you need a bank in Mexico, and then- and all of these integrations kind of look the same. You know, you're- you're getting money in, you're checking the money's in, you're sending money out, you know? And what we have built is the ability to sort of add all of these things very, very quickly. And so we took like, you know, what used to take, you know, two weeks to add a new blockchain now takes, you know, someone actually did it in a meeting the other day. Of like adding a new stablecoin on a new- on a new blockchain all with- with Claude that, you know- Does that mean- Does that mean we need 10,000 new Layer 1s? That- that is- that's the Jevons paradox of blockchains. Because it's easier to do a new chain we need 100,000 more, a million more. Yes. Maybe honestly one for one. Every American should have their own Layer 1. We're just maximalists across every dimension. There was a moment in stablecoins maybe last year where it was like this- this like- this small like county in New Mexico is going to have their own chain. Or it was like Wyoming. Wyoming! Wyoming has its own stablecoin, so we're going to have every state's going to have its own stablecoin. Every state's going to have its own blockchain. Maybe even every city. Yeah. You could have your own blockchain. Yeah, yeah, neighborhood. Yeah, neighborhood, there you go. Yeah, the Mission stablecoin. Yeah, the Presidio stablecoin. Which one would you buy? SoMA. Mavericks. I'm going for the Mavericks stablecoin. Okay. Well, that's just a taste thing. Anything else? No, this was great. Thank you for coming on. So good to see y'all. Thanks for having me. Great to meet in person. We're going to close it out. Close it out with us. Leave us five stars on Apple Podcast and Spotify, sign up for our newsletter at TBPN.com. We'll be back tomorrow in the TBPN Ultradome AM sharp. Thank you for tuning in and have a great day. Goodbye. It's been an honor. Cheers! Goodbye! Cheers!

Jerome Powell Decides to Stay on the Federal Reserve Board of Governors

Wall Street Journal Opinion(136.0K)·27:08·2.0K views·2026-04-30
Jerome PowellOutgoing Chair, Federal Reserve Kevin WarshFederal Reserve
TL;DRKevin Warsh is set to become Federal Reserve Chairman amid an AI-driven but inflationary economy, while outgoing Chair Jerome Powell’s unprecedented decision to remain on the Board of Governors creates a complex leadership dynamic that limits the…
Trump administration’s...

TL;DR

Kevin Warsh is set to become Federal Reserve Chairman amid an AI-driven but inflationary economy, while outgoing Chair Jerome Powell’s unprecedented decision to remain on the Board of Governors creates a complex leadership dynamic that limits the Trump administration’s ability to appoint a new majority.

Economic Stagnation vs. The AI Investment Boom

The U.S. economy shows modest growth with underlying "stagflationary" signals as Kevin Warsh prepares to take office.

  • GDP Trends: First-quarter GDP grew at 2%, an increase from the previous quarter’s 0.5%, but remains far from a "boom."
  • The AI Rescue: Investment in AI is currently the primary driver of growth.
      • Capital Expenditure: Big Tech firms (Meta, Microsoft, Google, Amazon) spent $130 billion on AI in Q1 alone, with annual spending projected to reach $700 billion.
      • Growth Concentration: Growth is heavily concentrated in IT equipment and intellectual property; without this "AI boom," the broader economy would likely be flat or mediocre.
  • Consumer Weakness: Consumer spending is cooling, with much of the current expenditure driven by necessities like healthcare and rising gasoline prices rather than discretionary confidence.

Persistent Inflation and Tariff Impacts

Despite the Federal Reserve's previous efforts, inflation remains significantly above the 2% target, complicating the transition to the Warsh era.

  • PCE Data: Personal Consumption Expenditure (PCE) inflation is at 4.3%–4.5% year-over-year, suggesting the Fed may have been premature in previous interest rate cuts.
  • Tariff Pressure: Significant price increases are appearing in goods subject to tariffs:
      • Recreational vehicles: Up 20.4%
      • Clothing and footwear: Up 6.8%
      • Household furnishings: Up 5.4%
  • Policy Miscalculation: Joe Sternberg notes the Fed has been "unduly optimistic" regarding inflation returning to target, failing to account for the "roller coaster" effect similar to the 1970s.

The Powell-Warsh Leadership Transition

Jerome Powell’s decision to stay on the Board of Governors after his chairmanship ends in May is a major historical anomaly with significant political implications.

  • Historical Rarity: Powell will be the first Fed Chair in 78 years to remain on the board as a governor after stepping down from the top post.
  • The DOJ Probe: Powell cites a recently closed Department of Justice criminal investigation into Fed reconstruction costs as his reason for staying, seeking "finality and transparency."
  • Political Strategy: By remaining on the board, Powell denies President Trump the opportunity to appoint a new governor, preventing the administration from securing an immediate majority on the seven-member board.
  • Board Dynamics: While Powell claims he will maintain a "low profile," his presence—and his deep connections to the press—may create an "over the shoulder" environment for Warsh.

Internal Fed Friction and Reform Agenda

Kevin Warsh inherits a Federal Reserve that is seeing increased internal dissent and a push for structural reform.

  • Regional Dissent: Three regional bank presidents recently dissented from the FOMC statement, objecting to "dovish" language—a signal that Warsh may face pressure to maintain a hawkish stance.
  • Warsh’s Philosophy: Warsh has expressed a desire for "messy meetings" with more open debate and less reliance on the consensus-driven models used by the current staff.
  • Structural Goals: Warsh is expected to push for a return to "classic" open market operations, moving away from the era of massive bond-buying (quantitative easing) that characterized the Bernanke and Powell eras.

Notable Quotes

"I’ve said that I will not leave the board until this investigation is well and truly over with transparency and finality, and I stand by that." — Jerome Powell, Outgoing Fed Chair
"If you connect the dots, this is what I would call an AI boom... it is an AI-driven economy, I would say, at this point." — Allysia Finley, WSJ Editorial Board
"The reality is the American households are now five years or more into this cycle of rapidly escalating prices... the Fed’s predictions for what inflation would do in the future have always turned out to be wrong." — Joe Sternberg, WSJ Editorial Board
"By Powell staying on, he's essentially denying Trump a majority on the board." — Allysia Finley, WSJ Editorial Board
The U.S. economy keeps growing modestly, but inflation is getting higher. As the new economic data rolls in from the first quarter of 2026, that is the economy that Kevin Warsh will inherit as the new chairman of the Federal Reserve. He's expected to be confirmed now after passing the Senate Banking Committee this week, be confirmed by the full Senate, and take the job sometime in the next few weeks before the next Fed Open Market Committee meeting in May. Meanwhile, his predecessor, Jerome Powell, this week said that he will stay on as a member of the seven-member Board of Governors at the Fed, even after his chairmanship ends. So, what's next for the Federal Reserve and for the American economy? Welcome. I'm Paul Gigot. Those are our subjects for today on Potomac Watch, and I'm here with my colleagues Allysia Finley and Joe Sternberg, two redoubtable Fed watchers and economic watchers. So let's talk first about the big economic news. First quarter GDP rolled in. Gross Domestic Product grew at 2%. That's about up from 0.5% in the fourth quarter, but it's hardly boom times. But the more interesting things in the report, Allysia, are the internals. I think that's what's really interesting and what components grew, what didn't. There was modest growth in consumer spending, but a big increase contribution from investment and a particular kind of investment. Right, so consumer spending made up about one percentage point of the 2% growth, which is actually down a little bit from the prior quarters, and actually most of that was healthcare. Now the business investment picked up, particularly in the equipment and in information, IT equipment. And you also saw, which contributed about .88, so .9 percentage points. And then another pretty strong growth in intellectual property products, and most of that was again software. And if you connect the dots, this is what I would call an AI boom. And actually there was an earnings report out this week from the four big tech companies—Meta, Microsoft, Google, and Amazon—saying that they spent $130 billion just in the first quarter on AI. It's an astonishing figure. And they're planning to increase it to seven, or increase the rate, so it'll be around $700 billion in total this year. So it is an AI-driven economy, I would say, at this point. Well, basically you're talking about most of the growth coming from artificial intelligence. Data centers, equipment, and whatnot. It's great to see that kind of investment and, but it's at the stage where it's kind of rescued the overall growth of the economy, Joe. Without that, we were looking at, you know, flat to mediocre. Yeah, and I think that that is a big problem. And we should understand that this is not just an economic issue. It's going to become a political problem because in case anyone's forgotten, we have a midterm election coming up in November. So I think that voters are going to be starting to think over the next couple months about whether they're happy with the economy and if they're unhappy whom they want to blame for that. And so I think from that perspective a couple things jumped out at me. I mean any kind of investment boom in anything is generally good news. But I think that what we're seeing is very different from the sort of broad-based boom, particularly in manufacturing, that the Trump administration seemed to be promising when he came into office more than a year ago. So I think that that's one factor that's in play here. And I think also you revisited that matter of the consumer spending and the fact that that does seem to be a bit weaker in this report than it had been in previous quarters. I mean that's an important signal because a certain amount of that is sentiment. People will go out and they will spend money when they feel confident about their jobs, when they feel confident about where the economy is heading. It's a little troubling that you can have a situation where on the one hand there is a form of investment boom going on that has huge promise for economic growth and productivity down the road. Households, consumers don't seem to be feeling that or or deriving optimism from that in quite the same way. I mean some of that might be related to the fact that it is AI. There's a lot of anxiety about the implications that AI might have in the job market. But I think part of it might be a symptom of the fact that we aren't getting the kind of broad-based growth that generally leaves households and voters feeling good about the economy. The consumer spending numbers were up better in March, Allysia, which is the third month of the first quarter. It's January through March. So that's somewhat encouraging. But let's break down because particularly the war started in February 28th, I think, in Iran. So that was the first month of real impact on consumer psychology from the war. But I wonder what role tariffs are playing here in dampening consumer spending. So as you mentioned, most of the increase, actually, when I looked into the details for March, it was mostly energy, gasoline, that drove about half of the increase in consumer spending. So that was the reason why we had a very strong March, as it turns out, as people didn't cut back too much on other things. They just spent a lot more on gasoline. On gasoline because they had to. Right. But if you dig into the PCE price numbers, it's interesting because you have a 4.5% growth actually increase in inflation at least as the Fed measures it in overall, and then 4.3% in the quarter. So there's not much of a difference from first quarter this year to first quarter last year. But then you look, dig into the components of that, and recreational goods and vehicles increased 20.4% year over year. Overall goods prices increased 5.4%. Household furnishings 5.4%. Clothing, footwear, 6.8%. These are all things that are subject to tariffs, and which does show that the tariffs' effects are having an effect because actually if you go back and look before President Trump's tariffs, prices were actually declining in many of these goods. They increased during the Biden inflation, but then they started to come down. So I think that that is one at least driver of at least the price increases in goods. Allysia mentioned PCE. That's Personal Consumption Expenditure Survey, which is one measure of inflation. And it happens to be the one, particularly the core without food and energy, that measure, that's what the Fed claims to follow, the Federal Reserve. And they follow it in part because it measures substitution effects, which means that if you're a consumer and you see your brand-name product going up in price, you'll say, "Okay, I'm going to buy the generic brand or something else that that might be cheaper." And PCE tries to capture that. But I have to say there's no reassurance, Joe, in in these numbers if you happen to be Kevin Warsh coming in at the Federal Reserve. And I'd want to talk about what, you know, Warsh and the politics of that in a minute, but just looking at the inflation numbers, PCE up 4.5% over the 12 months, 4.3% as Allysia said. This is not reassuring at all that inflation is coming down. And I think suggests to me that Powell and the Fed, Jerome Powell and the Fed, were premature again in in how they, how they handled interest rates. And maybe they shouldn't have eased as much as they have. I think it's an important point because remember, the thing about these inflation data is that there's always an excuse that is available if you really want one. So I mean for example in his press conferences and other statements, Chairman Powell has always been able to point to, for example, the pandemic was a major unforeseen external event that affected inflation. Or then you have the Iran war and the effect on energy prices that is going to filter into the inflation data. And you potentially had the tariffs and and their effect on the price level and the debate about whether or not that's inflationary. There's always something going on if you want to excuse or explain elevated inflation that you can grab onto. But the reality is the American households are now five years or more into this cycle of rapidly escalating prices. And remember the inflation is not as high now as it was at the peak when we were above 9% in some months. But any rise above zero still means that people are paying more for things. And we seem to be moving in the wrong direction in in some of these months where the inflation is actually accelerating instead of continuing its deceleration toward the Fed's 2% goal. And I think that suggests that there are things that are going on in the economy in terms of the effects of some of these factors on prices and the way the Fed has been making policy and the effects of those policies that the Fed itself has not really understood. Because their predictions for what inflation would do in the future have always turned out to be wrong. They've always been unduly optimistic that they would get back to their target much sooner than they have because they haven't gotten back to the target yet. So I think this is a big task for Kevin Warsh as he comes in, understanding exactly why it is that the Fed seems to be so bad at anticipating what's going to happen with inflation and then also coming up with some way via policy that he can cut through these one-off factors that keep popping up and deal with the underlying trend, which is still a big problem. The nightmare scenario for the economy and for the Federal Reserve would be have the 2020s be a repeat of the 1970s. I remember the '70s. You don't, but you have read about them. We have talked about them. And what I mean by that is you had the big coming out of the late '60s and early '70s, a big boost in inflation. And then you had a a kind of a respite. And then Arthur Burns under Richard Nixon boosted inflation again, it came up. And then they thought they'd, the interest rates went up. There was a big recession in the mid-'70s, one of the reasons Jimmy Carter won. And Nixon was impeached, by the way, in part because of of of that, the public was sour on the economy at the time. Not the only reason, but one of them. And then it accelerated again back in the late '70s. And you get this roller coaster inflation effect. And we all thought, okay, inflation goes up 9.1% under Biden at its peak, then it comes down as the Fed raises interest rates, and we think, okay, they're going to hit that 2% target. Well, it's kind of stuck at about three, a little under three, and now coming back up. Now I don't think it's time to panic if you're the Fed and suddenly start raising rates, particularly in the teeth of a big oil shock. But it's going to make, it does raise real questions about whether or not the Fed has been too easy and how it's going to get this down in the coming months. I think that's right, and you mentioned that it was probably, or could have been premature to cut when they started doing that in 2024. Inflation was by no means defeated by then. And you've seen asset prices in particular boom, which suggests that monetary policy by no means is restrictive. You're seeing it in higher or rather tighter spreads between junk bonds and and Treasuries, safe assets. And this has also led to a lot of, you know, risky investment, by which I mean you've got, you've seen, I wouldn't call it a private credit bubble, but there have been companies that have definitely benefited from very easy or loose monetary policy. And I think what we're starting to see is now with the higher rates some of those are struggling to repay debt and you're starting to see defaults. So I don't think it's, I mean I think there's some financial problems, that crack-ups that could be ahead too. Yeah, particularly if you see a a slowdown in growth. Let's turn to the Fed itself and the big transition that we're going to have there here coming. Kevin Warsh's nomination by President Trump was confirmed, sent out, moved out of the Senate Banking Committee on a party-line vote on Wednesday after Tom Tillis, the Republican who had put a hold on the Warsh nomination, agreed to withdraw his objection. This occurred after the Justice Department signaled it was dropping, it is dropping, the investigation into the Federal Reserve criminal probe about its reconstruction cost overruns and whatever comments Jerome Powell the chairman had said about that to the Congress. The subpoena the Justice Department had sent to the Fed was essentially just a fishing expedition, had no real evidence of any crime. So now the Fed has dropped, now the Justice Department has dropped that probe. It says it, it could. But Powell says he's still not going to step down. He wants to see even more progress. I guess he wants to see this thing dead and buried. Let's listen to the chairman on Wednesday. "I welcomed the announcement last Friday by the U.S. Attorney for the District of Columbia that she had closed the criminal investigation. She also noted, however, that she would not hesitate to restart the investigation. Over the weekend, the Department of Justice provided assurances that they will not reopen the investigation unless there's a criminal referral from the Fed's Inspector General. And absent such a referral, if they do appeal the recent court decision, they would not seek as part of that appeal to restart the investigation or send new subpoenas. I've said that I will not leave the board until this investigation is well and truly over with transparency and finality, and I stand by that. I'm encouraged by recent developments, and I'm watching the remaining steps in this process carefully." The court decision he's referring to, Joe, is the one from a court in the District of Columbia that had quashed that Justice Department subpoena. You would think from this, certainly Tom Tillis was satisfied enough to vote for Warsh, but Powell isn't. How do you read his decision to stay on? It's the first time, if he, if he does stay on after his chairmanship expires in mid-May. If he does stay on, he'll be the first Fed chairman who stays on as a governor in 78 years. So, is this really about the criminal probe, I guess, is the question? It's hard to say. I mean, I will confess that I am somewhat sympathetic to Powell only in the sense that Trump is so mercurial that I mean who ever knows from one day to the next what's going on. But I mean to the extent that you can have finality about anything in the Trump administration, I think that they have been pretty clear that this probe is over. And I think that the very good reason for that is that they understood that they were getting political pushback from quarters where they might not have expected that, including Senator Tillis, who is a Republican, remember. So I think that, you know, in in many ways you could look at this as actually a victory for Fed the Fed's institutional independence and rebuffing this spurious criminal probe that the Department of Justice had launched. And Powell has done his job as a good institutionalist. I mean the thing that then that worries me about this is that because of all of those factors, because Justice has been so clear that they are dropping the probe, because Tillis himself has taken yes for an answer from the Trump administration regarding the senator's own concerns about this situation, Powell hasn't really articulated what more he wants before he's prepared to leave. And I think that that puts him and potentially the Fed in a bit of a tricky spot because it now means that he's going to be in a form of staring contest with the Trump administration where I guess what they have to guess what more they have to do in order to convince him that they have sufficiently protected the Fed's independence that he can resign as a governor. I think that it is a really difficult spot that he is putting himself in and that he's putting the administration and the Fed in, and also frankly that he's putting Kevin Warsh in as his successor now comes in with the outgoing chairman still hovering over his shoulder at the meeting table. Powell was gracious to Warsh in his comments. He did say "Congratulations." and he also said that he doesn't plan to be a loud member of the Board of Governors. He'll keep a low profile. So I guess that suggests he's not going to go out and give a speech or counter-programming after every meeting at least in his public comments. The question is what he or his allies talk to the press on a confidential basis. Leaking in Washington is not unheard of and Powell has very, very good connections to the press. But this Powell decision is important not merely for being himself present in the intellectual councils and debates of the Fed, but because if he steps down from as a governor then Trump gets another appointee. And I wonder if in the back of Powell's mind it's "I don't want to give Trump another appointee." What do you think? Yeah, I've kind of wondered if this is just a little bit of a passive-aggressive move on his part to deny Trump another appointee. Not merely because he worries that, you know, the appointee could be Steve Myron, who's very dovish, but he's a little peeved by how Trump has tried to jawbone him and has lashed out and attacked him. And so this is his way of getting even, so to speak. Right now, Trump has three nominees on the board, technically, including his first term. You have Chris Waller, Miki Bowman, who's actually the head of the regulation or the supervision. And then you'll have Kevin Warsh. So if he got another one, he would then have a majority. So by Powell staying on, he's essentially denying Trump a majority on the board. Yeah, which is potentially significant. Kevin Warsh comes in with an ambitious agenda of reform at the Fed and he'll want allies on the board to be able to implement that because, Miki Bowman, I think, will be an ally on supervision, but you never know about Waller. I think Christopher Waller is sorry he didn't get the job. So I'm not counting on him to be a rubber stamp for Kevin Warsh. There's another thing that happened Wednesday, Joe, which I think is also going to be a challenge for Kevin Warsh, and that is the three regional bank presidents dissented from the Federal Open Market Committee statement. The Fed Open Market Committee didn't move interest rates at all, but they objected to the fact that what they said was a dovish element in the language of the statement. I'll be honest with you, I didn't see any real dovish statement in the FOMC news release, but they did. But what I wonder is, is that a deliberate shot at a warning to Kevin Warsh: "Don't come in here doing cutting rates willy-nilly like Donald Trump wants you to do." It may well be. Although I mean to be fair to Warsh, I don't think that that is what he has suggested, and I mean to the extent that, he has not suggested that. I'm just saying they may be taking their notes here from from Donald Trump. I agree with you, Warsh is not, that's not Warsh's agenda. Well, I think that an interesting and important factor here actually is just the fact that there's been more dissent in the meetings lately, and I think that's a very healthy development for the Fed. And that's actually probably a dynamic that Warsh, well not probably, that's definitely a dynamic that Warsh is going to welcome. I thought that one of the most important things that he said in his Senate confirmation hearing recently is that he would welcome much messier meetings was the phrase that he used, where there would be more dissension on the FOMC. Because I think given the uncertainties that they face around the economy, the uncertainties they face around how well the Fed understands the economy and the quality of the data that it's looking at and the quality of its economic models and the extent to which they've been wrong before, I think there is actually a lot of scope for the different members of the Fed to do their jobs and participate actively in these meetings and voice disagreements when they have them. And I think that if you have regional Reserve Bank presidents in particular voicing a particular view where they might prefer a somewhat more hawkish tilt in the statements which implies a a view about the kind of policy they might want to see in the future, that's a really useful signal for a chairman to have. Because remember the role that those regional Reserve Bank presidents play in the system is surveying their regional areas and really understanding what's happening on the economy in the economy in the particular regions where they are based. And I think that's very valuable feedback to have in Washington. And I think that Warsh is going to welcome that rather than being concerned that he is inheriting an FOMC where there is that degree of dissent. Based on my own reporting, I think that Kevin Warsh is not going to be upset necessarily by Powell's presence on the continuing presence on the Fed board. I think when it comes to intellectual debates at the FOMC meetings, Allysia, he has the experience, he has the intellectual candlepower. You remember he was a governor himself during the crisis for several years, financial crisis of the last decade. And so he was, he knows where some of the bodies are buried, he knows the staff. He comes in pretty well-armed to be able to make his case for whatever policy he thinks is appropriate. I think the bigger question is when it comes to reforming the Fed and changing its monetary policy, moving back as I think he wants to from an era of massive bond buying back to just the classic open market operations pre-crisis. Right, and he faces perhaps a resistant staff who are kind of ingrained and tied to their models. You also face a very press corps who will be opposed to what he's trying to do, and you've already seen or you've heard some criticism from other, you know, liberal economic, or economists Janet Yellen and Ben Bernanke, who were previous Fed chairs and he has criticized, or Warsh has criticized in his writings for expanding the Fed's remit into fiscal policy. And so you will probably hear some backbenchers try to undermine him from both within and without. All right, so we'll leave it at there today. But this drama at the Federal Reserve is going to be one thing to watch very closely this year in Washington. And of course it has enormous significance for the American economy and the livelihoods of each and every one of us. I want to, we'll leave it at there today. Thanks to Joe Sternberg. Thanks to Allysia Finley. And as always, thank you for listening. We are here every day on Potomac Watch.

OpenAI's Greg Brockman: Why Human Attention Is the New BottleneckOpenAI's

Sequoia Capital(163.0K)·28:27·21.6K views·2026-04-30
Greg BrockmanCo-founder and President, OpenAI Alfred LinPartner, Sequoia Capital
TL;DR"We're about 80% of the way there [to AGI] in that we have models that are smart, they're very…
Greg Brockman believes OpenAI is 80% of the way to AGI, shifting the primary bottleneck of productivity from "performing tasks" to "human attention and vision" as agentic tools now handle up to 80% of complex workflows like software engineering. Infinite Demand for...

TL;DR: Greg Brockman believes OpenAI is 80% of the way to AGI, shifting the primary bottleneck of productivity from "performing tasks" to "human attention and vision" as agentic tools now handle up to 80% of complex workflows like software engineering.

The Scaling Mystery and the Compute Hunger

  • Infinite Demand for Intelligence: OpenAI’s business model is a simple loop of buying/building compute and reselling it as intelligence; Brockman asserts that as long as margins are positive, demand for problem-solving remains unlimited.
      • GPU Scarcity: Despite aggressive acquisition, OpenAI is "definitely not" satisfied with current compute levels; Brockman’s standing order to his team since the launch of ChatGPT has been to secure "all of it."
  • The Empirical Truth of Scaling Laws: Brockman describes scaling laws as a "beautiful mystery" of the universe—fundamental truths that allow 1940s-era neural network concepts to gain massive capabilities simply by adding computation.
      • Architectural Innovation: While the Transformer remains foundational, OpenAI is moving beyond the original 2018 designs through "micro-tweaks" in data formatting and significant shifts in fundamental algorithms to avoid hitting a "wall."

The Agentic Shift: From Tools to Teammates

  • The 80% Milestone: In December 2025, agentic coding tools transitioned from writing 20% of code to 80%, marking their shift from a "sideshow" to the primary driver of development.
      • Autonomous Problem Solving: Brockman cited an internal case where an engineer provided a design doc to a model and woke up to find the model had not only implemented the code but had autonomously run a profiler, identified bottlenecks, and iterated until the result was optimized.
  • Chronicle and Contextual Memory: OpenAI announced a new tool called Chronicle that plugs into Codex, allowing the AI to see everything the user does and form a persistent memory.
      • Ending "Explanation Overhead": The goal is to stop humans from having to explain context to their computers; the AI should inherently know what the user was doing five minutes ago or what a colleague discussed in a previous meeting.

Organizational Evolution and the Attention Bottleneck

  • Human Attention as the New Scarcity: Because AI makes the "doing" of tasks cheap and easy, the scarcest resource in an AI-augmented world is the human ability to judge quality and alignment with values.
      • The "EQ" of Agents: Brockman highlighted a failure mode where an agent proactively escalated a slow Slack response to a manager; he argues that refining the "EQ" of models—knowing when to ask for permission versus when to act—is a critical frontier.
  • The Future of Company Structure: Traditional hierarchies may be replaced by "solopreneurs" or very small, flat teams managing massive agentic workforces.
      • The CEO of Agents: Brockman envisions a future where an individual acts as the CEO of an organization comprising 100,000 agents, focusing on vision rather than mechanics.
  • Internal Governance Challenges: As AI creates "derived artifacts" (like wikis or dashboards), companies need new technical architectures to track data provenance and permissions, ensuring that if a source document is restricted, the AI-generated summary is also invalidated.

Security, Responsibility, and the Science Frontier

  • The Defense-Forward Strategy: OpenAI is leaning into "Trusted Access" programs for cybersecurity, using models to perform end-to-end red teaming and codebase scanning to stay ahead of bad actors.
  • A Renaissance in Physical Science: While LLMs initially excelled in digital domains, Brockman sees "signs of life" in physical sciences.
      • Physics Breakthrough: OpenAI’s models recently derived a complex physics formula that experts previously deemed unsolvable, potentially aiding the quest for quantum gravity.
      • Biology and "Messy Reality": Moving into biology requires the AI to handle non-simulated, adversarial environments, a challenge Brockman believes OpenAI is solving by training agents on real-world software engineering complexities.

Notable Quotes

"We're about 80% of the way there [to AGI] in that we have models that are smart, they're very capable... if you give it all the context, then yes, I think they are just so capable." — Greg Brockman
"Human attention is going to be this incredibly scarce resource. The doing of things now is easy. The 'is this a good thing? Is this what I wanted? Is this aligned with my values?' That is going to become the single most important bottleneck." — Greg Brockman
"Do you want to be a CEO of an organization of like 100,000 agents? Like that actually seems pretty good. We're all going to be able to get so much more done, but the mechanics of it are going to feel as different as going from writing with a quill to being able to just send a text message." — Greg Brockman
So, Greg, thank you for coming back here. I don't think we ever charged you for rent, so maybe we'll send you an invoice later. But Greg, you've been part of like two really spectacular companies: Stripe, as employee number four and then the first CTO. I just recently heard that they process 1.6% of the global GDP. You must be proud of that. That's amazing. You must be even more proud of the fact that OpenAI has almost a billion, or maybe more than a billion, in weekly active users at this point. I mean, it's all very exciting. It shows you what technology can do. And you're not just co-founder and president, but you're also chief builder at OpenAI. I heard that was one of your titles. I'm not sure if there was ever an official title, but I've been called many things, let's just say that. Well, you have an audience of great builders here, so we'll start from all the way at the bottom of the stack. OpenAI has multiple stacks of the business, one of which is compute. And you guys have been very aggressive, very aggressive on securing compute. Why is that? Well, in many ways, we have a very simple business. We buy, rent, build compute and we resell it at a margin. That's it. As long as the margin's positive, then you want to scale it because the demand for solving problems, the demand for intelligence, that's unlimited. The AIs that we have right now really are able to rise to the challenge of effectively any kind of problem that you want to throw at them. Do you have enough compute? No. Really? Yeah, definitely not. I was just with Matt Garman, and he says the GPU compute availability in 2026 rounds to zero. Don't you guys have all of it? I mean, we would love more. We're constantly out there hunting for more, honestly. And I'll tell you, like when we first launched ChatGPT, I remember being on a call with my team, and they were like, "Alright, how much compute should we buy?" And I said, "All of it." And they're like, "No, no, no, seriously, like come on, how much should we buy?" I'm like, "No matter how fast we try to ramp compute, I guarantee we're not going to be able to keep up with demand." And that has been true ever since. That's fascinating. Moving up from compute, since I don't know if much of this audience can help you with securing more compute because most of them are founders of startups. About architecture and scaling laws, where are we in the scaling laws? Are they still doubling each year? Are you changing architecture? What are you guys pushing on the frontier on the research side? Well, I would say first of all, the scaling laws are a deep and very beautiful mystery. They feel deeply fundamental. It's like this scientific truth that just like you think about physics and Newton's laws and things like that, there's somehow this truth of the universe, and they're empirical. We don't necessarily have all the theory to explain exactly why it works, but to me, the most beautiful thing is that neural networks were really designed in the 1940s before there were computers. Somehow we've been able to take the exact ideas that were developed back then and apply increasing amounts of computation. As you pour more compute into the models, they get correspondingly more capable, and it just keeps going. There's no wall. And that, I think, is a beautiful thing. That's pretty beautiful. Are there more research or more algorithms that are in the works? Because in the past we had neural networks, to your point, in the 1940s, but we didn't have the compute for it. Now that we have the compute for it, are we just pushing the same things or are there new architectures and new ideas coming up? Yeah, so I would think of it as we absolutely have new ideas that are constantly powering what we do. It's very simplified to say, "Well, let's take a neural network from the 1940s and put it in a gigawatt data center." We have made tons of innovations and we constantly are improving things. Sometimes these are micro-tweaks, like you just realize that the way you've been formatting data was not quite right, and that can actually be a very big deal. Sometimes it's larger. You think about the shift from the LSTM to the Transformer, and I don't think the Transformer is, like, everyone's moved past the Transformer as described in the 2018 paper. There's constant innovation happening. And I think of places that have been perhaps the most invested in long-term research on how to improve the architectures, how to improve the fundamental algorithms, and how to get the paradigm shifts. I think OpenAI has been leading the pack there, and that's something we continue to invest in, and I see lots of fruit on the horizon. Got it. And on the models, does OpenAI have a formal definition for AGI? Are we close? Are we not close? Patronia published this thing that we are at AGI functionally. Do you agree with that? Do you not agree with that? Well, we do have a formal definition, but to some extent, one thing I have learned is that everyone has their own intuitions about what AGI is. And maybe you can view it as according to my view of where we are, I think we're about 80% of the way there in that we have models that are smart, they're very capable. They're able to, if you give... Are they smarter than you? I mean, they're certainly more capable than I am at writing software. If you give it all the context, then yes, I think that they are just so capable. It's really remarkable. Does anyone here feel better at writing software than GPT-5.4? Oh, we got one! We got one! Alright, writing kernels. So even there, we're seeing massive gains from, exactly, for some of our internal results. There we're really seeing if you pour the right kinds of, if you have the right setup for your problem, then you're able to get really massive results out of very low-level, even low-level tasks. Just to give you one example of how things have been trending, one of my systems engineers also very similarly was like, "Hey, I haven't been able to get value out of the models for GPT-5, for 5.1, for 5.2 as well." For 5.3, he on a lark had prepared this design document for a very complicated systems optimization he was about to do. He handed it over to the model and went to sleep, intending to like give this to his team to work on for the next week. And when he woke up, it was done. The model had actually implemented the initial spec, had seen that it was slow, had added instrumentation, had actually run the code, used a profiler to figure out where things were slow, and iterated multiple times until it got to an optimized result. And that is incredible. That's where we are. And so how, what would you advise all startups here to do? Because the models keep getting more and more capable. They kind of... I've asked this when Sam was here in the past. You know, if you're building today, do you need to rebuild in two years when a new model comes out because all the functionality and all the capabilities all change around you? Do you need to make sure that you're not in OpenAI's way because you're going to just run over startups because the models are so much more capable? How would you recommend a set of startup founders to build in this environment? Well, first of all, I would say to lean in. The tools right now have become incredibly useful. And if you look even over the course of December, I think that we went from these agentic coding tools being like, you know, they're like writing 20% of your code to writing 80% of your code. Which means they go from being kind of a sideshow to being the main thing that you're doing. And I think we're doing that across all of the work that people do with computers, all computer work this year. And you can look at the recent progress on Codex. It's really changing from a tool for software engineers to a tool for anyone who's doing work with a computer. And just over the past week, we've released a bunch of features that just make it so much more powerful and capable. One thing we just announced today is a new tool called Chronicle that plugs into Codex, where it actually can see everything you're doing with your computer and can form memories of what's going on. And so you ask it a question, you just... it instantly knows what you're talking about. You're like, "Huh, what was I doing five minutes ago?" It knows. You're like, "Oh, what was this person talking about?" It knows. It's to me, it was this real wake-up call to realize you spend so much of your effort right now just explaining to your computer what's going on. Like, why are you explaining to your computer what's going on? That makes no sense. And so I think what's going to happen over upcoming years is the models are going to get much more capable. We'll have better harnesses. We'll be able to solve harder and harder problems, come up with new knowledge, all of these things. But there is a one-time shift that's happening now, which is really about context. It's really about is your AI able to, you have all these meetings, you didn't include the AI, you know, that's not very nice to the AI. You're asking it to help you with things and it has no information. So I think really leaning into how do you make sure the AI even has enough information in theory to solve the problem and then trust that the models are going to really get there and improve. So I think it will be a constant cycle of improvement and iteration and leaning into the tools and kind of talking to your friends how they're using it, but that there is this investment that's a one-time investment that now is the time to make. And in terms of like, let's say you set that all up, how is OpenAI using Codex differently than you think everybody else outside is using it? Well, I think one of the amazing things about being at OpenAI is you do get to live in the future. You do get to really see the shape of what's emerging, and we can co-design. We can really change the models, the harness, everything together in order to better serve the needs that we see. And a lot of the approach we've been taking is, so we started with software engineering and we set some clear guidelines. For example, saying that we still want a human to be accountable for all code that gets merged. So at the end of the day, is it a good thing to merge this piece of code? Is it well-structured? Is it going to make our codebase more maintainable? We want to make sure there's a human who is signing off to say yes. And that thoughtfulness of not just saying, "Okay, let's just blindly use this," or, "Oh, we don't want to use this at all," like I think neither extreme is quite right. And then we are also going vertical by vertical within OpenAI to adopt these tools within finance, within sales, within IT. And there we have a small dedicated team who's really deeply understanding the domain, working with the people who are the experts in it, in order to build skills, in order to modify the Codex UI, whatever it is that is needed in order to get it to be good. And then that's something we can then, once we have it in good shape, we will externalize. And we're able to ship that to all of you. And so we are starting to work with certain customers as well. So for people who want to be very AI-forward and want to be part of defining this revolution, there's a place for that and I'd love to talk afterwards. But yeah, I think that just this desire to say, "Hey, we really want to be AI-forward, really live in the future and experience what it will be like for everyone else one year, two years, three years down the road." Do you guys structure company differently or the engineering teams differently because of living in the future? I mean, if you have to go way back, when my father learned computer science, he was just himself. And then we had these long software releases that became waterfall. And then when the web happened and the cloud happened, we had these two-pizza teams and we had Scrum. Now that we have these coding agents, how do you structure around everything differently? I think we're still figuring it out. And there's certain places where you really see it. For example, the cost of building a prototype is cheap now. It's so cheap. And if you want to build a dashboard that used to be like, "Oh, take someone like a week to do it," and you just do it now. And so actually a lot of the bottleneck has shifted to things like sharing. Like how do you, and so we actually have some internal work on this as well that again we will be externalizing, of how do you make it really easy for anyone in your enterprise to build a dashboard, a widget, a bot, whatever the thing is, and share it with others. Then that starts to really put pressure on having good governance. Like you want your IT organization to be able to see all these different threads of execution that are happening, all the little things that are being shared around, have some control over data provenance, right? To really make sure that okay, like a good example of this is I think people are now starting to take their internal knowledge dumps, turn them into wikis. We have some real cool one of these internally. And the thing you immediately think about is, well, if someone has a document in the internal knowledge base that was accidentally permissioned incorrectly, and they realize, "Oh no, I didn't want this information to be accessible," how do they fix that? Normally it's they go into the doc, they change the permissions. But now there's these derived artifacts. And so you need to make sure you have some way of tracking through the system to say, "Well, this output document came from this source one, the source one is no longer accessible to this audience, let's go and invalidate that as well." And so you have to start really building your technical architecture with awareness of the way that people are going to use this information. And it really changes how teams relate to each other because you can just, it really changes where the bottlenecks are and what's hard. Do you think team sizes are going to be a lot smaller? Are we going to have still human software engineers in a decade? Well, a decade is a long time from now. And the ceiling on this technology is really hard to internalize. I think that it is clear that what a company is will change in a lot of ways. I think that we're going to have this ability for solopreneurs to build very incredible businesses. And so anyone who has a vision, I think will be able to realize it. I think the jobs that you all have will become way easier in a lot of ways, way more fun. Now, might be more competitive too, because everyone's going to have these amazing tools. And so really figuring out what is your niche, what is your unique angle is probably going to become kind of the most important core. But a lot of how we run organizations right now, and it's almost only one way to organize large groups of people where you have teams and you have management structures and you have scopes and you have these hierarchies and all these things, maybe that can change. Maybe you can be much more flat, small teams that can really just do incredible things. Like we're seeing it right now in mathematics where these individuals on the internet are using GPT-5.4 Pro to solve these unsolved math problems. Normally you need a math team, and they're just doing it. My son's a math nerd and I just told him that maybe you should be studying something else besides math. But see this is the question, right? Is if you look at something like AlphaGo, move 37, this move that just like changed humanity's understanding of the game, but the thing that was surprising is it made the game more interesting and important for humans. And maybe that'll be true for these other domains too. What about common failure modes when you're building with production agentic workflows? What do you see as the common things that founders get wrong and they're building incorrectly these days? Well, I think that these models they have such power and really understanding how to operate them well takes thought. And so we've been investing a lot in primitives, security primitives, observability, having again good governance, things like that. But just to give you one anecdote that I think is evocative, I asked Codex to install some package that someone at OpenAI had written, ran into an error, and I was like, "Oh, ping that person on Slack and ask them for help." So it pinged the person on Slack. Two minutes later it said, "This is taking too long. I've escalated to the person's manager," and it actually pinged the person's manager. And you realize it's like on the one hand it's kind of a reasonable thing for the model to do. It's being proactive, it's trying to solve my problem, it's not just sitting around waiting to be told what to do. But on the other hand, like you know maybe it should have taken a little bit longer, maybe should have checked with me. And so I think really thinking about these questions where we're still building up the EQ of the model and that in some places it's getting very good. For example, clicking "approve, approve, approve" is kind of where we've been and humans are not very good at that either. They just default. And so now we're starting to have AIs that can actually take care of flagging "is this a high-risk action? Hey, this one should be escalated, this one's okay to auto-approve." It really makes you realize human attention is going to be this incredibly scarce resource. The doing of things now is easy. The "is this a good thing? Is this what I wanted? Is this aligned with my values, with my desires?" That is going to become the single most important bottleneck. And so I think building systems that take that into account and really think about the human factor, that's the most important thing to do now. Another human factor: security. How would you advise people to think about security in this world of AI? Heard about breaches left and right with Vercel recently, and these models are incredibly powerful at finding security holes. So how would you recommend people here use the models to find those security issues? Well, I think there's a couple levels to the answer. I do think that the internet has been a place where security has been just like a ratcheting important concern over time. You think about where it started going through the 90s with viruses and worms and malware and those things, and we've moved past that. I think we are also moving now to a much more ultimately secure regime, but it does require an internet-wide effort to get there. And so a lot of this honestly is just again leaning into the technology, having these models they can scan your codebase, they can actually be used for end-to-end red teaming. There's a lot that can be done with them. And a lot of how we're thinking about further models and improvements there is really leaning into how do we actually sort of leverage trusted access programs, how do we leverage the community of people who really care about being defenders and making the internet more secure. I think that's something where everyone has a role to play and can participate. But the number one thing is just sort of recognizing that these models are very powerful but they're not magic. They are just like a part of the overall resilience ecosystem. And I think that we as a society and every company really contributes to this, have something to build in terms of how do we incorporate these in a way that results in more assurance and more certainty on the impacts of whether it's this particular patch that you're taking, whether it's thinking about how do you make sure that you're just rolling in updates quickly as they're being released. So I think that there's a lot of work to be done but I have a lot of optimism for where this is going. Let's switch to speed. Seems like things are moving faster and faster and faster. We're in the world of accelerating change. We were talking about it when you were walking up here around how you're trying to keep up with things. How do you keep up with all the accelerating change? How would you recommend everybody here keep up with everything that's changing? Well, I think this is the new normal. And I think to some extent it's not really because of AI. I think it's just been the trend of technology for the past two decades. There's more people doing things. It's easier to do things than ever. Barriers to entry go down. It means it's also much more easy to build value, to have great successes. And so I think that really trying to keep your ear to the ground and understand what's changing and to some extent it always starts with the same thing, which is play with the technology yourself. It's very different to hear AI described versus to use it. But the beautiful thing about AI is it's so intuitive. Like that's the whole point is that rather than have the machine be something you have to contort yourself to, the machine contorts itself to you. It's doing work for you. And it should be something where you ask it and it does something. And so I think that just really trying to just get your finger on the pulse of what's changing, what's possible, where the models lag, that is I think the core skill that is going to really determine a lot of the success of companies in the future. And then on the flip side of that, you guys have held back models to work with security agents, so it's like the opposite of going as fast as possible. You're doing things responsibly too. So how do you think about the balance? Because you're in a competitive environment, you want to ship as quickly as possible and yet you're trying to do the right thing as well. Yeah, I think at a values level, like what OpenAI is about, we really want to put the power of AI in people's hands. We believe that people can, we want to empower people to build the future with the tools that are being created. But we need to do that in a thoughtful way. We really think about both sides of: here are the benefits, here are the risks. How do you maximize the benefits, how do you mitigate those risks? And I think that in cybersecurity and biosecurity, those are areas where we're very thoughtful. We've been working on these kinds of both mitigations and trusted access programs for quite a long time. What we see coming is models that are going to be increasingly powerful and capable in a continuous way across all dimensions of capability. And we announced last week the expansion of our Trusted Access for Cyber program. By the way, has anyone here applied? No one? Oh, I see one hand. Two hands. Okay. More of you should apply. It's great. We really need help because it's very important that people who are trustworthy and responsible and really want to push these models are participating in this because that is how that's going to pay dividends for everyone. We're going to have more to announce over upcoming weeks on how we're expanding the program but also when we release models to everyone, kind of the mitigations that we have and how we're going to tune those to be both, to really balance, to really try to bring these capabilities as broadly as possible while also making sure that the ones that are you know that we're thinking about the risks and able to have some observability over them and to ensure that this is maximally positive in terms of deployment. So I think the short answer is it's core to our mission. We care a lot about the impacts of what we're doing, not just building the technology in isolation, but it is a whole community and a whole world effort to really get to where we need to be. Now moving up from the models to the application layer, which is what a lot of people here are building. How does OpenAI decide what in the application layer you're going to build and what you're going to leave out? Well, people have probably seen the word "focus" being applied to OpenAI quite a lot recently, possibly for the first time in a while. It's been applied to her too. And it's hard because the field of AI is one of opportunity. It's like anything you can imagine is going to be great, no question it's going to be great. And we as a single company, no matter how much compute we build, no matter how many people we have, are only going to be able to do so much. And so a lot of where we've been, how we've been thinking about things is what is the sort of most focused strategy that covers the parts of the space, maybe it's an 80/20 or just like the parts of the space that we think we can have most impact on. And I think there it's very clear right now we're going through this agentic transition. And so products that are and it's not just about enterprise versus consumer, right? So it's like clear we are being very serious about enterprise, like we're selling to big companies and building a whole muscle and sales motion there. But consumer, what consumer is, is going to change. It's kind of a very broad term that buckets in multiple things. But that the slice of consumer that's about not just productivity but about goals, about achieving your goal, about even knowing what is your goal, being able to elicit that and having an AI that can proactively do that. It's all kind of the same thing. In the end we're trying to build an AGI that you can talk to that has all this context that you can use in your personal life, your work life, that's trustworthy, that you can go to it for advice and give you useful information, maybe health information or maybe about finances or you know about if you're trying to figure out what to do with your career. All of these things, they all kind of ladder into one thing, and it's meant we had to make some very painful decisions about what not to do. But I think I would just say that that's the aperture that we look at things through and that things that accrue to that singular vision of what we want to build, you should expect us to pursue. Do you think we'll be coding with command lines and agents in a few years or it's going to be completely changed? I mean, I think that we're in a very unnatural state right now for how we work. Like we all sit behind this box and kind of type away and it's very clear our bodies were not designed for this. We got our carpal tunnel and our hunched shoulders and all these things. And I don't think we want that. I don't think any of us wanted that. Like, I think that we want... more free time... but it's not even about free time necessarily, right? Like you want to spend more time with your loved ones, yes. You want to spend more time like talking to people and like coming up with like brilliant visions or just like what you're excited about or just understanding yourself. So it's kind of like do you want to be a CEO of an organization of like 100,000 agents? Like that actually seems pretty good. And I think that we're all going to be able to get so much more done, but the mechanics of it are going to feel as different as going from having to write out things by hand with a quill or something to being able to just send a text message and have people go and work on your behalf, on your goals. Alright, we talked about compute, we talked about model, security, and agents, and app layer. Let's talk about frontier. When are the models going to be good enough to push the frontiers of science, physical AI? It seems like we had Jensen here. It seems like LLMs have been a great scaling law for digital intelligence. It hasn't been as strong for robotics, for physical intelligence, for aspects of biology and science where the problems are probably a lot harder to verify or it takes a long time to verify. How are you keeping track of science and physical AI in the world? Well, science is one domain that we're really leaning into, and we see line of sight to really incredible progress. And we're starting to have some signs of life. I think it's always important to ground in what is happening today when trying to predict what will happen six months, a year from now. For example, we had a physics result where our AI came up with this very beautiful formula that physicists who have been working on this for quite some time thought was totally impossible. Thought it was like maybe an unsolvable problem. And it's pretty significant, right? Like real serious physicists who really view this as a step towards really being able to get to some sort of answer for quantum gravity and all these things. It's not there, but it's a step. And that's much bigger than where we were just a couple months ago. And so it makes you really wonder a year from now, like how far will we have traveled. Now things like biology, they are different from physics and math. They are you gotta leave your beautiful simulated world and deal with messy reality. But I think we've been learning how to deal with messy reality in other domains. Software engineering is a perfect example where we've really realized that just building the thing that solves competition programming competitions, like that's not enough. You need something that's seen real-world messy codebases, humans interrupting in different ways, this adversarial banging at it. And so I think that on science, I expect we're going to see a real Renaissance. You know, maybe we'll see some big results this year. Next year, I think it's going to be a totally wild, wild time. We live in interesting times. I promised that I get you out on time because you're a busy man. Before we let you leave, we got one minute on the shot clock.
